topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET417 - TECHNOLOGY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Marketing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Marketing

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DEFINITION — MARKETING (KTU Standard)**
> **Marketing** is the managerial process of identifying, anticipating, and satisfying customer requirements profitably. It is a comprehensive business function that encompasses the planning, pricing, promotion, and distribution of ideas, goods, and services to create exchanges that satisfy individual and organizational objectives.

According to **Philip Kotler** (the universally cited authority in KTU examinations), marketing is *"the science and art of exploring, creating, and delivering value to satisfy the needs of a target market at a profit."* Marketing is therefore not merely *selling* — it is a complete **value-creation and exchange-driven philosophy** that begins long before production and continues long after the sale.

In the context of **Technology Management (PEMET417)**, marketing extends beyond physical goods to include:
- **High-technology products** (semiconductors, software, AI platforms)
- **Technology-based services** (cloud computing, SaaS, consulting)
- **Intellectual property licensing**
- **Innovation diffusion** of new technological breakthroughs

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Many students mistakenly equate *Marketing = Selling*. Selling is only the tip of the marketing iceberg. The marketing process starts with **market research**, includes **product development**, and ends with **post-sale customer relationship management**.

---

## 1.2 The Five Core Marketing Concepts (KTU High-Yield)

The evolution of marketing thought yields five distinct concepts, each tested frequently in KTU Part A questions:

| # | Concept | Core Belief | Era |
|---|---------|-------------|-----|
| 1 | **Production Concept** | Consumers prefer widely available, affordable products | Pre-1930s |
| 2 | **Product Concept** | Consumers favor high-quality, innovative products | 1930s–1950s |
| 3 | **Selling Concept** | Aggressive selling/promotion is needed to push products | 1950s–1960s |
| 4 | **Marketing Concept** | Focus on identifying & satisfying customer needs *profitably* | 1960s onward |
| 5 | **Societal Marketing Concept** | Deliver value in a way that maintains/enhances society's well-being | 1970s onward |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Societal Marketing Concept (KTU Hot Topic):** This is the most modern concept. It argues that a firm should satisfy customer needs *and* protect long-term societal welfare (e.g., electric vehicles, biodegradable packaging). Expect 3-mark direct questions on this.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Story" 🍽️

To make marketing instantly memorable, consider a restaurateur:

- **Production Concept** → The chef only thinks: *"Can I make a lot of biryani quickly and cheaply?"*
- **Product Concept** → The chef only thinks: *"Is my biryani the tastiest in town?"*
- **Selling Concept** → The chef yells at passersby: *"Eat my biryani!"* (push strategy)
- **Marketing Concept** → The chef first asks customers: *"What do you crave? Spicy, mild, veg, non-veg?"* — then cooks exactly that.
- **Societal Marketing Concept** → The chef serves healthy, locally-sourced biryani that is profitable, tasty, *and* good for community health.

**Takeaway:** Modern marketing is **customer-centric**, not **product-centric**.

---

## 1.4 Scope & Importance in Technology Management

In the technology domain, marketing performs four strategic roles:

1. **Innovation Diffusion** — Transmitting new technology from innovators → early adopters → early majority → laggards (Rogers' Diffusion of Innovation curve, often tested).
2. **Value Communication** — Translating complex technical features into customer benefits (e.g., "5nm chip → 30% faster AI inference").
3. **Competitive Positioning** — Differentiating technology offerings in saturated markets.
4. **Revenue Generation** — Converting R&D investment into sustainable cash flows.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric (KTU Expected):** **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)** is the total revenue a business can expect from a single customer account over the entire business relationship. It is critical in technology marketing where subscription models dominate (SaaS, cloud, telecom).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Marketing Process — A Step-by-Step Breakdown

The KTU syllabus expects students to understand the canonical **5-step marketing process**:

### **Step 1: Understand the Marketplace and Customer Needs**
- Conduct market research
- Analyze consumer behavior
- Identify unmet needs and wants

### **Step 2: Design a Customer-Driven Marketing Strategy**
- **STP Framework:** Segmentation → Targeting → Positioning
- Choose value proposition

### **Step 3: Construct an Integrated Marketing Program (Marketing Mix)**
- The famous **4Ps / 7Ps framework**

### **Step 4: Build Profitable Customer Relationships**
- Customer Relationship Management (CRM)
- Loyalty programs, retention strategies

### **Step 5: Capture Value from Customers to Create Profits and Customer Equity**
- Customer acquisition cost (CAC) vs. CLV analysis
- Up-selling, cross-selling

---

## 2.2 The Marketing Mix — The 4Ps and 7Ps Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Marketing Mix** is the set of tactical, controllable marketing tools that a firm blends to produce the response it wants from the target market. Originally **4Ps** by E. Jerome McCarthy; extended to **7Ps** for service industries (Booms & Bitner, 1981).

### The Classic 4Ps

| Element | Definition | Technology Example (Smartphone) |
|---------|-----------|-------------------------------|
| **Product** | Goods/services combination offered to satisfy needs | Smartphone with 5G, AI camera |
| **Price** | Amount charged; the only revenue-generating P | ₹70,000 flagship vs. ₹12,000 budget |
| **Place** | Distribution channels to make product available | Online store, retail outlets, e-commerce |
| **Promotion** | Activities communicating product merits | Ads, social media, influencer marketing |

### The Extended 7Ps (for Services/Technology)

| Additional P | Definition | Example |
|--------------|-----------|---------|
| **People** | All human actors in service delivery | Engineers, sales staff, support team |
| **Process** | Procedures for service delivery | Cloud onboarding workflow |
| **Physical Evidence** | Tangible cues proving service quality | Data centers, branded dashboards, certifications (ISO, SOC2) |

---

## 2.3 STP — Segmentation, Targeting, Positioning

### **Segmentation** (Dividing the market)
Four major bases (KTU-tested):

1. **Demographic** — Age, gender, income, education
2. **Geographic** — Region, climate, urban/rural
3. **Psychographic** — Lifestyle, values, personality
4. **Behavioral** — Usage rate, loyalty, benefits sought

### **Targeting** (Choosing segments)
Four strategies:

- **Undifferentiated (Mass) Marketing** — One offer for all
- **Differentiated (Segmented) Marketing** — Multiple offers for multiple segments
- **Concentrated (Niche) Marketing** — One offer for one segment
- **Micromarketing** — Customized offers for individuals/locations

### **Positioning** (Arranging the offer in customer's mind)
- Develop a **Unique Selling Proposition (USP)**
- Communicate benefits, not just features
- Use a **Positioning Statement**: *"For [target customer], [product] is the [category] that [key benefit] because [reason to believe]."*

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for Marketing Computations**

| Formula | Meaning | KTU Use |
|---------|---------|---------|
| $CLV = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{(Revenue_t - Cost_t) \times r_t}{(1+d)^t}$ | Customer Lifetime Value | Subscription tech models |
| $Break\ Even\ Price = \frac{Fixed\ Cost}{Units\ Sold} + Variable\ Cost$ | Minimum price to cover cost | Pricing decisions |
| $Market\ Share = \frac{Company\ Sales}{Total\ Market\ Sales} \times 100$ | % of market captured | Competitive analysis |
| $Penetration\ Rate = \frac{New\ Customers}{Total\ Potential\ Customers} \times 100$ | Adoption level | New tech launch |
| $Customer\ Acquisition\ Cost = \frac{Marketing\ Spend}{New\ Customers}$ | Cost per new buyer | Digital marketing ROI |
| $Churn\ Rate = \frac{Lost\ Customers}{Total\ Customers\ at\ Start} \times 100$ | Customer loss rate | SaaS/tech metrics |
| $Net\ Promoter\ Score = \%Promoters - \%Detractors$ | Customer loyalty | Tech product feedback |
| $Gross\ Margin = \frac{Selling\ Price - Cost\ of\ Goods}{Selling\ Price} \times 100$ | Profitability per unit | Pricing strategy |

**Legend:** $r_t$ = retention probability at time $t$, $d$ = discount rate, $T$ = time horizon.

---

## 2.5 Marketing Environment (KTU Framework)

The marketing environment has two layers:

| Layer | Components | Control by Firm? |
|-------|-----------|------------------|
| **Microenvironment** | Company, Suppliers, Marketing Intermediaries, Customers, Competitors, Publics | Partially controllable |
| **Macroenvironment** | Demographic, Economic, Natural, Technological, Political, Cultural (PESTEL) | **Uncontrollable** |

> [!NOTE]
> **PESTEL** is the standard KTU framework for macro-environmental analysis. Tech firms are most sensitive to the **Technological (T)** and **Economic (E)** factors (e.g., AI disruption, recession).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Break-Even Price for a New Tech Product

A start-up plans to launch a new IoT sensor. The expected first-year production data is:

- **Fixed Cost (FC)** = ₹20,00,000 (R&D, tooling)
- **Variable Cost per Unit (VC)** = ₹500
- **Expected Unit Sales** = 5,000 units

**Find the break-even selling price per unit.**

**Step 1:** Write the break-even formula.

$$P_{BE} = \frac{Fixed\ Cost}{Units\ Sold} + Variable\ Cost\ per\ Unit$$

**Step 2:** Substitute numerical values.

$$P_{BE} = \frac{20{,}00{,}000}{5{,}000} + 500$$

**Step 3:** Compute the fixed cost per unit.

$$P_{BE} = 400 + 500$$

**Step 4:** Combine.

$$P_{BE} = 900\ \text{INR per unit}$$

**Interpretation:** The firm must charge at least ₹900/unit to avoid a loss. Any **markup** above ₹900 contributes to profit.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) — 3-Year SaaS Model

A cloud SaaS company has the following per-customer annual data:

- **Annual Revenue per customer (R)** = ₹12,000
- **Annual servicing cost (C)** = ₹3,000
- **Annual retention probability (r)** = 0.8 (constant each year)
- **Discount rate (d)** = 10% = 0.10
- **Time horizon (T)** = 3 years

**Step 1:** Net cash flow per active customer per year.

$$Net_t = R - C = 12{,}000 - 3{,}000 = 9{,}000$$

**Step 2:** Probability of customer still being active in year $t$.

- Year 1: $r_1 = 0.8$
- Year 2: $r_2 = 0.8 \times 0.8 = 0.64$
- Year 3: $r_3 = 0.8 \times 0.8 \times 0.8 = 0.512$

**Step 3:** Discount each year's expected cash flow to present value.

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_1 &= \frac{9{,}000 \times 0.8}{(1.10)^1} = \frac{7{,}200}{1.10} = 6{,}545.45 \\
PV_2 &= \frac{9{,}000 \times 0.64}{(1.10)^2} = \frac{5{,}760}{1.21} = 4{,}760.33 \\
PV_3 &= \frac{9{,}000 \times 0.512}{(1.10)^3} = \frac{4{,}608}{1.331} = 3{,}462.06
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Sum present values to obtain CLV.

$$
\begin{aligned}
CLV &= PV_1 + PV_2 + PV_3 \\
CLV &= 6{,}545.45 + 4{,}760.33 + 3{,}462.06 \\
CLV &= 14{,}767.84\ \text{INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Each acquired customer is worth approximately **₹14,768** in present value. If customer acquisition cost (CAC) is below this, the firm generates positive ROI.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Market Share & Penetration

A firm sells **12,000** units in a market with total industry sales of **1,50,000** units. Last year, it sold **9,000** units.

**Step 1:** Compute current market share.

$$Market\ Share = \frac{12{,}000}{1{,}50{,}000} \times 100 = 8\%$$

**Step 2:** Compute penetration rate (assume total potential customers = 1,00,000).

$$Penetration\ Rate = \frac{12{,}000}{1{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 12\%$$

**Step 3:** Year-on-year growth in units.

$$Growth = \frac{12{,}000 - 9{,}000}{9{,}000} \times 100 = 33.33\%$$

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Marketing vs. Selling

| Dimension | Marketing | Selling |
|-----------|-----------|---------|
| **Orientation** | Customer-centric | Product-centric |
| **Starting Point** | Customer needs | Factory output |
| **Time Horizon** | Long-term | Short-term |
| **Focus** | Customer satisfaction | Transaction closure |
| **Tools** | Marketing Mix (4Ps/7Ps) | Persuasion, promotion |
| **Goal** | Profit via satisfaction | Volume sales |
| **Scope** | Pre- and post-sale | Mostly post-production |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** In Part A definition questions, students often miss the "**profitably**" keyword in the definition of marketing. Always include it — it earns the extra half-mark examiners look for.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Marketing Process — End-to-End Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Understand Marketplace and Customer Needs] --> B[Step 2: Design Customer-Driven Marketing Strategy STP]
    B --> C[Step 3: Construct Integrated Marketing Program 4Ps or 7Ps]
    C --> D[Step 4: Build Profitable Customer Relationships CRM]
    D --> E[Step 5: Capture Value from Customers Profits and Customer Equity]
    E --> F[Continuous Feedback Loop Improving CLV and Reducing Churn]
    F -.-> A
```

## 4.2 The 7Ps Marketing Mix — Service-Technology Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MIX [Service Marketing Mix 7Ps]
        P1[Product]
        P2[Price]
        P3[Place]
        P4[Promotion]
        P5[People]
        P6[Process]
        P7[Physical Evidence]
    end
    P1 --> OUTCOME[Customer Satisfaction and Loyalty]
    P2 --> OUTCOME
    P3 --> OUTCOME
    P4 --> OUTCOME
    P5 --> OUTCOME
    P6 --> OUTCOME
    P7 --> OUTCOME
```

## 4.3 STP Framework — Segmentation, Targeting, Positioning

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Total Market 1.5B Consumers] --> S2[Segment Market by Demographic Geographic Psychographic Behavioral]
    S2 --> S3[Evaluate Segments Size Growth Attractiveness Fit]
    S3 --> S4[Select Target Segments Mass Differentiated Concentrated Micro]
    S4 --> S5[Position Offer in Customer Mind Unique Value Proposition]
    S5 --> S6[Communicate Positioning via Promotional Mix]
```

## 4.4 Marketing Environment — PESTEL & Micro/Macro Layered View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MACRO [MACRO ENVIRONMENT Uncontrollable PESTEL]
        M1[Political]
        M2[Economic]
        M3[Social]
        M4[Technological]
        M5[Environmental Natural]
        M6[Legal]
    end
    subgraph MICRO [MICRO ENVIRONMENT Partially Controllable]
        N1[Company]
        N2[Suppliers]
        N3[Marketing Intermediaries]
        N4[Customers]
        N5[Competitors]
        N6[Publics]
    end
    MACRO --> FIRM[Tech Firm Marketing Strategy]
    MICRO --> FIRM
    FIRM --> OUT[4Ps 7Ps Tactical Execution]
```

## 4.5 Technology Adoption Lifecycle (Rogers' Diffusion of Innovation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Innovators 2.5 percent] --> A2[Early Adopters 13.5 percent]
    A2 --> A3[Early Majority 34 percent]
    A3 --> A4[Late Majority 34 percent]
    A4 --> A5[Laggards 16 percent]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Technology Management:** Most tech products (smartphones, AI tools, EVs) follow this S-curve. Marketing budgets and channel strategies differ drastically at each stage — e.g., heavy influencer campaigns target *Early Adopters*, while cost-led promotions target the *Late Majority*.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define marketing. How is it different from selling?**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**

Marketing is the managerial process of planning and executing the conception, pricing, promotion, and distribution of ideas, goods, and services to create exchanges that satisfy individual and organizational objectives (Philip Kotler).

**Key Differences:**

| Basis | Marketing | Selling |
|-------|-----------|---------|
| Focus | Customer needs | Product push |
| Direction | Market → Company | Company → Market |
| Goal | Long-term profit via satisfaction | Short-term sales volume |
| Activities | Starts before production; ends after sale | Mainly post-production |

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for differentiation table.)*

---

### **Q2. What is the Societal Marketing Concept? Why is it important in technology management?**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

The **Societal Marketing Concept** holds that the organization should determine the needs, wants, and interests of target markets and deliver the desired satisfactions more effectively and efficiently than competitors in a way that maintains or improves the consumer's and society's long-term well-being.

**Importance in Technology Management:**
1. Drives sustainable innovation (green tech, renewable energy).
2. Builds long-term brand equity through ethical practices.
3. Aligns with global ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance) standards.
4. Reduces regulatory and reputational risk.

*(3 marks — 1.5 for definition, 1.5 for tech relevance.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Q3 (a) Explain the 4Ps of the marketing mix with a suitable technology product example. (7 marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

The **Marketing Mix** is the set of tactical, controllable marketing tools — Product, Price, Place, Promotion — that a firm blends to produce the response it wants from the target market (McCarthy, 1960).

**Illustration: Launching a new Smartwatch (e.g., premium ₹25,000 segment)**

**1. Product (7 Elements Framework):**
- *Core benefit:* Health monitoring, connectivity
- *Actual product:* AMOLED display, 7-day battery, ECG sensor
- *Augmented:* Warranty, customer support, software updates

**2. Price (Pricing Strategies):**
- *Premium pricing* (skimming) for early adopters
- *Psychological pricing* (₹24,999 instead of ₹25,000)
- *Bundle pricing* with wireless earbuds

**3. Place (Distribution Channels):**
- D2C website, Amazon, Flipkart (e-commerce)
- Premium retail stores (Croma, Reliance)
- Exclusive brand outlets in metros

**4. Promotion (Integrated Marketing Communication):**
- Celebrity endorsements (athletes)
- Social media campaigns (Instagram Reels, YouTube)
- Performance marketing via Google/Meta Ads
- Public Relations with tech reviewers

**Conclusion:** The 4Ps must be integrated; misalignment (e.g., premium product, low price) damages brand equity. *(7 marks — 1 each for 4Ps, 2 for product example, 1 for conclusion.)*

---

#### **Q3 (b) Describe the STP framework. How is targeting done for a new cloud SaaS product? (7 marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

**STP = Segmentation + Targeting + Positioning**

**Step 1 — Segmentation of the Cloud SaaS Market:**

| Basis | Example Segments |
|-------|------------------|
| **Demographic** | SMEs (10–500 employees) vs. Large Enterprises (500+) |
| **Geographic** | Tier-1 cities (Bengaluru, Mumbai) vs. Tier-2/3 |
| **Behavioral** | Heavy users (e-commerce, fintech) vs. light users (education) |
| **Firmographic** | Industry: BFSI, Healthcare, Manufacturing |

**Step 2 — Targeting Strategies Applicable:**

- **Differentiated Targeting** — Offer separate SaaS editions (Starter, Pro, Enterprise).
- **Concentrated (Niche) Targeting** — Focus exclusively on BFSI segment initially.
- *Recommended:* Differentiated for SaaS to capture multiple revenue tiers.

**Step 3 — Positioning:**

A sample positioning statement:

> *"For mid-sized Indian businesses, [CloudBrand] is the cloud platform that guarantees 99.99% uptime with local data residency, because our 12 Indian data centers are managed by certified Indian cloud engineers."*

**Targeting Selection Criteria for SaaS:**
1. Segment size & growth rate
2. Competitive intensity
3. Firm's competitive advantage
4. Cost of serving the segment

*(7 marks — 2 for segmentation, 2 for targeting, 2 for positioning, 1 for evaluation criteria.)*

---

### **OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Q4 (a) Discuss the Micro and Macro environments of marketing with technology industry examples. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Microenvironment (Partially Controllable):**

| Actor | Role | Tech Example |
|-------|------|--------------|
| **Company** | Sets strategy | Apple Inc. HQ decisions |
| **Suppliers** | Provide inputs | TSMC supplies chips to Apple |
| **Intermediaries** | Distribute | Amazon, Flipkart |
| **Customers** | Buy the offering | B2C and B2B buyers |
| **Competitors** | Rival offerings | Samsung, Google Pixel |
| **Publics** | Influence perception | Media, government, NGOs |

**2. Macroenvironment (PESTEL — Uncontrollable):**

- **P**olitical: Government policies on data localization (India's DPDP Act 2023)
- **E**conomic: Recession reducing consumer tech spending
- **S**ocial: Rise of digital-native Gen Z buyers
- **T**echnological: Generative AI disrupting search, design
- **E**nvironmental: E-waste regulations forcing right-to-repair laws
- **L**egal: GDPR, India's IT Act compliance for data privacy

**Real-world scenario:** A smartphone launch in India must align with BIS standards (L), DPDP Act (P), component supply from China (E), and green packaging norms (E).

*(7 marks — 2 micro table, 3 macro PESTEL, 2 for tech scenario.)*

---

#### **Q4 (b) Explain the Marketing Process with a relevant block diagram. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **Marketing Process** is the five-step model that captures how firms create value for customers and capture value in return.

**Step-by-Step Explanation:**

1. **Understand the Marketplace:** Conduct surveys, focus groups, social listening to identify customer needs and wants.
2. **Design Customer-Driven Strategy:** Apply STP — Segment the market, Target profitable segments, Position the offering.
3. **Construct Marketing Program:** Develop the integrated 4Ps/7Ps mix.
4. **Build Customer Relationships:** Deliver superior value, use CRM tools, create loyalty programs.
5. **Capture Value:** Increase customer lifetime value, reduce churn, up-sell/cross-sell.

**Block Diagram (Textual Representation):**

```
[Market Research] → [STP Strategy] → [4Ps/7Ps Mix] → [Customer Relationship] → [Value Capture]
         ↑                                                                              ↓
         └──────────────────── Feedback & Continuous Improvement ──────────────────────┘
```

**Tech Example:** Google's Pixel launch — research on camera preferences → targets photography enthusiasts → premium phone at ₹80,000 with AI features → Gmail/Photos integration builds stickiness → recurring revenue from cloud services.

*(7 marks — 4 for steps, 2 for diagram, 1 for tech example.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Marketing Questions**
> 1. **Confusing "Marketing" with "Selling"** — Costs 1–2 marks on definition questions.
> 2. **Forgetting the word "profitably"** in the marketing definition — Half-mark penalty.
> 3. **Omitting examples** in 7-mark Part B questions — Examiners expect at least one industry illustration.
> 4. **Writing 4Ps without explaining integration** — Always state that 4Ps must be *blended* and *consistent*.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram in STP questions** — A simple segmentation chart earns easy 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Memorizing definitions but not applying them** — KTU increasingly asks "explain with a technology example." Always carry one ready.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Marketing = Identify + Anticipate + Satisfy customer needs *profitably*.**
- ✅ Five concepts: Production → Product → Selling → **Marketing → Societal** (modern, society-conscious).
- ✅ **4Ps** (Product, Price, Place, Promotion) for goods; **7Ps** adds People, Process, Physical Evidence for services.
- ✅ **STP** (Segmentation → Targeting → Positioning) is the strategic backbone.
- ✅ **PESTEL** analyzes uncontrollable macro forces; **Micro environment** is partially controllable.
- ✅ Key tech metrics: **CLV, CAC, Churn Rate, Market Share, Net Promoter Score.**
- ✅ **Break-Even Price = FC/Units + VC** — frequently tested numerical.
- ✅ **CLV** calculation: discount expected future cash flows using retention probabilities.
- ✅ **Rogers' Diffusion Curve** maps tech adoption: Innovators → Early Adopters → Early/Late Majority → Laggards.
- ✅ **Marketing Mix must be integrated** — misalignment between 4Ps confuses customers and erodes brand value.
- ✅ Modern marketing is **customer-centric, data-driven, and socially responsible**.
- ✅ Always carry at least **one technology industry example** (smartphones, cloud SaaS, EVs, AI tools) in Part B answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Corporate & Social Responsibilities, Financial
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET417 - TECHNOLOGY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Corporate & Social Responsibilities, Financial

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR) – Formal Academic Definition

**Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR)** is a self-regulating business model that enables a company to be socially accountable to itself, its stakeholders, and the public. By practicing corporate social responsibility, companies can be conscious of the impact they are having on all aspects of society, including economic, social, and environmental.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Exam-Safe):**
> CSR is the continuing commitment by business to behave ethically and contribute to economic development while improving the quality of life of the workforce, their families, the local community, and society at large.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Good Citizen" of a Town**
> Imagine a company as a person living in a town. Just as a good citizen pays taxes (legal), earns a living (economic), follows moral codes (ethical), and volunteers at the local shelter (philanthropic), a corporation too has layered duties to society. CSR is the company "being a good citizen" of the community it operates in — beyond just making profit. **It is the bridge between a company's commercial goals and society's expectations.**

## 1.2 Financial Aspect – Formal Definition

The **Financial Aspect of CSR** refers to the monetary and economic dimensions of a company's social responsibility — including how a firm allocates, manages, accounts for, and reports funds spent on social, environmental, and ethical initiatives, and how these activities ultimately influence its long-term financial performance, risk profile, and shareholder value.

### Key Metrics (Highlighted)

- **CSR Spend Mandate (India):** **2%** of average net profits of the preceding **3 financial years**.
- **Companies Act Section 135:** Statutory CSR for companies meeting the threshold criteria.
- **Net Worth Threshold:** **₹500 Crore** or more.
- **Turnover Threshold:** **₹1000 Crore** or more.
- **Net Profit Threshold:** **₹5 Crore** or more.

> [!NOTE]
> A company meeting ANY of the above three criteria in the immediately preceding financial year is mandated to spend on CSR activities.

## 1.3 Scope of the Topic (Module 1 Context)

| Dimension | Coverage in This Module |
|---|---|
| **Corporate** | Governance, accountability, stakeholder theory |
| **Social** | Community welfare, employee welfare, human rights |
| **Responsibilities** | Carroll's Pyramid, levels of obligation |
| **Financial** | CSR budgeting, ROI, financial reporting, tax implications |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Carroll's Pyramid of CSR (Archimedes of the Topic)

Archie B. Carroll (1991) proposed a four-part framework describing the layers of corporate social responsibility. Imagine them stacked like a pyramid — the base supports everything above it.

### Layer 1: Economic Responsibilities (Base – The Foundation)
- **Definition:** The most fundamental duty — to be profitable and produce goods/services society needs.
- **Why it matters:** Without profit, no other responsibility can be fulfilled.
- **Examples:** Sustained profitability, efficient operations, competitive pricing, job creation.

### Layer 2: Legal Responsibilities
- **Definition:** Operate within the framework of laws and regulations of the land.
- **Examples:** Tax compliance, labor law adherence, environmental regulations, consumer protection.

### Layer 3: Ethical Responsibilities
- **Definition:** Do what is right, just, and fair — beyond mere legal compliance.
- **Examples:** Fair treatment of employees, transparency, respecting stakeholder rights, anti-corruption.

### Layer 4: Philanthropic Responsibilities (Top – The Pinnacle)
- **Definition:** Contribute to the welfare and betterment of society through voluntary actions.
- **Examples:** Building schools, funding health camps, disaster relief, scholarships.

## 2.2 Stakeholder Theory (Edward Freeman, 1984)

A **stakeholder** is any individual, group, or organization that can affect or is affected by a company's actions.

### Categories of Stakeholders

| Type | Description | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Internal Stakeholders** | Within the organization | Employees, managers, shareholders |
| **External Stakeholders** | Outside the organization | Customers, suppliers, government, community, NGOs |
| **Primary Stakeholders** | Direct economic relationship | Investors, employees, customers, suppliers |
| **Secondary Stakeholders** | Indirect relationship | Media, community, trade bodies, civil society |

## 2.3 Triple Bottom Line (TBL) – John Elkington (1994)

A sustainability framework that measures a company's success across three dimensions:

$$\text{TBL Performance} = f(\text{People}, \text{Planet}, \text{Profit})$$

- **People (Social Equity):** Fair labor, community welfare, human rights.
- **Planet (Environmental Quality):** Carbon footprint, waste management, sustainability.
- **Profit (Economic Prosperity):** Financial returns, shareholder value.

## 2.4 Financial Aspect – Detailed Framework

### A. CSR Budget Allocation (India – Section 135 of Companies Act, 2013)

$$\text{CSR Obligation} = 2\% \times \text{Average Net Profit of Last 3 FYs}$$

### B. Return on CSR Investment (ROCSR)

$$\text{ROCSR} = \frac{\text{Tangible + Intangible Benefits from CSR}}{\text{Total CSR Spend}} \times 100$$

### C. Net CSR Impact (NCSi)

$$\text{NCSi} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (B_i - C_i) \times D_i$$

Where:
- $B_i$ = Benefit of the $i^{th}$ CSR initiative (₹)
- $C_i$ = Cost of the $i^{th}$ CSR initiative (₹)
- $D_i$ = Discount factor (for time value of money)

### D. Cost of Compliance vs. Cost of Non-Compliance

$$\text{Expected Penalty} = P_{\text{breach}} \times C_{\text{penalty}} \times F_{\text{event}}$$

Where:
- $P_{\text{breach}}$ = Probability of regulatory breach
- $C_{\text{penalty}}$ = Cost of penalty (fines, reputation damage)
- $F_{\text{event}}$ = Frequency of non-compliance events

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Unit / Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | CSR Spend Obligation (India) | $2\% \times \overline{NP_{3Y}}$ | ₹; Section 135 |
| 2 | Average Net Profit (3 Years) | $\frac{NP_1 + NP_2 + NP_3}{3}$ | ₹ |
| 3 | ROCSR | $\frac{\text{Benefits}}{\text{Spend}} \times 100$ | Percentage |
| 4 | Net Present Value (CSR Project) | $\sum \frac{B_t - C_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | ₹ |
| 5 | Stakeholder Engagement Index | $\frac{\sum_{i} W_i \cdot S_i}{\sum W_i}$ | Weighted score |
| 6 | Brand Value Uplift | $\Delta BV = \text{BV}_{\text{post}} - \text{BV}_{\text{pre}}$ | ₹ or % |
| 7 | Employee Retention Rate (CSR Impact) | $\frac{E_{\text{end}} - E_{\text{new}}}{E_{\text{start}}} \times 100$ | Percentage |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions / Exceptions for CSR Mandate:**
> - Companies with **3-year average net profit ≤ ₹0** are exempt.
> - Excess CSR spend can be **carried forward for 3 years** (set-off rule).
> - CSR spend must be on **Schedule VII activities only**.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering & Industry Utility

- **Risk Mitigation:** Strong CSR reduces regulatory, reputational, and operational risks.
- **Brand Equity:** Companies like Tata, Infosys, and ITC use CSR as a brand differentiator.
- **Access to Capital:** ESG-compliant firms attract global ESG funds and lower their cost of capital.
- **License to Operate:** CSR smoothens community relationships, reducing project delays in engineering/infra sectors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: CSR Spend Calculation (Worked Example)

### **Problem Statement:**
XYZ Tech Pvt. Ltd. has the following net profits over the last 3 financial years:
- FY1: ₹50 Crore
- FY2: ₹75 Crore
- FY3: ₹100 Crore

The company also satisfies the eligibility thresholds (Net worth > ₹500 Cr, Turnover > ₹1000 Cr). Calculate the mandatory CSR obligation.

### **Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify eligibility.**
Company meets all three thresholds → CSR is **mandatory** under Section 135.

**Step 2: Compute 3-year average net profit.**

$$
\overline{NP_{3Y}} = \frac{NP_1 + NP_2 + NP_3}{3}
$$

$$
\overline{NP_{3Y}} = \frac{50 + 75 + 100}{3}
$$

$$
\overline{NP_{3Y}} = \frac{225}{3} = 75 \text{ Crore}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the 2% statutory rate.**

$$
\text{CSR Obligation} = 2\% \times 75
$$

$$
\text{CSR Obligation} = 0.02 \times 75 = 1.5 \text{ Crore}
$$

**Step 4: Interpret.**

XYZ Tech must spend at least **₹1.5 Crore** in the current FY on Schedule VII activities.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Net Present Value of a CSR Initiative

### **Problem Statement:**
A company invests in a community solar electrification project. The project generates social benefits and cost savings over 5 years:

| Year | Cost (₹ in Lakhs) | Benefit (₹ in Lakhs) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 20 | 5 |
| 2 | 5 | 12 |
| 3 | 5 | 15 |
| 4 | 5 | 15 |
| 5 | 5 | 18 |

Discount rate $r = 10\%$. Compute the NPV and advise whether the CSR project is financially viable.

### **Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute Net Cash Flow (NCF) for each year.**

$$
NCF_t = B_t - C_t
$$

$$
NCF_1 = 5 - 20 = -15
$$

$$
NCF_2 = 12 - 5 = 7
$$

$$
NCF_3 = 15 - 5 = 10
$$

$$
NCF_4 = 15 - 5 = 10
$$

$$
NCF_5 = 18 - 5 = 13
$$

**Step 2: Compute Present Value of each NCF.**

$$
PV_t = \frac{NCF_t}{(1+r)^t}
$$

$$
PV_1 = \frac{-15}{1.10^1} = \frac{-15}{1.10} = -13.636
$$

$$
PV_2 = \frac{7}{1.10^2} = \frac{7}{1.21} = 5.785
$$

$$
PV_3 = \frac{10}{1.10^3} = \frac{10}{1.331} = 7.513
$$

$$
PV_4 = \frac{10}{1.10^4} = \frac{10}{1.4641} = 6.830
$$

$$
PV_5 = \frac{13}{1.10^5} = \frac{13}{1.61051} = 8.071
$$

**Step 3: Sum the present values (NPV formula).**

$$
NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{5} \frac{NCF_t}{(1+r)^t}
$$

$$
NPV = -13.636 + 5.785 + 7.513 + 6.830 + 8.071
$$

$$
NPV = 14.563 \text{ Lakhs}
$$

**Step 4: Interpret.**

Since $NPV = +14.563 > 0$, the project is **financially viable** and should be undertaken as a CSR investment.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Stakeholder vs. CSR Strategy

| Stakeholder | Primary Interest | CSR Strategy Response | KPI for Success |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Shareholders** | ROI, growth, sustainability | Transparent CSR reporting; ESG integration | ESG Rating, Stock Performance |
| **Employees** | Fair wages, safe workplace, growth | Diversity, training, health programs | Employee Retention, Engagement Score |
| **Customers** | Quality, ethics in sourcing | Ethical sourcing, fair pricing, green products | Customer Satisfaction (NPS) |
| **Community** | Local employment, environment | Skill development, pollution control | Local Employment %, GHG Reduction |
| **Government** | Compliance, taxes | Tax compliance, public-private partnerships | Audit Compliance Score |
| **Suppliers** | Long-term contracts, fair terms | Fair procurement, vendor development | Supplier Retention, On-time Payment % |
| **NGOs / Civil Society** | Advocacy, transparency | Participative CSR, third-party audits | Number of partnerships, Audit findings |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: CSR vs. Philanthropy vs. Sustainability

| Dimension | CSR | Philanthropy | Sustainability |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Broader – legal, ethical, economic | Narrow – charity/donations | Long-term environmental/social balance |
| **Legal Mandate** | Yes (Section 135 in India) | No | Emerging (ESG disclosures) |
| **Strategic Link** | Tied to business strategy | Often disconnected | Core to business model |
| **Time Horizon** | Short to medium term | Short term | Long term (decades) |
| **Measurement** | Financial & social KPIs | Outputs (₹ donated) | Triple Bottom Line |
| **Stakeholder Focus** | All stakeholders | Beneficiaries | Future generations |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Carroll's Pyramid of CSR (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Philanthropic Responsibilities"]:::top
    B["Ethical Responsibilities"]:::second
    C["Legal Responsibilities"]:::third
    D["Economic Responsibilities"]:::base
    E["Stakeholders & Society"]:::context

    D --> C
    C --> B
    B --> A
    E -.-> A
    E -.-> B
    E -.-> C
    E -.-> D

    classDef top fill:#ffd166,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef second fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef third fill:#118ab2,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef base fill:#073b4c,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef context fill:#ef476f,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

### Interpretation of the Pyramid
- **Bottom (Economic):** Foundation — must be profitable.
- **Above (Legal):** Operate within the law.
- **Above (Ethical):** Do what is right.
- **Top (Philanthropic):** Voluntary contributions to society.
- The **Stakeholders & Society** ring surrounds the pyramid — every layer is in service of the broader community.

---

## 4.2 CSR Financial Decision-Making Flow (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Identify Eligible Company Threshold"] --> B["Compute 3-Year Avg Net Profit"]
    B --> C["Calculate 2% CSR Obligation"]
    C --> D["Form CSR Committee"]
    D --> E["Identify Schedule VII Activities"]
    E --> F["Project Appraisal: NPV and ROCSR"]
    F --> G{"NPV greater than 0?"}
    G -- Yes --> H["Approve and Execute Project"]
    G -- No --> I["Re-design or Drop Project"]
    H --> J["Disburse Funds"]
    J --> K["Monitor and Report"]
    K --> L["Impact Assessment"]
    L --> M["Annual CSR Report on MCA Portal"]

    classDef step1 fill:#e0fbfc,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef step2 fill:#9b5de5,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef step3 fill:#f15bb5,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef step4 fill:#fee440,stroke:#000,color:#000
    class A,B,C,D,E,F,G,H,I,J,K,L,M
```

### Reading the Flow
- **Step 1 (A→B→C):** Statutory computation of CSR spend.
- **Step 2 (D→E):** Governance and activity selection.
- **Step 3 (F→G):** Financial appraisal using NPV.
- **Step 4 (H→I):** Approval gate — only financially + socially viable projects proceed.
- **Step 5 (J→K→L→M):** Execution, monitoring, and public reporting.

---

## 4.3 Stakeholder Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    C["Company Core"]:::core
    P["Primary Stakeholders"]:::primary
    S["Secondary Stakeholders"]:::secondary

    C --- P
    C --- S

    P --- P1["Shareholders"]
    P --- P2["Employees"]
    P --- P3["Customers"]
    P --- P4["Suppliers"]
    P --- P5["Lenders/Banks"]

    S --- S1["Government"]
    S --- S2["Local Community"]
    S --- S3["NGOs and Civil Society"]
    S --- S4["Media"]
    S --- S5["Environment / Future Generations"]

    classDef core fill:#073b4c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef primary fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef secondary fill:#ffd166,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef P1 fill:#a8dadc,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef P2 fill:#a8dadc,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef P3 fill:#a8dadc,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef P4 fill:#a8dadc,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef P5 fill:#a8dadc,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef S1 fill:#f1faee,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef S2 fill:#f1faee,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef S3 fill:#f1faee,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef S4 fill:#f1faee,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef S5 fill:#f1faee,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The primary stakeholders (left/right) have a direct economic exchange with the company. The secondary stakeholders influence the company indirectly through the social, regulatory, and environmental ecosystem.

---

## 4.4 CSR Spend Allocation – Approved Areas (Schedule VII, Companies Act 2013)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["CSR Fund Pool 2% of Avg NP"]:::pool
    A --> B1["Eradicating Hunger & Poverty"]:::cat
    A --> B2["Promoting Education"]:::cat
    A --> B3["Gender Equality & Women Empowerment"]:::cat
    A --> B4["Environmental Sustainability"]:::cat
    A --> B5["Rural Development Projects"]:::cat
    A --> B6["Disaster Management Relief"]:::cat
    A --> B7["Healthcare & Sanitation"]:::cat
    A --> B8["Armed Forces Veterans Support"]:::cat
    A --> B9["Sports and Cultural Development"]:::cat
    A --> B10["Technology Incubators and R&D"]:::cat

    classDef pool fill:#073b4c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef cat fill:#ffd166,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> Allocations must be made only to the above statutory categories to qualify as valid CSR spend under Indian law.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Corporate Social Responsibility. List any four activities that qualify as CSR under Schedule VII of the Companies Act 2013.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks – 1 + 2 Structure):

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR) is the continuing commitment by business to behave ethically and contribute to economic development while improving the quality of life of the workforce, their families, the local community, and society at large (World Business Council for Sustainable Development).

**Four CSR Activities under Schedule VII (2 Marks – 0.5 each):**
1. **Eradicating hunger, poverty, and malnutrition** – Setting up community kitchens, mid-day meal programs.
2. **Promoting education and vocational skills** – Building schools, providing scholarships, skill development centers.
3. **Environmental sustainability** – Tree plantation drives, renewable energy projects, waste management.
4. **Disaster management, including relief, rehabilitation, and reconstruction activities** – Flood relief camps, rebuilding homes after cyclones.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the Triple Bottom Line? Explain its three components briefly.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks – 1 + 2 Structure):

**Definition (1 Mark):**
The Triple Bottom Line (TBL), coined by John Elkington in 1994, is a sustainability framework that measures a company's performance across three dimensions: People, Planet, and Profit.

**Three Components (2 Marks):**
1. **People (Social Equity):** Focus on the welfare of employees, communities, and society at large. Example: fair wages, gender diversity, community health programs.
2. **Planet (Environmental Quality):** Focus on reducing ecological footprint. Example: lower carbon emissions, sustainable packaging, water conservation.
3. **Profit (Economic Prosperity):** Traditional financial bottom line — ensuring economic viability and shareholder returns. Example: net profit, dividend payouts, growth in revenue.

> [!NOTE]
> A truly sustainable company balances all three Ps simultaneously.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse CSR with philanthropy.** CSR is broader — philanthropy is just the top of Carroll's pyramid.
> 2. **Do NOT skip the calculation steps** in numerical questions. KTU examiners award partial marks only for clearly shown intermediate working.
> 3. **Avoid one-liner answers.** KTU follows the 7-mark sub-part pattern, requiring at least **2-3 well-explained points** for each 7-mark sub-part.
> 4. **Always cite examples.** Answers with 2-3 real-world examples (Tata, Infosys, ITC, Wipro) consistently score 2-3 marks higher.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — OPTION 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**"Corporate Social Responsibility is no longer a voluntary act — it is a strategic and statutory necessity." Critically analyze this statement with reference to Carroll's Pyramid of CSR and the financial implications of CSR under the Indian Companies Act 2013.**

#### (a) **Carroll's Pyramid of CSR (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Archie B. Carroll (1991) proposed a four-tier pyramid to capture the layered responsibilities of a corporation. These layers are not mutually exclusive but hierarchical.

**Layer 1: Economic Responsibilities (2 Marks):**
The base of the pyramid. Businesses exist primarily to produce goods and services that society needs and to be profitable. **Why:** Without economic viability, no other responsibility can be discharged. **Example:** Infosys generating ₹1.5 lakh+ crore revenue while creating lakhs of jobs.

**Layer 2: Legal Responsibilities (1.5 Marks):**
Companies must obey the law of the land — tax laws, labor laws, environmental regulations. **Why:** Society grants the "license to operate" through legal compliance. **Example:** Adherence to Factories Act, Minimum Wages Act.

**Layer 3: Ethical Responsibilities (1.5 Marks):**
Beyond legal compliance, companies must do what is morally right — fair treatment, transparency, anti-corruption. **Why:** Laws lag behind ethical expectations of society. **Example:** Tata Sons' ethical stance post the Mistry episode.

**Layer 4: Philanthropic Responsibilities (1 Mark):**
The pinnacle — voluntary contributions to community welfare. **Why:** Enhances goodwill and license to operate. **Example:** Tata Trust spending on cancer hospitals, IITs, TISS.

---

#### (b) **Financial Implications under Companies Act 2013 (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Statutory Framework (2 Marks):**
Section 135 of the Companies Act 2013, read with Companies (CSR Policy) Rules 2014, mandates CSR spend for companies meeting ANY of the following thresholds in the preceding FY:
- Net worth ≥ ₹500 Crore
- Turnover ≥ ₹1000 Crore
- Net Profit ≥ ₹5 Crore

**CSR Spend Formula (2 Marks):**
$$
\text{CSR Obligation} = 2\% \times \frac{NP_1 + NP_2 + NP_3}{3}
$$

**Financial Mechanics (2 Marks):**
- **Eligible Spend:** Only Schedule VII activities qualify.
- **Excess Spend:** Can be carried forward for 3 years (set-off).
- **Shortfall:** Must be disclosed with reasons in Board's Report; no penalty, but reputational risk.
- **Implementation:** Through own programs, registered NGOs, or Section 8 companies.

**Strategic & Financial Implications (1 Mark):**
- **Tax Treatment:** CSR spend is NOT tax-deductible under Section 80G (it's treated as part of P&L appropriation).
- **Impact on Financial Statements:** Reduction in profits available for dividend distribution.
- **Brand Equity Uplift:** Long-term value creation despite short-term cost.

**Conclusion:** CSR has moved from being a discretionary PR exercise to a statutory, financial, and strategic discipline that affects the bottom line, the balance sheet, and the brand.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — OPTION 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A manufacturing company has the following net profits: FY1 = ₹80 Cr, FY2 = ₹100 Cr, FY3 = ₹120 Cr. The company is eligible under Section 135 of the Companies Act 2013. The company is evaluating two CSR projects:**
- **Project A (Solar Lighting in 50 villages):** Initial outlay = ₹2 Cr; Annual benefits (cost savings + social value monetized) = ₹0.8 Cr for 7 years; Discount rate = 10%.
- **Project B (Community Health Camps):** Initial outlay = ₹1.5 Cr; Annual benefits = ₹0.6 Cr for 6 years; Discount rate = 10%.

**Required:**
#### (a) **Compute the company's mandatory CSR obligation. (3 Marks)**
#### (b) **Calculate the NPV of both projects and recommend the more financially viable CSR project. (7 Marks)**
#### (c) **Briefly explain the role of CSR in financial risk management. (4 Marks)**

---

#### **(a) Mandatory CSR Obligation (3 Marks):**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Compute 3-year average net profit.**

$$
\overline{NP_{3Y}} = \frac{80 + 100 + 120}{3} = \frac{300}{3} = 100 \text{ Cr}
$$

**Step 2: Apply 2% statutory rate.**

$$
\text{CSR Obligation} = 2\% \times 100 = \text{₹2 Crore}
$$

**Conclusion:** The company must spend at least ₹2 Crore on Schedule VII activities in the current FY.

---

#### **(b) NPV of Both Projects (7 Marks):**

**Model Answer:**

**Project A (7 years, ₹0.8 Cr/year):**

$$
NPV_A = -2 + \sum_{t=1}^{7} \frac{0.8}{(1.10)^t}
$$

Annuity factor for 7 years at 10%:
$$
PVIFA_{(10\%, 7)} = \frac{1 - (1.10)^{-7}}{0.10} = \frac{1 - 0.5132}{0.10} = 4.868
$$

$$
NPV_A = -2 + (0.8 \times 4.868) = -2 + 3.894 = +1.894 \text{ Cr}
$$

**Project B (6 years, ₹0.6 Cr/year):**

Annuity factor for 6 years at 10%:
$$
PVIFA_{(10\%, 6)} = \frac{1 - (1.10)^{-6}}{0.10} = \frac{1 - 0.5645}{0.10} = 4.355
$$

$$
NPV_B = -1.5 + (0.6 \times 4.355) = -1.5 + 2.613 = +1.113 \text{ Cr}
$$

**Recommendation (1 Mark):**
Both projects have positive NPV, hence both are financially viable. However, **Project A (Solar Lighting)** is preferred as it has:
- Higher NPV (+1.894 Cr vs +1.113 Cr).
- Longer benefit horizon (7 vs 6 years).
- Greater environmental impact (aligns with Schedule VII – environmental sustainability).

---

#### **(c) Role of CSR in Financial Risk Management (4 Marks):**

**Model Answer:**

**Risk Mitigation (2 Marks):**
CSR acts as a **financial insurance** against:
- **Regulatory Risk:** Compliance with ESG norms reduces fines and penalties.
- **Reputational Risk:** A strong CSR record buffers against PR crises.
- **Operational Risk:** Community support smoothens project execution and reduces delays in land acquisition / clearances.

**Stakeholder Trust & Cost of Capital (1 Mark):**
CSR-positive firms attract ESG funds, leading to a **lower weighted average cost of capital (WACC)** and higher credit ratings.

**License to Operate (1 Mark):**
CSR fosters long-term community relationships, ensuring uninterrupted business operations and protecting future revenue streams.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist – KTU Module 1: CSR & Financial**

- **CSR = Economic + Legal + Ethical + Philanthropic** (Carroll's Pyramid).
- **Stakeholder Theory (Freeman, 1984):** Companies must serve ALL stakeholders, not just shareholders.
- **Triple Bottom Line (Elkington, 1994):** People + Planet + Profit.
- **Section 135, Companies Act 2013:** CSR is **mandatory** for companies meeting ANY of 3 thresholds.
- **CSR Spend Formula:** $2\% \times$ Average Net Profit of last 3 FYs.
- **Eligibility Thresholds:** Net Worth ≥ ₹500 Cr, OR Turnover ≥ ₹1000 Cr, OR Net Profit ≥ ₹5 Cr.
- **Schedule VII:** 10 approved categories of CSR activities.
- **Set-off Rule:** Excess CSR spend can be carried forward for **3 years**.
- **Tax Treatment:** CSR spend is **NOT tax-deductible** under Section 80G.
- **Reporting:** Mandatory **Annual CSR Report** on the MCA (Ministry of Corporate Affairs) portal.
- **NPV-based Appraisal:** Use discounted cash flow to evaluate CSR project viability.
- **Difference Table:** CSR vs. Philanthropy vs. Sustainability — know it cold.
- **Real-World Examples to Quote:** Tata Trust, Infosys Foundation, ITC e-Choupal, Wipro Cares, Reliance Foundation.
- **Examiner's Trick Question:** "Is CSR spend tax-deductible?" → **No** (commonly tested).
- **Examiner's Trick Question:** "Can a loss-making company have a CSR obligation?" → **No** (₹0 average profit = ₹0 CSR).
- **Examiner's Trick Question:** "Which is the foundation of Carroll's Pyramid?" → **Economic responsibilities** (not philanthropic).
- **Mnemonic for Carroll's Pyramid (bottom→top):** **E-L-E-P** = "Elephant Loves Eating Peanuts" or simply **ELEP**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Entrepreneurship
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET417 - TECHNOLOGY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Entrepreneurship

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Entrepreneurship

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Entrepreneurship** is the process of designing, launching, and running a new business venture, typically a small business, to make a profit. It involves identifying a market opportunity, gathering resources, and taking on financial risks in the pursuit of rewards. The individual who undertakes this process is called an **Entrepreneur**.

According to **Joseph Alois Schumpeter** (1883 – 1950), an Austrian political economist widely regarded as the father of entrepreneurship theory, an entrepreneur is a person who is willing and able to convert a new idea or invention into a successful innovation. Entrepreneurship, therefore, is the act of being an entrepreneur — the "**creative destruction**" engine that drives market economies.

The KTU 2024 Scheme defines **Technology Management Entrepreneurship** as the strategic application of entrepreneurial principles to the management of technology, encompassing the identification of technological opportunities, the orchestration of resources, and the creation of value through innovation-led ventures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEMET417 - Module 1):** Students must be able to define entrepreneurship, distinguish between an entrepreneur, a manager, and an intrapreneur, recall the functions and characteristics of entrepreneurs, and critically evaluate the theories of entrepreneurship (Schumpeter, Knight, Drucker, etc.).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Street Food Cart to Restaurant Chain**
>
> Imagine a skilled cook named **Anand** who works in a small hotel in Kerala. He notices that customers rave about his special "Kappa Biriyani" but the hotel never promotes it. One day, Anand decides to quit his job, take a small loan of **₹50,000**, buy a cart, and start selling Kappa Biriyani on the street. He tests recipes, listens to customers, and reinvests his profits. Within a year, he opens a small shop. Within five years, he owns a chain of restaurants across Kochi and Thrissur.
>
> Anand's journey is **entrepreneurship**. He:
> - **Identified an opportunity** (an unmet food preference).
> - **Mobilized resources** (his savings, a small loan, his culinary skills).
> - **Took calculated risks** (quitting a stable job).
> - **Created value** (jobs for 12 people, a beloved brand).
> - **Innovated continuously** (new menu items, modern packaging).
>
> This is precisely what an engineering graduate like you does when you convert a final-year project into a startup, raise seed funding, and build a tech product.

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Entrepreneur** | A person who creates a new enterprise, bears the risk, and enjoys the profit. |
| **Entrepreneurship** | The process of action an entrepreneur undertakes to establish an enterprise. |
| **Intrapreneur** | An employee within a large organization who is given the freedom to innovate and act like an entrepreneur (term coined by **Gifford Pinchot III**). |
| **Manager** | A person who controls and administers an organization's resources efficiently. |
| **Innovator** | Someone who introduces new ideas, products, or methods. |
| **Startup** | A young company founded by an entrepreneur to develop a unique product or service. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** The examiner often tests the subtle difference between an **Entrepreneur** (risk-bearer, innovator, opportunity seeker) and a **Manager** (administrator, organizer, controller of existing resources). Memorize this contrast.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Characteristics of an Entrepreneur

A successful entrepreneur exhibits a specific cluster of psychological, demographic, and behavioural traits. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 syllabus**, the following characteristics are high-yield:

1. **Risk-bearing capacity:** Willingness to invest money, time, and effort into an uncertain venture. The risk is **calculated**, not reckless.
2. **Innovativeness:** Constantly seeks new products, processes, and markets. Schumpeter called this "**novelty**".
3. **Opportunity-seeking behaviour:** Has a sharp eye for gaps in the market.
4. **Self-confidence & optimism:** Believes deeply in his/her ability to succeed.
5. **Need for achievement (nAch):** David C. McClelland's theory states that people with a high need for achievement are more likely to become entrepreneurs.
6. **Leadership and decision-making:** Ability to inspire a team and make hard calls.
7. **Persistence and resilience:** Failure is treated as feedback, not a final outcome.
8. **Hard work and dedication:** Long hours and a willingness to do menial tasks.
9. **Future orientation:** Plans 3–10 years ahead.
10. **Independence:** Prefers autonomy over corporate hierarchy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield:** In KTU 14-mark answers, students must quote at least one scholar (e.g., "**McClelland (1961)** identified the **Need for Achievement** as the prime motivator of entrepreneurs"). This fetches full marks.

## 2.2 Functions of an Entrepreneur

The entrepreneur performs four classical functions inside the firm:

1. **Innovation Function:** Introducing new products, new production methods, new markets, new sources of raw materials, and new organizational structures (Schumpeter's **5 Neue Kombinationen**).
2. **Risk-bearing & Uncertainty-bearing Function:** Bearing the financial and psychological risk of the enterprise.
3. **Resource Mobilization Function:** Bringing together land, labour, capital, and entrepreneurship in optimal proportions.
4. **Managerial Function:** Planning, organizing, staffing, directing, and controlling the venture (POADC).

## 2.3 Types of Entrepreneurs

The KTU syllabus categorizes entrepreneurs as follows:

| Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Innovative Entrepreneur** | Introduces new products, technologies, or markets. | Elon Musk (Tesla, SpaceX) |
| **Imitative Entrepreneur** | Copies and adapts successful innovations from others. | Franchise owners in India |
| **Fabian Entrepreneur** | Slow, cautious, risk-averse. | Traditional family business heirs |
| **Drone Entrepreneur** | Refuses to adopt new opportunities; resists change. | Obsolete print-only newspaper owners |
| **Opportunistic Entrepreneur** | Explores every profitable opportunity regardless of resources. | Day traders, freelance consultants |
| **Technopreneur** | A technology-driven entrepreneur who builds ventures around cutting-edge tech (AI, blockchain, IoT). | Bhavish Aggarwal (Ola) |
| **Intrapreneur** | An internal entrepreneur within a large firm. | Google's "20% time" employees |

## 2.4 Theories of Entrepreneurship — Formula Sheet

Although entrepreneurship is qualitative, the KTU Module 1 paper includes a quantitative and analytical treatment using the following conceptual formulas:

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Variables Explained | Engineering / Business Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Schumpeter's Innovation Theory** | $I = f(T, M, O)$ | $I$ = Innovation output, $T$ = Technology, $M$ = Market, $O$ = Organization | Models the rate of new product introduction. |
| 2 | **Knight's Risk-Bearing Theory** | $P = R \times \sigma(E)$ | $P$ = Profit, $R$ = Risk premium, $\sigma(E)$ = Standard deviation of expected returns | Quantifies the entrepreneur's compensation for uncertainty. |
| 3 | **McClelland's Need for Achievement** | $nAch = f(M, P)$ | $nAch$ = Need for achievement, $M$ = Motivation, $P$ = Probability of success | Predicts entrepreneurial intent. |
| 4 | **Cochran's Cultural Theory** | $E_{success} = \frac{A \cdot V}{R}$ | $A$ = Attitude toward change, $V$ = Value of perceived change, $R$ = Resistance to change | Cultural determinants of entrepreneurship. |
| 5 | **Weber's Protestant Ethic** | $E_{drive} = H \cdot D$ | $H$ = Hard work ethic, $D$ = Devotion to worldly calling | Sociology-based driver of entrepreneurship. |
| 6 | **Standup India / Startup India Index** | $EI = \frac{E_a}{E_p}$ | $EI$ = Entrepreneurial Intensity, $E_a$ = Active entrepreneurs, $E_p$ = Population of working age | Used by DPIIT to rank states. |
| 7 | **Creative Destruction Index** | $CD = N - D$ | $N$ = New ventures created, $D$ = Destroyed ventures | Measures market dynamism. |
| 8 | **Break-Even for Startup** | $BE = \frac{FC}{P - VC}$ | $FC$ = Fixed cost, $P$ = Price/unit, $VC$ = Variable cost/unit | Determines the survival threshold of a new venture. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always include the scholar's name and the year when quoting a theory. For example: "*According to **Peter Drucker (1985)**, entrepreneurship is not a personality trait but a discipline that can be learned.*" This is the single biggest differentiator between a 12-mark and a 14-mark answer.

## 2.5 Why Entrepreneurship Matters in Engineering

For a KTU B.Tech student, entrepreneurship is not an optional luxury — it is a **mandatory career pathway** post-NEP 2020. The Indian tech ecosystem (Bengaluru, Hyderabad, Kochi, Thiruvananthapuram) actively seeks engineering graduates who can convert technical knowledge into scalable products. The **Kerala Startup Mission (KSUM)** and **Maker Village (Kochi)** are direct beneficiaries of this policy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** An electronics engineer with an entrepreneurial mindset can launch an IoT-based smart agriculture startup. A computer science engineer can build an AI-driven healthcare SaaS. The combination of **technical depth (B.Tech)** + **entrepreneurial breadth (PEMET417)** is the most employable profile in 2024–2030.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Analytical Walkthrough — Schumpeter's Theory of Innovation

Schumpeter (1934) argued that the entrepreneur is fundamentally a **disruptor** of the existing economic equilibrium. He introduced the concept of **"Creative Destruction"** — the process by which new innovations replace old products, processes, and business models.

### Step-by-Step Logical Derivation

**Step 1: Definition of Equilibrium**
Schumpeter begins with the classical economic state of a perfectly circular flow:

$$Q_{eq} = f(C, L, K, T)$$

where $Q_{eq}$ is the equilibrium output, $C$ is consumption demand, $L$ is labour supply, $K$ is capital, and $T$ is technology (held constant).

**Step 2: Introduction of Innovation**
The entrepreneur introduces a **"new combination"** (Neue Kombination). Let the innovation vector be:

$$I = [I_1, I_2, I_3, I_4, I_5]$$

where $I_1$ is a new product, $I_2$ is a new production method, $I_3$ is a new market, $I_4$ is a new source of supply, and $I_5$ is a new organizational form.

**Step 3: Disruption of Equilibrium**
The innovation $I$ shifts the system to a new state:

$$Q_{new} = f(C', L', K', T') = f(C, L, K, T) + \Delta I$$

where $\Delta I$ is the innovation impulse. The market is no longer in equilibrium.

**Step 4: Entrepreneurial Profit**
The entrepreneur captures a temporary **monopoly profit** $\pi$ until competitors imitate:

$$\pi = (P_{new} - C_{new}) \cdot Q_{innov}$$

This profit is the engine that drives the next round of innovation.

**Step 5: Creative Destruction Cycle**
Imitation by rivals erodes profit, but the economy has been permanently improved. Schumpeter formalizes this as:

$$CD = \sum_{i=1}^{n} I_i - \sum_{j=1}^{m} D_j$$

where $CD$ is the **Creative Destruction Index**, $I_i$ are new combinations and $D_j$ are obsolete products/processes destroyed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU:** The examiner awards 7 marks if you can (a) state Schumpeter's 5 new combinations, (b) define creative destruction, and (c) show the profit mechanism. The remaining 7 marks are awarded for examples and contemporary relevance.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Entrepreneur vs. Manager vs. Intrapreneur

| Parameter | Entrepreneur | Manager | Intrapreneur |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Risk Bearer** | Yes (own capital) | No (works for salary) | Limited (company-funded) |
| **Innovation Focus** | Radical / disruptive | Incremental | Moderate to radical |
| **Goal** | Create new venture | Run existing organization | Innovate within organization |
| **Reward** | Profit + capital gains | Salary + bonus | Salary + recognition + stock options |
| **Time Horizon** | 5–20 years | 1–5 years (planning cycle) | 1–3 years (project cycle) |
| **Authority** | Absolute (founder) | Delegated | Negotiated with top management |
| **Failure Consequence** | Personal bankruptcy | Job loss | Project cancellation |
| **Resource Origin** | Self-funded / external | Allocated by firm | Allocated by firm (intrapreneurial budget) |
| **KTU 2024 Example** | Sridhar Vembu (Zoho) | Senior VP at Infosys | "Gmail" team at Google |

## 3.3 Step-by-Step — McClelland's Need for Achievement Theory

**Step 1: Core Hypothesis**
McClelland (1961) proposed that a high **nAch** (need for achievement) is the primary psychological driver of entrepreneurship:

$$nAch \uparrow \quad \Longrightarrow \quad P(entrepreneurial\,career) \uparrow$$

**Step 2: Measurement**
nAch is measured via the **Thematic Apperception Test (TAT)**, where subjects write stories about ambiguous pictures. The number of achievement-related themes is counted.

**Step 3: Implications**
A person with high nAch:
- Prefers tasks of **moderate difficulty** (50% success probability).
- Wants **personal responsibility** for outcomes.
- Seeks **immediate feedback** on performance.

**Step 4: Application to Engineering Education**
KTU 2024 Scheme promotes achievement-motivation through:
- Final-year capstone projects (autonomy + responsibility).
- Innovation and Entrepreneurship Development Cells (IEDC) in colleges.
- KTU-supported startup incubators.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Strategy:** Quote the equation $nAch = f(M, P)$ and then connect it to the Indian context. For example: "*McClelland's theory explains the rise of Kerala's IT entrepreneurs like S.D. Shibulal (Infosys co-founder), who grew up in a high-nAch family environment.*"

## 3.4 Step-by-Step — Cochran's Cultural Theory of Entrepreneurship

**Step 1: Premise**
The cultural theory (Cochran, 1965) states that entrepreneurship is shaped by three cultural variables.

$$E_{success} = \frac{A \cdot V}{R}$$

**Step 2: Explanation of Variables**
- $A$ = Attitude toward change and new experience.
- $V$ = Value placed on new wealth creation.
- $R$ = Resistance to change from social institutions.

**Step 3: Cultural Inertia**
A high $R$ (e.g., caste-based occupational rigidity) reduces entrepreneurship. A high $A \cdot V$ promotes it.

**Step 4: Application to Kerala**
- **Kerala has high literacy** (high $A$).
- **Kerala's remittance economy** has historically lowered $V$ (preference for stable Gulf jobs over risky ventures).
- **KSUM and IEDCs are explicitly designed to lower $R$** by changing the cultural narrative around startups.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: The Entrepreneurship Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD

```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Types of Entrepreneurs (Hierarchical Classification)

```mermaid
flowchart TD

```
