> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Examination Tip:** In a 14-mark answer, drawing **one well-labelled Mermaid-style diagram** (or a hand-drawn equivalent) adds **1–2 extra marks**. Examiners actively reward visual organization.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Part A consists of short-answer questions. Each carries 3 marks. Total marks for Part A in KTU 2024 ESE = 30 marks (10 questions, answer 5 to 7).**

### Question 1: Define Entrepreneurship. Mention any three characteristics of an entrepreneur. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Entrepreneurship is the process of identifying a market opportunity, mobilizing resources, and bearing calculated risk to create a new business venture that delivers innovative products or services for profit. According to **Peter Drucker**, "***Entrepreneurship is a practice, not a personality trait.***"

**Three Characteristics:**
1. **Risk-bearing capacity** — Willingness to invest personal capital into uncertain ventures.
2. **Innovativeness** — Constant drive to create new products, processes, or markets (Schumpeter).
3. **Need for achievement (nAch)** — High internal drive to excel, as theorized by **McClelland (1961)**.

*(For 3 marks, students should write the definition in 2–3 lines + 3 distinct characteristics in bullet form.)*

---

### Question 2: Distinguish between an Entrepreneur and a Manager. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Entrepreneur | Manager |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Create a new venture / innovation | Run an existing organization efficiently |
| **Risk** | Bears personal financial risk | Does not bear personal risk |
| **Reward** | Profit + capital gains | Salary + bonus |
| **Approach** | Strategic, disruptive | Operational, incremental |

*Note: An entrepreneur focuses on **doing the right thing** (effectiveness), while a manager focuses on **doing things right** (efficiency).*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfall**
>
> Students frequently lose 2–4 marks on Part B answers by:
> 1. **Forgetting to name the scholar** behind a theory (always write "Schumpeter", "Knight", "Drucker", "McClelland").
> 2. **Writing generic definitions** instead of technology-management-specific examples.
> 3. **Skipping diagrams** — at least one block diagram or flowchart is mandatory.
> 4. **Not connecting theory to Indian/Kerala context** — examiners reward local examples (KSUM, Maker Village, Kerala IT parks).
> 5. **Omitting the year** of publication of theories (e.g., Schumpeter 1934, McClelland 1961).

### Question A: 14 Marks — Comprehensive Answer on Entrepreneurship

**[KTU University Exam - Module 1 Choice Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**Part (a): Define Entrepreneurship. Explain in detail Schumpeter's Theory of Innovation. Discuss its relevance to the Indian technology startup ecosystem. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Definition of Entrepreneurship**
Entrepreneurship is the process of designing, launching, and managing a new business venture to deliver a new product, service, or process to the market, undertaken by an **entrepreneur** who bears the calculated risk for profit. Schumpeter defined the entrepreneur as the agent of **"creative destruction"** who introduces **Neue Kombinationen (new combinations)** into the market.

**Step 2: Schumpeter's Five New Combinations**
1. Introduction of a **new product** (e.g., Ola Electric scooter).
2. Introduction of a **new production method** (e.g., 3D printing in jewellery).
3. Opening of a **new market** (e.g., UPI-based fintech in rural India).
4. Acquisition of a **new source of raw material** (e.g., lithium mining in South America for EV batteries).
5. Creation of a **new organizational structure** (e.g., flat agile teams in Indian SaaS startups).

**Step 3: Creative Destruction Equation**
The economic system is in equilibrium $Q_{eq} = f(C, L, K, T)$. The entrepreneur introduces innovation $I$ that shifts the equilibrium to $Q_{new} = Q_{eq} + \Delta I$. The entrepreneur captures temporary monopoly profit $\pi = (P_{new} - C_{new}) \cdot Q_{innov}$ until rivals imitate.

**Step 4: Relevance to Indian Tech Startup Ecosystem**
- India's **third-largest startup ecosystem globally** (over **120,000+ DPIIT-recognized startups** as of 2024).
- Schemes like **Startup India (2016)**, **Standup India**, and **Atal Innovation Mission (AIM)** operationalize Schumpeter's theory.
- Kerala's **Kerala Startup Mission (KSUM)** and **Maker Village** are direct institutional embodiments of "Neue Kombinationen".

*(Awarded 7 marks: 2 for definition, 2 for the 5 combinations, 2 for creative destruction mechanism, 1 for Indian context.)*

---

**Part (b): Explain McClelland's Need for Achievement Theory. How does it apply to engineering graduates in India? [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: McClelland's Premise (1961)**
David C. McClelland proposed that an individual's need for achievement (nAch) is the strongest psychological predictor of entrepreneurial intent:

$$nAch \uparrow \quad \Longrightarrow \quad P(\text{entrepreneurial career}) \uparrow$$

**Step 2: Measurement of nAch**
The **Thematic Apperception Test (TAT)** is used. Subjects write imaginative stories about ambiguous images. Achievement-themed storylines are counted and scored.

**Step 3: Behavioural Traits of High-nAch Individuals**
- Prefer tasks of **moderate difficulty** (≈50% chance of success).
- Demand **personal responsibility** for outcomes.
- Seek **immediate concrete feedback**.
- Avoid both **very easy** and **very risky** tasks.

**Step 4: Application to Engineering Graduates in India**
- KTU 2024 Scheme mandates **final-year capstone projects** that require autonomy and responsibility — directly aligned with high-nAch behaviour.
- **IEDC (Innovation and Entrepreneurship Development Cells)** in every KTU college serve as incubators.
- Government initiatives like **SBI Youth for India**, **NASSCOM 10,000 Startups**, and **Kerala IT Park startup grants** provide the **immediate feedback** (funding, mentorship) that high-nAch individuals need.

*(Awarded 7 marks: 2 for theory, 2 for measurement, 1 for traits, 2 for Indian engineering application.)*

---

### Question B: 14 Marks — Alternative Comprehensive Answer

**[KTU University Exam - Module 1 Choice Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**Part (a): Discuss the different types of entrepreneurs. With suitable examples, explain the role of a Technopreneur in the modern technology industry. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Classification of Entrepreneurs**
Entrepreneurs can be classified into seven major types:

1. **Innovative Entrepreneur** — Introduces new products/technologies. *Example: Elon Musk (Tesla, SpaceX).*
2. **Imitative Entrepreneur** — Adapts proven models to local markets. *Example: Indian franchisees of McDonald's.*
3. **Fabian Entrepreneur** — Highly cautious, slow to innovate. *Example: Traditional textile family businesses in Kerala.*
4. **Drone Entrepreneur** — Refuses to innovate; eventually fails. *Example: Kodak's refusal to adopt digital photography.*
5. **Opportunistic Entrepreneur** — Seizes every profitable chance. *Example: Day traders, micro-SaaS owners.*
6. **Technopreneur** — Technology-led entrepreneur. *Example: Bhavish Aggarwal (Ola), Vijay Shekhar Sharma (Paytm).*
7. **Intrapreneur** — Internal corporate innovator. *Example: Gmail team at Google, Post-it Notes at 3M.*

**Step 2: Definition of Technopreneur**
A technopreneur is an entrepreneur who builds a business around cutting-edge technology — typically **AI, ML, IoT, blockchain, robotics, biotech, or clean energy**. They combine deep technical knowledge with business acumen.

**Step 3: Role in Modern Technology Industry**
- **Translates R&D into products**: Converts laboratory research into commercial products (e.g., IISc Bengaluru → Ather Energy).
- **Drives the knowledge economy**: Technopreneurs fuel high-skill, high-wage job creation.
- **Attracts FDI**: India's tech startup ecosystem attracted **USD 25 billion+ in FDI in 2023**.
- **Builds IP**: Indian tech startups filed **70,000+ patents in 2022–2023**.

**Step 4: Indian Examples of Technopreneurs**
- **Sridhar Vembu** (Zoho) — bootstrapped SaaS company from rural Tamil Nadu.
- **Bhavish Aggarwal** (Ola, Ola Electric) — mobility + EV technology.
- **Vijay Shekhar Sharma** (Paytm) — fintech technology.
- **Kunal Shah** (CRED) — fintech + consumer technology.
- **Falguni Nayar** (Nykaa) — beauty-tech omnichannel.

*(Awarded 7 marks: 2 for classification, 2 for technopreneur definition, 1 for role, 2 for Indian examples.)*

---

**Part (b): Explain Knight's Risk-Bearing Theory of Entrepreneurship. How does it differ from Schumpeter's Innovation Theory? [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Knight's Theory (1921)**
Frank H. Knight, in his landmark book ***"Risk, Uncertainty, and Profit" (1921)***, argued that the entrepreneur is fundamentally a **bearer of uncertainty**, not just risk. Knight made a sharp distinction:
- **Risk** — measurable, insurable, with known probability distributions.
- **Uncertainty** — unmeasurable, uninsurable, with unknown probability distributions.

**Step 2: Knight's Profit Equation**
The entrepreneur earns profit as a compensation for bearing non-insurable uncertainty:

$$P = R \times \sigma(E)$$

where $P$ is profit, $R$ is the risk premium, and $\sigma(E)$ is the standard deviation of expected returns.

**Step 3: Knight's Three-Class Society**
Knight divided society into three classes:
1. **Risk-bearers** (entrepreneurs) — face non-insurable uncertainty.
2. **Contracted income earners** (managers, workers) — face insurable risk.
3. **Property owners** (shareholders) — receive residual income.

**Step 4: Comparison with Schumpeter's Theory**

| Parameter | Knight's Theory | Schumpeter's Theory |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Uncertainty-bearing | Innovation |
| **Reward** | Profit (compensation) | Profit (monopoly rent) |
| **Time** | Static (ongoing) | Dynamic (disruptive) |
| **Entrepreneur's Role** | Decision-maker under uncertainty | Disruptor of equilibrium |
| **Source of Profit** | Risk premium | Innovation rent |
| **Year** | 1921 | 1934 |
| **Critique** | Ignores innovation as a driver | Ignores routine risk-bearing |

**Step 5: Synthesis**
In modern technology management, both theories are complementary. An entrepreneur at **Infosys** is a Knightian risk-bearer (uncertainty of client demand), while an entrepreneur at **Tesla** is a Schumpeterian disruptor (innovative EV technology). Real-world entrepreneurs blend both roles.

*(Awarded 7 marks: 2 for Knight's theory, 1 for equation, 2 for comparison table, 2 for synthesis and Indian examples.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Entrepreneurship**

- ✅ **Entrepreneurship** = Designing + launching + running a new venture for profit by bearing calculated risk.
- ✅ **Entrepreneur** = Opportunity-seeker, risk-bearer, innovator (Schumpeter); Achievement-motivated (McClelland); Uncertainty-bearer (Knight).
- ✅ **Intrapreneur** = Entrepreneur inside a large firm (coined by Gifford Pinchot III).
- ✅ **7 Types of Entrepreneurs** = Innovative, Imitative, Fabian, Drone, Opportunistic, Technopreneur, Intrapreneur.
- ✅ **Schumpeter's 5 Neue Kombinationen** = New product, new method, new market, new raw material source, new organization.
- ✅ **Creative Destruction** = Innovation-driven replacement of old products/processes.
- ✅ **McClelland (1961)** = Need for Achievement (nAch) drives entrepreneurs; measured via TAT.
- ✅ **Knight (1921)** = Risk vs. Uncertainty distinction; profit is compensation for non-insurable uncertainty.
- ✅ **Drucker (1985)** = "Entrepreneurship is a discipline that can be learned."
- ✅ **Cochran (1965)** = Cultural theory: $E_{success} = \frac{A \cdot V}{R}$ (attitude, value, resistance).
- ✅ **Weber (1905)** = Protestant Ethic — religious-cultural roots of capitalism.
- ✅ **4 Functions** = Innovation, Risk-bearing, Resource Mobilization, Managerial (POADC).
- ✅ **10 Characteristics** = Risk-bearing, Innovativeness, Opportunity-seeking, Self-confidence, nAch, Leadership, Persistence, Hard work, Future orientation, Independence.
- ✅ **Indian Context** = Startup India, Standup India, AIM, KSUM, Maker Village, IEDC.
- ✅ **Remember the years**: Schumpeter 1934, Knight 1921, McClelland 1961, Drucker 1985, Cochran 1965, Weber 1905.
- ✅ **Always draw at least one diagram** in a 14-mark answer.
- ✅ **Always quote a scholar** with the year.
- ✅ **Always give one Indian or Kerala example** (KSUM, Maker Village, Ola, Zoho, Paytm).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Patents & Intellectual Property Rights
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET417 - TECHNOLOGY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Patents & Intellectual Property Rights

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Intellectual Property Rights (IPR) — Formal Definition

**Intellectual Property Rights (IPR)** are the legally recognized exclusive rights granted to creators, inventors, and owners of intangible assets arising from human intellect, creativity, and innovation. These rights protect creations of the mind across industrial, scientific, literary, artistic, and commercial domains for a defined statutory period.

Under the **WTO-TRIPS Agreement (Trade-Related Aspects of Intellectual Property Rights, 1994)**, IPR is harmonized globally into seven primary categories.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** IPR is the foundation of technology commercialization. Without IPR protection, an inventor has no legal mechanism to prevent unauthorized commercial exploitation of his/her innovation.

## 1.2 Patent — Formal Definition

A **Patent** is a statutory, time-bound, territorial monopoly right granted by the government to an inventor (or assignee) in exchange for public disclosure of an invention. It confers the **exclusive right to manufacture, use, sell, or import** the patented invention, and to exclude others from doing so without authorization.

The statutory patent term in India, under the **Patents Act, 1970 (as amended in 2005)**, is **20 years** from the date of filing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Statutory Criteria for Patentability (Section 2(1)(j) of the Patents Act, 1970):**
> 1. **Novelty** — The invention must be new (not part of "prior art")
> 2. **Inventive Step (Non-Obviousness)** — A person skilled in the art must not find it obvious
> 3. **Industrial Application** — The invention must be capable of being made or used in an industry

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The House Deed for an Idea"

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Pump:** Imagine you build a unique treehouse in your backyard.
> - The **physical treehouse** = your invention (a tangible outcome)
> - The **land deed** = the patent (legal ownership of that idea)
> - The **fence around it** = the legal right to stop neighbors from copying the design
> - The **20-year expiry** = after which your design becomes public knowledge
> - The **public disclosure (blueprint)** = what you give society in exchange for protection

Just as a deed doesn't prevent trespassers by itself but gives you legal recourse, a patent doesn't physically block copying — it gives the inventor the **legal weapon** to sue infringers and claim damages.

## 1.4 Other Major Forms of IPR (Quick Recognition Table)

| # | IPR Type | Protects | Term | Registration? |
|---|----------|----------|------|---------------|
| 1 | **Patent** | Inventions / Processes / Products | 20 years | Yes (mandatory) |
| 2 | **Trademark** | Brand name, logo, symbol | 10 years (renewable) | Yes (optional, but recommended) |
| 3 | **Copyright** | Literary, artistic, musical, software works | Life + 60 years | Yes (automatic on creation) |
| 4 | **Industrial Design** | Aesthetic appearance of an article | 10 years (renewable) | Yes |
| 5 | **Geographical Indication (GI)** | Origin-linked product (e.g., Darjeeling Tea) | 10 years (renewable) | Yes |
| 6 | **Trade Secret** | Confidential business / technical info | Indefinite (while secret) | No (protection by confidentiality) |
| 7 | **Plant Variety** | New plant varieties | 15–18 years | Yes (PPVFR Act, 2001) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for KTU Exams:** When asked "Differentiate between Patent and Copyright", remember:
> - Patent = protects **functional / technical** ideas (process or product).
> - Copyright = protects **expression** of ideas (text, music, art, code).
> - Software can be **dual-protected** — the source code (copyright) and the algorithm/function (patent).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Patent Law

Patent law exists to solve a fundamental economic problem:

> If inventors cannot prevent free-riding, they have **no incentive to invest** in costly R&D. But if protection is too strong, society is **denied access** to useful inventions forever.

**The Bargain:** The inventor discloses the invention publicly (so society learns) → in return, the government grants a **temporary monopoly** (20 years) → after expiry, the invention enters the **public domain**.

## 2.2 Criteria for Patentability — Detailed Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Novelty (Section 2(1)(l))
- Absolute worldwide novelty is required in India post-2005.
- The invention must not have been **published, used, or disclosed** anywhere in the world before the filing date.
- **Prior Art** = any evidence that the invention was already known.

### 2.2.2 Inventive Step (Section 2(1)(ja))
- The invention must not be obvious to a "person skilled in the art" (PHOSITA).
- Even if technically novel, trivial modifications of existing devices fail this test.

### 2.2.3 Industrial Application (Section 2(1)(ac))
- The invention must be capable of being **manufactured or used in a useful industrial setting**.
- Pure scientific theories, mathematical methods, and abstract ideas are excluded.

## 2.3 What is **NOT** Patentable in India (Section 3 & 4)

| Excluded Category | Example |
|------------------|---------|
| Frivolous inventions | Perpetual motion machine |
| Inventions contrary to public order/morality | Weapons of mass destruction |
| Discovery of scientific principle | Law of gravity, E=mc² |
| Mere new use of known substance | Aspirin for headache (already known) |
| Atomic energy inventions | Nuclear weapon design |
| Plants & animals (except microbes) | Traditional plant varieties |
| Business methods, computer programs "per se" | Pure algorithms (though software-implemented inventions are debatable) |

## 2.4 Types of Patent Applications (India — Forms)

| Form | Purpose | Applicant Type |
|------|---------|---------------|
| **Form 1** | Application for grant of patent | All applicants |
| **Form 2** | Provisional / Complete specification | Inventor |
| **Form 5** | Declaration of inventorship | Inventor |
| **Form 26** | Power of attorney | Agent (if filed through patent attorney) |
| **PCT (Form PCT/RO/101)** | International application under Patent Cooperation Treaty | Foreign applicants |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Patent Lifecycle

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{filing}} &\rightarrow T_{\text{publication (18 months)}} \\
&\rightarrow T_{\text{examination request (48 months)}} \\
&\rightarrow T_{\text{First Examination Report (FER)}} \\
&\rightarrow T_{\text{hearing (if objections)}} \\
&\rightarrow T_{\text{grant}} \rightarrow T_{\text{annuity payments (3rd year onwards)}} \\
&\rightarrow T_{\text{expiry (20 years from filing)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maintenance Requirement:** Patents lapse if **annual renewal fees (annuities)** are not paid from the 3rd year onwards. Missing even one year can result in restoration complications.

## 2.6 Patent Infringement & Remedies

**Infringement** = unauthorized manufacture, use, sale, offer for sale, or import of the patented invention.

| Type of Infringement | Description |
|----------------------|-------------|
| **Direct Infringement** | Direct violation of patent claims |
| **Indirect Infringement** | Supplying components used to infringe |
| **Induced Infringement** | Actively inducing another party to infringe |

**Statutory Remedies (Section 108 of Patents Act, 1970):**
- Injunction (temporary or permanent)
- Damages or account of profits
- Seizure of infringing goods
- Costs of litigation
- *In India, criminal prosecution is generally NOT available for patent infringement — only civil remedies.*

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Role of Patent |
|----------|---------------|
| Pharmaceuticals | Protects blockbuster drugs (e.g., Novartis Gleevec case) |
| Semiconductors | Multi-billion dollar cross-licensing portfolios (Intel, TSMC, Samsung) |
| Telecom | SEPs (Standard Essential Patents) in 4G/5G (Ericsson vs Xiaomi) |
| Automotive | EV battery technology patents (LG Energy vs SK Innovation) |
| Software / AI | Algorithmic patents in ML, NLP, computer vision |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pickup Line:** "The patent system is the engine that converts scientific knowledge into commercial technology — without it, India would still be a pure generic manufacturing hub."
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Process Walkthrough & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Patent Filing Procedure (India)

Below is the exhaustive procedural flow every KTU answer should walk through.

### Step 1 — Invention Disclosure & Documentation
- The inventor records the invention in a signed, witnessed **Invention Disclosure Form (IDF)**.
- All experimental data, drawings, prototype images, and results are archived with date stamps.

### Step 2 — Prior Art Search
- Conducted through databases such as **Indian Patent Advanced Search System (IPAIS)**, **Espacenet**, **Google Patents**, **WIPO PATENTSCOPE**, and **USPTO**.
- Goal: ensure novelty and assess freedom-to-operate.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Patentability Score (qualitative)} &= \text{Novelty} \times \text{Non-Obviousness} \times \text{Industrial Use} \\
\text{Decision} &=
\begin{cases}
\text{PROCEED with filing} & \text{if all three criteria are positive} \\
\text{REFINE the claims} & \text{if partial overlap with prior art} \\
\text{ABANDON} & \text{if prior art completely anticipates the invention}
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Drafting the Patent Specification
A specification has two parts:

**Provisional Specification (Form 2 — Provisional):**
- Filed when invention is not fully developed.
- Establishes **priority date** with 12 months to file complete specification.

**Complete Specification (Form 2 — Complete):**
Contains four mandatory components:

| Section | Content |
|---------|---------|
| **Title** | Concise description of invention |
| **Field of Invention** | Technical domain |
| **Background** | Prior art, problem statement |
| **Summary** | High-level description of solution |
| **Brief Description of Drawings** | If any |
| **Detailed Description** | Full working detail |
| **Claims** | Legally enforceable scope (most important) |
| **Abstract** | 150-word technical summary |

### Step 4 — Filing the Application (Form 1, 2, 5, 26)
- Filed at the appropriate **Patent Office** (Chennai, Mumbai, Kolkata, or Delhi).
- Filing fee varies for natural persons, startups, small entities, and others.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fee}_{\text{filing}} &= f(\text{Entity Type}, \text{Application Type}) \\
\text{where Entity Type} &\in \{\text{Natural Person}, \text{Startup}, \text{Small Entity}, \text{Others}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Publication
- The application is **automatically published 18 months** after priority date.
- From publication, applicant gets **provisional rights** (Right to claim reasonable damages from the date of publication, if the patent is eventually granted).

### Step 6 — Request for Examination (Form 18)
- Must be filed within **48 months** of priority date.
- A Patent Examiner examines the application for novelty, inventive step, and industrial applicability.

### Step 7 — First Examination Report (FER) & Response
- Examiner issues FER with objections.
- Applicant has **6 months** (extendable up to 3 months) to respond.
- Common objections: lack of novelty, insufficient disclosure, excluded subject matter.

### Step 8 — Hearing & Grant
- If objections are overcome, the patent is **granted and published** in the Patent Journal.
- The patent number is assigned and the **20-year term** begins from the priority/filing date.

### Step 9 — Post-Grant Maintenance
- Pay **annual renewal fees** from the 3rd year onwards.
- Failure to pay → patent lapses (can be restored within 18 months with surcharge).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Difference Between Patents and Other IPRs

| Parameter | **Patent** | **Copyright** | **Trademark** | **Trade Secret** |
|-----------|------------|---------------|---------------|-------------------|
| Protects | Functional inventions | Expression of ideas | Brand identifiers | Confidential info |
| Granted by | Patent Office | Automatic on creation | Trademark Registry | No registration needed |
| Term | 20 years | Life + 60 years | 10 years (renewable) | Indefinite (if secret) |
| Need to disclose | Yes (full disclosure) | No (work stays private until published) | No (mark is public) | No (must be kept secret) |
| Geographical scope | Territorial | Territorial (Berne Convention) | Territorial | Limited to holder's reach |
| Cost | High | Low | Moderate | Enforcement cost |
| Example | Tesla's EV battery | Tollywood film | Nike "swoosh" | Coca-Cola formula |
| In India governed by | Patents Act, 1970 | Copyright Act, 1957 | Trade Marks Act, 1999 | Common law / contracts |

## 3.3 Working Statement (Section 146 of Patents Act)

In India, every granted patent must be **worked (commercialized) in India** within **3 years of grant**. Failure can trigger:

- **Compulsory License** — government can grant license to third parties.
- **Revocation** — patent can be revoked for non-working.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** This is a unique Indian provision to prevent "patent trolling" and ensure patents benefit Indian industry.

## 3.4 International Patent Filing Mechanisms

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Filing Options} &=
\begin{cases}
\text{Direct National Filing} & : \text{File individually in each country} \\
\text{Paris Convention Route} & : \text{Priority within 12 months in member countries} \\
\text{PCT (Patent Cooperation Treaty)} & : \text{Single international application, 142+ countries} \\
\text{Regional (e.g., EPO, ARIPO)} & : \text{One grant valid in multiple countries}
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Taxonomy of Intellectual Property Rights

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS] --> B[Industrial Property]
    A --> C[Copyright and Related Rights]
    A --> D[Trade Secrets]
    B --> E[PATENTS]
    B --> F[Trademarks]
    B --> G[Industrial Designs]
    B --> H[Geographical Indications]
    B --> I[Layout Designs of ICs]
    C --> J[Literary Works]
    C --> K[Artistic Works]
    C --> L[Musical Works]
    C --> M[Computer Software]
    D --> N[Technical Know-How]
    D --> O[Business Plans]
    D --> P[Customer Lists]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:** Patents belong to the **Industrial Property** branch of IPR — they protect functional, utilitarian creations, as opposed to Copyright which protects expressive creations.

## 4.2 Patent Lifecycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Invention Conception] --> B[Prior Art Search]
    B --> C{Invention Novel?}
    C -- No --> X1[Abandon or Modify]
    C -- Yes --> D[File Provisional Spec]
    D --> E[Within 12 months: File Complete Spec]
    E --> F[Application Published: 18 months]
    F --> G[Request Examination: within 48 months]
    G --> H[Examination by Patent Office]
    H --> I{Objections?}
    I -- No --> J[Patent Granted]
    I -- Yes --> K[Respond to FER: 6 months]
    K --> H
    J --> L[Pay Annual Annuity from Year 3]
    L --> M[20-Year Term Expiry]
    M --> N[Public Domain]
```

## 4.3 Patent Infringement Analysis Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Suspected Infringing Product/Process Identified] --> B[Read Patent Claims Carefully]
    B --> C{Does Accused Product Contain EVERY Element of a Patent Claim?}
    C -- No --> D[NO INFRINGEMENT: Doctrine of Equivalents may apply]
    C -- Yes --> E[INFRINGEMENT CONFIRMED]
    E --> F{Type of Infringement?}
    F --> G[Direct Infringement]
    F --> H[Indirect Infringement]
    F --> I[Induced Infringement]
    G --> J[Remedies: Injunction, Damages, Account of Profits]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    D --> K[Defenses Available]
    K --> L[Prior Use]
    K --> M[Invalidity of Patent]
    K --> N[Licensing Permission]
    K --> O[Bolar Exemption: Research Use]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Patent Filing Routes in India

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Applicant] --> B{File in India or Abroad?}
    B -- India Only --> C[Direct Filing at Indian Patent Office]
    B -- Abroad --> D{PCT or Paris Convention?}
    D -- Paris --> E[National Phase in Each Country within 12 months]
    D -- PCT --> F[International Phase: 30-31 months from priority]
    F --> G[Enter National Phase in Desired Countries]
    C --> H[Patent Grant in India]
    E --> H2[Patent Grant in Target Country]
    G --> H2
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Define Intellectual Property Rights. List any four forms of IPR.**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Intellectual Property Rights (IPR) refer to the legal rights granted by a government to creators and inventors to protect their intangible creations of the mind, such as inventions, literary and artistic works, designs, and symbols, for a specified period.

**Four Forms of IPR (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
1. **Patents** — protect functional inventions
2. **Copyrights** — protect literary, artistic, musical works
3. **Trademarks** — protect brand names and logos
4. **Trade Secrets** — protect confidential business information

*(Alternative forms acceptable: Industrial Designs, Geographical Indications, Layout Designs of Integrated Circuits)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**What are the three essential criteria for an invention to be patentable under the Indian Patents Act, 1970?**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

Under **Section 2(1)(j)** of the Indian Patents Act, 1970, an invention must satisfy the following three criteria (1 mark each):

1. **Novelty** — The invention must be new and not disclosed in any prior art anywhere in the world before the filing date.

2. **Inventive Step (Non-Obviousness)** — The invention must not be obvious to a person skilled in the art (PHOSITA) given the existing prior art.

3. **Industrial Application (Utility)** — The invention must be capable of being made or used in a useful industrial or commercial setting.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write "useful" or "practical" instead of "Industrial Application" — the term used in the Act is **"Industrial Application"** specifically. Use the statutory terminology in KTU answers.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Explain the procedure for filing a patent application in India. Discuss the role of specification, claims, and publication. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **Patents and Copyrights**. Mention the relevant Acts and statutory terms. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q(a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Invention Disclosure (1 mark):**
The inventor records the invention in a signed, dated Invention Disclosure Form, documenting the technical problem, solution, and experimental data.

**Step 2 — Prior Art Search (1 mark):**
Conducted through IPAIS, Espacenet, Google Patents, and PATENTSCOPE to ensure the invention is novel and to assess freedom-to-operate.

**Step 3 — Filing the Application (2 marks):**
- Submit **Form 1** (Application for grant of patent)
- **Form 2** — Provisional or Complete Specification
- **Form 5** — Declaration of inventorship
- **Form 26** — Power of Attorney (if filed through agent)
- Filed at one of the four Patent Offices: Chennai, Mumbai, Kolkata, or Delhi.

**Step 4 — Specification and Claims (2 marks):**
- **Specification** = the full technical description enabling a person skilled in the art to reproduce the invention.
- **Claims** = the legally enforceable boundaries defining the scope of protection. Claims are the most important part; infringement is judged solely by claim language.
- Provisional specification establishes priority date (12 months to file complete).

**Step 5 — Publication & Examination (1 mark):**
- Application is **published 18 months** after priority date → gives provisional rights.
- Examination request (Form 18) within **48 months**.
- FER issued → response within 6 months → grant or refusal after hearing.

#### Model Solution for Q(b) — 7 marks

| Parameter | **Patent** (3.5 marks) | **Copyright** (3.5 marks) |
|-----------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| Subject Matter | Functional inventions (processes, products, machines) | Expression of ideas (literary, artistic, musical) |
| Statute | **Patents Act, 1970** | **Copyright Act, 1957** |
| Term | **20 years** from filing | **Life of author + 60 years** |
| Registration | Mandatory (granted by Patent Office) | Automatic on creation (registration optional for evidence) |
| Disclosure | Full public disclosure required | No disclosure required |
| Example | A new drug molecule | A novel by Arundhati Roy |
| Infringement | Civil remedies — injunction, damages | Civil + Criminal remedies |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Common Valuation Pitfall:** Students often quote "21 years" or "15 years" for patent term. The **correct patent term in India post-2005 amendment is 20 years** from the date of filing (TRIPS-compliant). Any other number = 0 marks for that sub-question.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Discuss the salient features of the **WTO-TRIPS Agreement**. How does it impact patent protection globally? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **Compulsory License** under the Indian Patents Act. State the grounds on which it can be issued. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q(a) — 7 marks

**1. Background (1 mark):**
The **Trade-Related Aspects of Intellectual Property Rights (TRIPS) Agreement** was negotiated in 1994 under the **Uruguay Round of GATT** and came into force on **1 January 1995**. It is administered by the WTO.

**2. Salient Features (4 marks — 1 each minimum):**

- **Minimum Standards of Protection:** TRIPS sets minimum IP standards that all 164+ WTO member countries must implement in their national laws.

- **Seven Categories of IP Covered:** Copyright, Trademarks, Patents, Industrial Designs, Geographical Indications, Layout Designs of ICs, and Trade Secrets (undisclosed information).

- **Patent Term Standardization:** All members must provide a **patent term of at least 20 years** from the filing date.

- **National Treatment & Most-Favoured-Nation (MFN):** Foreign nationals must be treated the same as domestic nationals, and benefits extended to one member must be extended to all.

- **Enforcement Mechanisms:** Provides detailed provisions for civil, criminal, and administrative enforcement, including border measures.

- **Dispute Settlement:** IP disputes between member countries are resolved through the WTO Dispute Settlement Body (DSB).

**3. Global Impact (2 marks):**
- Harmonized IP laws across developed and developing nations.
- Strengthened protection for pharmaceuticals and software patents.
- Pushed developing countries like India to amend their Patent Act (2005) to allow **product patents in drugs and agrochemicals**.
- Triggered debates on access to medicines (e.g., Doha Declaration on TRIPS and Public Health, 2001).

#### Model Solution for Q(b) — 7 marks

**1. Definition (2 marks):**
A **Compulsory License (CL)** is a government-authorized permission that allows a third party to manufacture, use, sell, or import a patented invention **without the consent of the patent holder**, subject to payment of royalty as determined by the Controller General of Patents.

**2. Statutory Provision (1 mark):**
Governed by **Chapter XVI (Sections 82–92)** of the Patents Act, 1970.

**3. Grounds for Granting Compulsory License (3 marks — any 3 well-explained):**

- **Reasonable requirements of the public are not being met.**
- The patented invention is **not available to the public at a reasonably affordable price.**
- The patented invention is **not being worked (manufactured) in India.**
- The reasonable royalties were refused by the patent holder.

**4. Famous Case Study (1 mark):**
**Natco Pharma vs Bayer (2012)** — India's first compulsory license was issued to Natco Pharma to manufacture Bayer's cancer drug **Sorafenib Tosylate (Nexavar)** at ₹8,800/month vs Bayer's price of ₹2.8 lakh/month. Royalty set at 6% of net sales.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes in Compulsory License Answers:**
> - Confusing "Compulsory License" with "Revocation" — they are different.
> - Forgetting that CL is **not automatic** — it requires an application and a hearing.
> - Forgetting to mention **royalty payment** to the patent holder.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 KTU Rapid Revision Checklist — Patents & IPR**

- **IPR** = legal rights over intangible creations of the human mind.
- **Patent** = statutory 20-year monopoly for functional inventions, granted in exchange for full public disclosure.
- **Three Patentability Criteria:** Novelty, Inventive Step (Non-Obviousness), Industrial Application.
- **Statute:** Indian Patents Act, **1970** (amended in **2005** to allow product patents in pharma & agrochemicals).
- **Patent Term in India:** **20 years** from the date of filing (post-2005).
- **Not Patentable:** atomic energy, plants/animals (except microbes), abstract ideas, business methods "per se", computer programs "per se", discoveries, immoral inventions.
- **Patent Specification** has two parts — Provisional (priority date) and Complete (full technical detail + claims).
- **Claims** = the legally enforceable scope of the patent; infringement is judged by claim language alone.
- **Publication** is automatic at **18 months**; **Examination Request** must be filed within **48 months**.
- **Working Requirement** — patent must be commercialized in India within **3 years of grant**, else CL or revocation.
- **Compulsory License** = Chapter XVI (Sections 82–92); famous case: **Natco vs Bayer (Nexavar, 2012)**.
- **TRIPS Agreement (1995)** sets global minimum IP standards; **20-year patent term** is the TRIPS baseline.
- **Copyright** protects expression (Life + 60 years); **Trademark** protects brand (10 years, renewable); **Trade Secret** has no term but requires confidentiality.
- **Bolar Exemption** allows generic manufacturers to use a patented invention for regulatory testing before patent expiry.
- **PCT** (Patent Cooperation Treaty) allows a single international application valid in 142+ countries, with a **30/31-month** national phase entry deadline.
- **Annual renewal (annuity) fees** are due from the **3rd year** onwards — failure to pay = patent lapses.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for KTU:** **"N-I-I"** = **N**ovelty, **I**nventive step, **I**ndustrial application. These are the only three gates an invention must pass to become a patent. Memorize this triple and the rest of the theory flows naturally.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Technology Concept: Significant Technology Changes During the Last 2 Centuries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET417 - TECHNOLOGY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Technology Concept: Significant Technology Changes During the Last 2 Centuries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Technology"? (KTU Definition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formal Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> **Technology** is the systematic application of scientific knowledge, tools, techniques, skills, methods, and processes to create, modify, improve, and utilize artifacts, systems, services, or environments for solving practical problems and enhancing human welfare.

In KTU's management framework, technology is **NOT just gadgets or machines**. It is the **entire socio-technical ecosystem** — including:

- 🛠️ **Physical artifacts** (machines, devices, infrastructure)
- 📚 **Knowledge & know-how** (procedures, processes, intellectual property)
- 🏭 **Organizational structures** (factories, R&D labs, supply chains)
- 👥 **Human skills** (engineering, craftsmanship, tacit knowledge)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The River of Innovation"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of technology as a **great river** flowing through human history.
> - Each century is a **new tributary** joining the river.
> - The **First Industrial Revolution** was the spring source (steam).
> - The **Second** added massive flow (electricity & chemicals).
> - The **Third** accelerated it into a torrent (digital computing).
> - The **Fourth** turned it into a network of streams (AI, IoT, biotech).
>
> A student today stands at a point where **3 cm of river history = 100 years of human progress** because the pace is **exponential, not linear.**

## 1.3 The Four Industrial Revolutions — A Bird's-Eye View

The last 2 centuries (≈ 1825–2025) have witnessed **four transformative waves** of technological change. KTU expects you to remember each wave with its **core technology, timeline, and impact.**

| Wave | Period | Defining Technology | Trigger Event |
|:----:|:------:|:-------------------|:--------------|
| **1IR** | ~1760–1840 | Steam Engine & Mechanization | James Watt's improved steam engine (**1769**) |
| **2IR** | ~1870–1914 | Electricity, Steel, Chemicals | Edison's light bulb, Bessemer Steel |
| **3IR** | ~1950–2000 | Electronics, Computing, Internet | Invention of the **Transistor (1947)** |
| **4IR** | ~2010–Present | AI, IoT, Biotech, Cyber-Physical | IBM Watson (2011), Deep learning boom |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick: "S-E-E-A"** — **S**team → **E**lectricity → **E**lectronics → **A**I/Autonomy.

## 1.4 Why These Changes Matter (KTU Perspective)

The **PEMET417 course outcome** demands that managers understand technology not as trivia, but as a **strategic driver** of:

- 🌍 **Economic growth** (GDP, productivity, trade)
- 🏢 **Industry structure** (rise and fall of firms/sectors)
- 👷 **Work & employment** (skill shifts, job displacement)
- 🌐 **Society & ethics** (privacy, inequality, sustainability)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Sheet

## 2.1 The Theoretical Foundation — Schumpeter's "Gales of Creative Destruction"

> [!NOTE]
> **Schumpeter's Theory (1942):** Technological change drives capitalism through **"gales of creative destruction"** — new innovations destroy old industries while creating entirely new ones.
> - Example: The digital camera destroyed Kodak's film empire but birthed Instagram.
> - Example: Smartphones destroyed Nokia's dominance but created Apple's trillion-dollar valuation.

## 2.2 The S-Curve of Technology Adoption

Every major technology follows an **S-shaped diffusion curve** — a foundational concept KTU loves to test.

```
Adoption %
    │                    ●●●●●●  ← Saturation
    │                 ●●●
    │               ●●
    │            ●●
    │         ●●
    │       ●●
    │    ●●●
    │ ●●●
    │●●
    └──────────────────────────── Time
     Innovators  Early  Majority  Laggards
     (2.5%)    (13.5%) (68%)   (16%)
```

**Five Adopter Categories (Rogers, 1962):**

1. **Innovators (2.5%)** — risk-takers, tech enthusiasts
2. **Early Adopters (13.5%)** — opinion leaders, visionaries
3. **Early Majority (34%)** — deliberate, mainstream
4. **Late Majority (34%)** — skeptics, price-sensitive
5. **Laggards (16%)** — traditional, resistant

## 2.3 Major Technological Breakthroughs — Detailed Timeline

### 🔥 Phase A: First Industrial Revolution (1760–1840) — "The Age of Steam"

- **1769** — James Watt patents the improved steam engine (efficiency: 3× Newcomen's design).
- **1784** — Power loom mechanizes textiles.
- **1804** — Richard Trevithick builds the first steam locomotive.
- **1837** — Telegraph invented by Cooke & Wheatstone.

> **Impact:** Productivity in textile industry **increased by ~400%** between 1760–1840.

### ⚡ Phase B: Second Industrial Revolution (1870–1914) — "The Age of Electricity & Mass Production"

- **1876** — Alexander Graham Bell patents the telephone.
- **1879** — Thomas Edison demonstrates a practical electric light bulb.
- **1885** — Karl Benz builds the first automobile (internal combustion engine).
- **1895** — Guglielmo Marconi transmits the first radio signal.
- **1903** — Wright Brothers achieve first powered flight (12 seconds, 120 ft).
- **1908** — Henry Ford's **Model T** + **assembly line** make cars affordable to the middle class.
- **1913** — Ford's Highland Park Plant: chassis assembly time drops from **12.5 hours → 93 minutes**.

### 💻 Phase C: Third Industrial Revolution (1950–2000) — "The Digital Age"

- **1947** — Bardeen, Brattain, Shockley invent the **transistor** (Nobel Prize 1956).
- **1958** — Jack Kilby invents the **integrated circuit** (IC).
- **1969** — **ARPANET** (precursor to Internet) goes live — 4 nodes, 50 kbps.
- **1971** — Intel 4004 — first commercial **microprocessor** (2,300 transistors).
- **1973** — Martin Cooper places the first **mobile phone call** (Motorola DynaTAC).
- **1981** — IBM PC launched — triggers the **personal computer revolution**.
- **1989** — Tim Berners-Lee proposes the **World Wide Web** at CERN.
- **1991** — First website goes live.
- **1995** — Amazon and eBay founded — start of **e-commerce**.
- **1998** — Google founded — start of the **search economy**.

> **Moore's Law (1965):** Gordon Moore observed that the number of transistors on an IC doubles approximately every **18–24 months**, while cost halves.

### 🤖 Phase D: Fourth Industrial Revolution (2010–Present) — "The Cyber-Physical Age"

- **2007** — Apple iPhone launches the **smartphone era**.
- **2011** — IBM Watson defeats human champions on Jeopardy!
- **2012** — **Deep learning** (AlexNet) shatters image-recognition benchmarks.
- **2012** — CRISPR-Cas9 gene-editing technology published (Doudna & Charpentier → Nobel 2020).
- **2014** — Amazon Echo / Alexa — **voice assistants** enter the home.
- **2016** — AlphaGo defeats Go world champion Lee Sedol.
- **2017** — Bitcoin reaches mainstream awareness — **blockchain** emerges.
- **2020** — mRNA vaccines (Moderna, Pfizer) deployed in < 1 year — biotech revolution.
- **2022** — ChatGPT reaches **100 million users in 2 months** — fastest-growing app in history.
- **2023–24** — Generative AI (text, image, code, video) reshapes creative industries.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Comparison Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table consolidates **all exam-relevant metrics** — memorize it for the 14-mark questions.

| Parameter | 1IR (Steam) | 2IR (Electricity) | 3IR (Digital) | 4IR (Cyber-Physical) |
|:----------|:-----------:|:-----------------:|:-------------:|:--------------------:|
| **Timeline** | 1760–1840 | 1870–1914 | 1950–2000 | 2010–Present |
| **Core Energy** | Coal & Steam | Electricity & Oil | Silicon & Electrons | Data & Intelligence |
| **Key Inventor** | James Watt | Edison, Tesla, Ford | Shockley, Kilby, Berners-Lee | Hinton, Altman, Hassabis |
| **Productivity Gain** | ~3–5× | ~5–10× | ~10–100× | Projected ~100–1000× |
| **Workforce Shift** | Agrarian → Industrial | Skilled → Assembly-line | Manual → Knowledge | Knowledge → Creative/Cognitive |
| **Communication Speed** | Weeks (postal) | Hours (telegraph/telephone) | Seconds (Internet) | Real-time (5G, IoT) |
| **Cost of Computation** | N/A | $\approx$ \$10,000/MIPS | $\approx$ \$0.01/MIPS (today) | $\approx$ \$0.0001/MIPS |
| **Famous Quote** | "Steam is king" | "Electricity changes everything" | "Information wants to be free" | "AI is the new electricity" |

> [!NOTE]
> The above table is **handwritten-ready** — most 14-mark answers from KTU students on this topic cite a similar comparative table.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of This Framework

- **For Managers:** Predicting which technology wave is coming next (Strategic Foresight).
- **For Investors:** Identifying "early adopter" companies in emerging waves.
- **For Policymakers:** Designing industrial policy aligned with the current technological epoch.
- **For Engineers:** Recognizing whether their skill set is becoming obsolete or rising in value.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Derivation

## 3.1 Deriving the Exponential Pace of Technological Change

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most important analytical exercise** KTU expects students to perform in Module 2. We derive the *acceleration* of technology mathematically using **Moore's Law** and **Ray Kurzweil's Law of Accelerating Returns.**

### 📐 Step 1 — Define Moore's Law Function

Moore's Law states that **transistor count $N$** on an integrated circuit at time $t$ follows an exponential relation:

$$
N(t) = N_0 \cdot 2^{t / T_d}
$$

where:
- $N_0$ = initial transistor count (e.g., $2{,}300$ for Intel 4004, 1971)
- $T_d$ = doubling time (≈ **2 years** = 24 months)

### 📐 Step 2 — Solve for Time to Reach Modern Scale

We want to know: how long did it take to go from $2{,}300$ transistors (1971) to $\approx 100$ **billion** transistors (Apple M2 Ultra, 2023)?

$$
\frac{N(t)}{N_0} = \frac{10^{11}}{2{,}300} \approx 4.35 \times 10^{7}
$$

$$
2^{t / T_d} = 4.35 \times 10^{7}
$$

$$
\frac{t}{T_d} = \log_2(4.35 \times 10^{7}) \approx \frac{\ln(4.35 \times 10^{7})}{\ln(2)} \approx \frac{17.585}{0.693} \approx 25.37
$$

$$
t \approx 25.37 \times 2 \approx 50.7 \text{ years}
$$

$$
\text{Calculated: } t \approx 50 \text{ years (1971 → 2021), which matches reality ✅}
$$

### 📐 Step 3 — Apply Kurzweil's Law of Accelerating Returns

Kurzweil extends Moore's Law: the **rate of paradigm shifts themselves** accelerates. If $P(t)$ is paradigm shift rate:

$$
P(t) = P_0 \cdot e^{k t}
$$

Differentiating shows acceleration:

$$
\frac{dP}{dt} = k \cdot P_0 \cdot e^{k t} = k \cdot P(t)
$$

This means the **speed of change itself changes faster** — explaining why the 21st century packs 20th-century-worth of progress into a single decade.

### 📐 Step 4 — Map Each Industrial Revolution to Exponential Logic

| Revolution | Approximate Period | Years Elapsed | Why "Faster"? |
|:----------:|:-----------------:|:-------------:|:--------------|
| **1IR** | 1760–1840 | **80 years** | Slow diffusion; mostly mechanical |
| **2IR** | 1870–1914 | **44 years** | Electricity + global communication |
| **3IR** | 1950–2000 | **50 years** | But 1000× faster compute capacity added |
| **4IR** | 2010–2025 | **15 years** | Global digital infrastructure already in place |

### 📐 Step 5 — Connect to KTU Course Outcomes

- **CO2 (Module 2):** *Analyze technological change and its impact on industry/society.*
- **Bloom's Level:** Apply → Analyze → Evaluate
- **What we proved:** Technological progress is **not linear**, but **logarithmic-to-exponential**, validating why 4IR breakthroughs occur in **months, not decades.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
**Define technology in the context of technology management. List any two defining characteristics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Technology is the systematic application of scientific knowledge, tools, techniques, methods, and processes to solve practical problems and create value for society.
>
> **Two Defining Characteristics:**
> 1. **Multi-dimensional:** Encompasses both tangible (machines) and intangible (know-how, processes) elements.
> 2. **Purpose-driven:** Always aims at solving a problem or fulfilling a human need — never an end in itself.
>
> *(Third mark for clarity of expression & using KTU terminology.)* ✅

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**State any three differences between the First and Third Industrial Revolutions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | 1st IR (1760–1840) | 3rd IR (1950–2000) |
|:----------|:-------------------|:--------------------|
| **Core Technology** | Steam power, mechanical | Electronics, computing |
| **Energy Source** | Coal | Electricity, semiconductors |
| **Communication** | Postal, slow | Internet, near-instant |

*(1 mark per valid difference with example.)* ✅

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks):**
**[KTU University Exam – ESE 2024 Model Question]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the **four industrial revolutions** that have shaped human civilization over the last two centuries. Highlight the defining technologies, key inventors, and societal impact of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With suitable examples, discuss how the **Third Industrial Revolution** transformed the global business landscape in the domains of communication, manufacturing, and finance. **(7 marks)**

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):**
**[KTU University Exam – ESE 2024 Alternative Model Question]**
**(a)** Describe the **Fourth Industrial Revolution (4IR)**. Identify its **core technologies** and explain how each differs from the technologies of previous revolutions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using **Schumpeter's theory of creative destruction**, analyze the disruption caused by digital technologies in **two industries** of your choice. What lessons does this offer to technology managers? **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Model Solutions — Question A

### **A(a) Model Answer — Four Industrial Revolutions (7 marks)**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The last 250 years have witnessed four transformative industrial revolutions, each driven by a new general-purpose technology (GPT) that reshaped economies and societies.

**First Industrial Revolution (1760–1840) — Steam & Mechanization (2 marks):**
- **Defining technology:** Steam engine (James Watt, 1769)
- **Impact:** Mechanized textiles, railways emerged; productivity in textiles rose by ~400%.
- **Society:** Migration from villages to factory towns; birth of the urban working class.

**Second Industrial Revolution (1870–1914) — Electricity & Mass Production (1.5 marks):**
- **Defining technologies:** Electricity (Edison, 1879), internal combustion engine (Benz, 1885), telephone (Bell, 1876).
- **Impact:** Assembly-line production (Ford, 1913) cut car prices, making automobiles affordable to the middle class.
- **Society:** Rise of corporations, standardized time zones, global telegraph networks.

**Third Industrial Revolution (1950–2000) — Electronics & Computing (1.5 marks):**
- **Defining technologies:** Transistor (1947), IC (1958), microprocessor (1971), World Wide Web (1991).
- **Impact:** Birth of the knowledge economy, e-commerce, globalization of services.
- **Society:** Rise of Silicon Valley, IT services industry, mobile communications.

**Fourth Industrial Revolution (2010–Present) — Cyber-Physical Systems (1 mark):**
- **Defining technologies:** AI, IoT, blockchain, CRISPR, quantum computing.
- **Impact:** Autonomous systems, personalized medicine, generative AI reshaping creative work.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Each revolution built upon the previous one, but the *speed* of adoption has accelerated exponentially — a phenomenon Kurzweil called the *Law of Accelerating Returns.* ✅

---

### **A(b) Model Answer — 3IR's Impact on Business (7 marks)**

**Communication (2 marks):**
- ARPANET (1969) → Internet (1991) → Mobile broadband → Email, video conferencing.
- **Example:** A business deal that earlier required physical letters (weeks) now completes via Zoom in minutes. Globalization of remote teams (e.g., Indian IT firms serving US clients 24×7).

**Manufacturing (2.5 marks):**
- Computer Numerical Control (CNC), robotics, and CAD/CAM transformed factories.
- **Example:** Toyota's "Just-In-Time" inventory + computer-integrated manufacturing reduced waste dramatically.
- Adoption of ERP systems (SAP, Oracle) integrated global supply chains.

**Finance (2.5 marks):**
- ATMs (1967), credit cards, electronic trading, online banking (1995).
- **Example:** NASDAQ (1971) introduced fully electronic stock trading — eliminating paper certificates and physical trading floors.
- Rise of fintech (PayPal 1998, mobile wallets, cryptocurrencies).

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The Third Industrial Revolution turned information into the **most valuable resource**, dwarfing land, labor, and capital — setting the stage for 4IR. ✅

---

## 5.4 Model Solutions — Question B

### **B(a) Model Answer — Fourth Industrial Revolution (7 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Coined by **Klaus Schwab (World Economic Forum, 2016)**, the 4IR is characterized by the fusion of physical, digital, and biological realms through cyber-physical systems.

**Core Technologies (4 marks — 1 mark each):**
1. **Artificial Intelligence (AI)** — machine learning, deep learning, generative AI (ChatGPT, 2022).
2. **Internet of Things (IoT)** — billions of connected sensors in homes, factories, cities.
3. **Biotechnology** — CRISPR gene editing (Doudna & Charpentier, 2012, Nobel 2020).
4. **Blockchain & Distributed Ledger Tech** — Bitcoin (2008), Ethereum (2015), smart contracts.

**Differences from Previous Revolutions (2 marks):**
- **Velocity:** 4IR evolves at an exponential, not linear, pace.
- **Breadth & Depth:** It is systemic — simultaneously transforming manufacturing, services, healthcare, and finance — unlike 1IR which was primarily industrial.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** 4IR blurs the lines between man, machine, and biology — demanding new ethical and managerial frameworks. ✅

---

### **B(b) Model Answer — Creative Destruction (7 marks)**

**Schumpeter Recap (1 mark):**
Schumpeter's "creative destruction" describes how new innovations make old industries obsolete while creating new ones — a perpetual cycle.

**Industry 1: Photography / Film (3 marks):**
- **Old:** Kodak dominated 90% of US film market in 1976.
- **Disruption:** Digital camera (1990s) → Smartphone cameras (2007) → Instagram (2010).
- **Result:** Kodak filed bankruptcy (2012); Instagram sold to Facebook for \$1 billion (2012).
- **Lesson:** Refusing to cannibalize your own product (Kodak invented the digital camera in 1975 but shelved it) is a strategic death sentence.

**Industry 2: Taxi / Transportation (3 marks):**
- **Old:** Yellow Cabs with medallion-based licensing.
- **Disruption:** Uber (2009) and Lyft used GPS + smartphones + gig economy.
- **Result:** Traditional taxi industries globally lost 30–50% revenue; regulatory frameworks collapsed.
- **Lesson:** Platform-based business models leverage network effects — winner-take-most dynamics.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Managers must build organizations that are **adaptable, learning-oriented, and willing to disrupt themselves** before competitors do — the essence of strategic technology management. ✅

---

## 5.5 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **❌ Confusing "Industrial Revolution" with "Industrialization"** — they are NOT synonyms.
> 2. **❌ Confusing the "Second" with the "Third"** — Electricity = 2IR; Computers/Internet = 3IR.
> 3. **❌ Listing dates without explaining *impact*** — KTU rewards "why" more than "when."
> 4. **❌ Writing generic answers about AI without naming a *specific* technology** (e.g., ChatGPT, CRISPR).
> 5. **❌ Ignoring the KTU CO mapping** — explicitly state how your answer satisfies CO2 of Module 2.
> 6. **❌ Skipping tables/diagrams** — even a hand-drawn S-curve or timeline earns 1–2 bonus marks.
> 7. **❌ Writing without chronological structure** — always organize answers **chronologically** (1IR → 4IR).

---

## 5.6 📌 Topic Recap — High-Density Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize these before the exam:**

- ✅ Technology = **science + application + value creation**
- ✅ **4 Industrial Revolutions** in 2 centuries: 1IR (Steam) → 2IR (Electricity) → 3IR (Digital) → 4IR (AI/Cyber-Physical)
- ✅ **Moore's Law:** Transistors double every **~2 years**; cost halves
- ✅ **Schumpeter's Creative Destruction:** New tech destroys old, creates new
- ✅ **Rogers' Adopter Categories:** Innovators (2.5%) → Laggards (16%)
- ✅ **S-Curve:** Slow start → rapid growth → saturation
- ✅ **Key 4IR Tech:** AI, IoT, Blockchain, CRISPR, Quantum
- ✅ **Pace is EXPONENTIAL, not linear** — 4IR ≈ 15 years ≈ entire 3IR compressed
- ✅ **For 14-mark Qs:** Always include **timeline + examples + business impact + manager's lesson**
- ✅ **Link to Course Outcome CO2:** *Analyze the impact of technological change on industry and society*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Direct and Indirect Effect of Technology on Human Need Factors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET417 - TECHNOLOGY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Direct and Indirect Effect of Technology on Human Need Factors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2: Direct and Indirect Effect of Technology on Human Need Factors

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Human Need Factors** refer to the fundamental physiological, psychological, and social requirements that drive human behavior, motivation, and well-being. The most widely accepted academic framework for classifying these factors is **Abraham Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs** (1943), which arranges human needs in a five-tier pyramid model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> **Technology** is the systematic application of scientific knowledge, tools, techniques, and processes to solve practical problems, extend human capabilities, and improve the quality of life. The **Direct Effect** of technology on human need factors refers to immediate, tangible, and intentional outcomes that address a specific need (e.g., a vaccine directly addresses health). The **Indirect Effect** refers to secondary, unintended, or cascading consequences that emerge through socio-economic, environmental, or cultural pathways (e.g., industrial technology indirectly affects social structures via urbanization).

### The Maslow Hierarchy of Needs — Foundation Framework

| Tier | Need Category | Description | Example Technology Link |
|:----:|:--------------|:------------|:------------------------|
| 5 (Top) | **Self-Actualization** | Personal growth, creativity, moral fulfillment | E-learning platforms, creative software |
| 4 | **Esteem Needs** | Respect, status, recognition, confidence | Social media, professional networking |
| 3 | **Social / Belongingness** | Family, friendship, intimacy, acceptance | Communication tech, social networks |
| 2 | **Safety Needs** | Security, stability, protection, order | Surveillance systems, cybersecurity, insurance tech |
| 1 (Base) | **Physiological Needs** | Food, water, sleep, shelter, clothing | Agricultural tech, medical devices, housing tech |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Smartphone Story"**
> Imagine a person in a rural village in 1990 versus 2024. In 1990, the person relied on walking to fetch water (physiological need met manually), lived in a thatched house (shelter), had no way to call family in the city, and worked a manual agricultural job. Fast-forward to 2024: **directly**, the smartphone provides water-purification app guides, weather alerts (safety), video calls with family (social), and online learning (self-actualization). **Indirectly**, the same smartphone disrupted local markets (e-commerce replacing village shops), changed employment patterns (digital jobs replacing manual labor), altered cultural communication (texting replacing face-to-face greetings), and reshaped sleep patterns (blue-light exposure). One device, two layers of impact.

### Why This Topic Matters in Technology Management

From a **Technology Management** perspective, every technological intervention must be evaluated on two dimensions:

1. **Direct Effect** — Does it solve the intended problem efficiently?
2. **Indirect Effect** — What ripple effects does it create across society, economy, environment, and culture?

A KTU technology manager must anticipate both layers because a product with strong direct benefits but severe indirect harm (e.g., asbestos roofing — directly solves shelter, indirectly causes cancer) is a **management failure**.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Direct Effects of Technology on Human Need Factors

Direct effects are **first-order, intentional, and immediate** outcomes. They target a specific need and produce measurable benefit without intermediate socio-economic transformation.

#### Breakdown by Need Tier

**A. Direct Effect on Physiological Needs**
- **Food:** Genetically modified crops, drip irrigation, mechanized farming → increased yield, nutrition, calorie availability.
- **Water:** Filtration systems, desalination plants, bore-well technology → clean drinking water access.
- **Shelter:** Reinforced cement concrete (RCC), prefabricated housing, climate-controlled buildings.
- **Health:** Vaccines, MRI machines, insulin pumps, robotic surgery → increased life expectancy.

**B. Direct Effect on Safety Needs**
- **Personal Safety:** CCTV, biometric locks, GPS tracking, home security alarms.
- **Financial Safety:** Insurance technology, digital banking, KYC verification systems.
- **National Safety:** Defense technology, surveillance drones, cybersecurity firewalls.

**C. Direct Effect on Social / Belongingness Needs**
- **Connectivity:** Mobile phones, video conferencing (Zoom, WhatsApp), social media platforms.
- **Community Building:** Online forums, dating apps, alumni networks.
- **Family Bonding:** Shared streaming services, family locator apps.

**D. Direct Effect on Esteem Needs**
- **Recognition:** LinkedIn profiles, YouTube channels, digital portfolios.
- **Status Symbols:** Luxury tech products (Apple ecosystem, premium EVs).
- **Skill Validation:** Online certifications (Coursera, NPTEL), digital badges.

**E. Direct Effect on Self-Actualization**
- **Knowledge:** Wikipedia, MOOCs, AI tutors, research databases.
- **Creativity:** Adobe Creative Suite, music production software, 3D printers.
- **Purpose:** Volunteer-matching platforms, open-source contribution.

### 2.2 Indirect Effects of Technology on Human Need Factors

Indirect effects are **second-order, sometimes unintended, and emerge through systemic changes** in economy, environment, culture, and behavior.

#### Major Categories of Indirect Effects

**1. Economic Restructuring**
- Automation → job displacement in some sectors, creation in others.
- E-commerce → decline of brick-and-mortar retail, rise of logistics.
- Cryptocurrency → reshaping financial inclusion and monetary systems.

**2. Environmental Transformation**
- Fossil-fuel technology (cars, factories) → global warming, indirectly threatens physiological safety.
- Renewable energy tech (solar, wind) → indirect improvement in long-term survival.
- E-waste → indirect health hazard from improper disposal.

**3. Cultural & Social Shifts**
- Smartphones → reduced face-to-face interaction, altered dating norms.
- Streaming platforms → decline of communal TV viewing, rise of personalized content.
- AI translation tools → erosion of language barriers but also of linguistic diversity.

**4. Psychological & Behavioral Changes**
- Social media → comparison culture, anxiety, depression (indirect harm to esteem).
- Gaming technology → addiction patterns, altered reward systems.
- Information overload → reduced attention span, decision fatigue.

**5. Demographic & Urbanization Patterns**
- Industrial technology → rural-to-urban migration.
- Remote work tech → reverse migration (digital nomads), suburban revival.
- Healthcare tech → aging populations, lower birth rates in developed nations.

**6. Political & Governance Impact**
- Internet → rise of digital democracy, e-governance, but also misinformation.
- Surveillance tech → erosion of privacy (indirect threat to esteem and safety).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following analytical formulas and frameworks are used in Technology Management to quantify or conceptualize the impact of technology on human needs.

| Formula / Framework | Expression | Description | Application |
|:--------------------|:-----------|:------------|:------------|
| Maslow Need Satisfaction Index (Conceptual) | $NSI = \sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i \cdot N_i$ | Weighted sum of need fulfillment across 5 tiers; $w_i$ is importance weight, $N_i$ is satisfaction level | Measures how well a technology addresses human needs |
| Technology Impact Ratio (Conceptual) | $TIR = \dfrac{D_e}{D_e + I_e}$ | Ratio of direct effect ($D_e$) to total impact (direct + indirect, $I_e$) | Indicates whether a technology has primarily direct or indirect impact |
| Adoption Diffusion (Bass Model) | $\dfrac{dN(t)}{dt} = \left( p + q \cdot \dfrac{N(t)}{m} \right) \cdot (m - N(t))$ | Rate of new adopters; $p$ = innovation coeff., $q$ = imitation coeff., $m$ = market potential | Predicts how a technology spreads and indirectly reshapes society |
| Cost-Benefit of Technology | $CBT = \dfrac{\sum B_{direct} + \sum B_{indirect}}{\sum C_{direct} + \sum C_{indirect}}$ | Total benefits (direct + indirect) divided by total costs | KTU-style feasibility analysis of a technological intervention |
| Net Technology Effect | $NTE = \alpha \cdot D_e - \beta \cdot I_{neg} + \gamma \cdot I_{pos}$ | Weighted combination of direct effect, negative indirect effects, and positive indirect effects | Holistic scoring for technology managers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on KTU Examinations:** These formulas are typically used in **conceptual/qualitative** form in the PEMET417 exam. Numerical calculations are rare. Focus on **explaining the relationship**, not computing values.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In engineering project management, every technological decision must pass a **dual-impact audit**:
- **Direct Audit:** Does this design fulfill its functional specification?
- **Indirect Audit:** Will this design cause environmental degradation, social disruption, or economic imbalance?

**Example:** A civil engineer designing a flyover must consider:
- *Direct:* Reduces traffic congestion (saves time = safety + physiological).
- *Indirect:* Displaces nearby businesses, increases pollution in adjacent areas, alters pedestrian flow.

Failing the indirect audit is a common reason for project delays and public opposition in Indian smart-city projects.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Technology Impact Ratio (TIR)

The **Technology Impact Ratio (TIR)** is a conceptual model used in Technology Management to classify whether a technology exerts more direct or indirect influence on human need satisfaction.

### Starting Premise

Let $T$ be a technology intervention, and let the total impact on human need factors be decomposed into two orthogonal components:

$$T_{total} = D_e + I_e$$

Where:
- $D_e$ = Direct effect (immediate, first-order impact on a specific human need)
- $I_e$ = Indirect effect (secondary, cascading, second-order impact across systems)

### Step 1: Define the Direct Effect Component

The direct effect $D_e$ is calculated as a weighted sum of need satisfaction across Maslow's five tiers that the technology intentionally targets:

$$D_e = \sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i \cdot N_i^{direct}$$

Where:
- $w_i$ = weight (importance) assigned to need tier $i$ (typically physiological > safety > social > esteem > self-actualization, but context-dependent)
- $N_i^{direct}$ = degree (0 to 1) to which technology $T$ directly satisfies need tier $i$

### Step 2: Define the Indirect Effect Component

The indirect effect $I_e$ captures cascading changes across economic, environmental, cultural, and behavioral systems:

$$I_e = \sum_{j=1}^{k} v_j \cdot S_j^{indirect}$$

Where:
- $v_j$ = weight of secondary system $j$ (e.g., economy, environment, culture)
- $S_j^{indirect}$ = magnitude of indirect change in system $j$ caused by $T$
- $k$ = total number of affected secondary systems

### Step 3: Formulate the Technology Impact Ratio

To compare direct vs. indirect influence, we normalize the direct effect by total impact:

$$TIR = \dfrac{D_e}{D_e + I_e}$$

### Step 4: Interpretation Rules

- If $TIR \rightarrow 1$: Technology is **direct-dominant** (e.g., a water purifier — primarily serves physiological need).
- If $TIR \rightarrow 0$: Technology is **indirect-dominant** (e.g., social media — primary effects are cascading socio-cultural).
- If $TIR \approx 0.5$: Technology has **balanced dual impact** (e.g., electric vehicles — directly serve transport, indirectly reshape energy markets).

### Step 5: Worked Example — Electric Vehicles (EVs)

Consider an electric vehicle technology deployed in Kerala:

$$\begin{aligned}
D_e &= 0.7 \cdot 1.0 + 0.5 \cdot 0.8 + 0.0 \cdot 0.2 + 0.3 \cdot 0.5 + 0.0 \cdot 0.1 \\
&= 0.70 + 0.40 + 0.00 + 0.15 + 0.00 \\
&= 1.25
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
I_e &= 0.6 \cdot 0.9 + 0.8 \cdot 0.7 + 0.4 \cdot 0.5 + 0.3 \cdot 0.4 \\
&= 0.54 + 0.56 + 0.20 + 0.12 \\
&= 1.42
\end{aligned}$$

$$TIR = \dfrac{1.25}{1.25 + 1.42} = \dfrac{1.25}{2.67} \approx 0.468$$

**Interpretation:** $TIR \approx 0.47$ indicates a **balanced dual impact** EV — directly serves transport (physiological/safety) while indirectly reshaping energy grids, oil economies, and urban air quality.

> [!NOTE]
> The numbers above are illustrative. In KTU examinations, the student is expected to **explain the framework qualitatively** rather than perform exact numerical substitution unless specifically asked.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Direct vs. Indirect Effect Comparison

| Parameter | **Direct Effect** | **Indirect Effect** |
|:----------|:------------------|:--------------------|
| **Time Horizon** | Immediate (short-term) | Long-term, delayed |
| **Intentionality** | Intended by designer | Often unintended |
| **Visibility** | Highly visible to user | Often invisible or misattributed |
| **Scope** | Narrow, need-specific | Broad, systemic |
| **Measurability** | Easier to quantify (KPIs, ROI) | Harder to quantify (externalities) |
| **Stakeholders Affected** | Primary user | Society, environment, future generations |
| **Predictability** | High | Low to moderate |
| **Example** | A blood-test machine detects diabetes (health) | Same machine's data trains AI, reshaping healthcare insurance pricing (economic) |
| **Management Lever** | Engineering design | Policy, regulation, ethics |
| **Failure Mode** | Functional failure (product breaks) | Systemic failure (society rejects product) |

## 3.3 Detailed Scenario Analysis

### Scenario A: Mobile Phone Technology

**Direct Effects on Human Needs:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Physiological} &: \text{Mobile health apps, emergency ambulance alerts} \\
\text{Safety} &: \text{Real-time location sharing, anti-theft tracking} \\
\text{Social} &: \text{WhatsApp, video calls — direct family contact} \\
\text{Esteem} &: \text{Status display via latest model, social media followers} \\
\text{Self-Actualization} &: \text{YouTube tutorials, online courses}
\end{aligned}$$

**Indirect Effects on Human Needs:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Economy} &: \text{Rise of gig economy, decline of landline jobs, UPI revolution} \\
\text{Environment} &: \text{E-waste mountains, rare-earth mining in Africa} \\
\text{Culture} &: \text{Shorter attention spans, decline of letter writing, new dating norms} \\
\text{Health} &: \text{Sleep disorders, text-neck syndrome, eye strain (negative)} \\
\text{Governance} &: \text{Digital democracy via Twitter, Aadhaar linkage to welfare}
\end{aligned}$$

### Scenario B: Agricultural Biotechnology (Bt Cotton in India)

**Direct Effects:**
- Reduced pesticide use → better farmer health (physiological).
- Higher yield → increased income (physiological + safety).

**Indirect Effects:**
- Higher seed costs → small farmer indebtedness (negative safety).
- Pollinator decline → long-term ecosystem impact (negative physiological).
- India became net cotton exporter → macro-economic shift (positive safety/social).

### Scenario C: Artificial Intelligence in Healthcare

**Direct Effects:**
- Faster diagnosis (physiological, safety).
- Robotic surgery precision (physiological).

**Indirect Effects:**
- Doctor deskilling over time (negative esteem for medical profession).
- Algorithmic bias in diagnosis (negative safety for marginalized groups).
- Healthcare cost reduction long-term (positive safety).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Pattern:** In Module 2 questions, students are often asked to *"Discuss the direct and indirect effects of a given technology on human needs."* Always structure the answer as: **(i) Define direct/indirect, (ii) List direct effects tier-wise, (iii) List indirect effects across systems, (iv) Provide a balanced conclusion.**

---
