<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the "Scientific Policy Resolution, 1958" and state its three primary objectives.
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 | Module 4 | CO4 | Bloom: Remember/Understand]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Scientific Policy Resolution (1958) was the **first formal S&T policy of independent India**, adopted by Parliament under Jawaharlal Nehru's leadership. It laid the foundation for state-led scientific development.

**Three Primary Objectives:**
1. To foster, promote, and sustain, by all appropriate means, the cultivation of science and scientific research in all its aspects — pure, applied, and educational.
2. To ensure an adequate supply, within the country, of research scientists of the highest quality, and to recognise their work as an important element in the national life.
3. To secure for the people of India, the benefits of the acquisition and application of scientific knowledge, and in particular, to use it for the well-being of the people.

*(Examiner's note: For full 3 marks, the policy year and at least two objectives must be quoted verbatim.)*

---

### Question 2: What is GERD and what is India's current GERD as a percentage of GDP?
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 | Module 4 | CO4 | Bloom: Remember]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**GERD** stands for **Gross Expenditure on Research and Development**. It is the total monetary expenditure (current + capital) on R&D performed on the national territory in a given year, expressed as a **percentage of GDP (Gross Domestic Product)**.

- **India's current GERD:** approximately **0.65% to 0.70% of GDP** (2022–23 estimates).
- **National target:** 2% of GDP (set originally in S&T Policy 2003, reiterated in STI Policy 2013 and STIP 2020).
- **Global benchmark:** Israel ~5.4%, South Korea ~4.8%, USA ~3.4%, China ~2.4%.

*(Examiner's note: Writing just "GERD = 0.65%" gets 2 marks; adding the global comparison and the 2% target gives full 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A: "The Science, Technology and Innovation Policy (STIP) 2020 represents a paradigm shift from the 1958 Scientific Policy Resolution." Critically analyze this statement in the context of India's S&T evolution.
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 | Module 4 | CO4 | Bloom: Analyze/Evaluate | 14 Marks]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Trace the evolution of S&T policies in India from 1958 to 2020.

**Model Solution:**

1. **1958 — Scientific Policy Resolution:** First S&T policy; emphasized self-reliance, atomic energy, agriculture, and industrial R&D. Led to the creation of IITs, ISRO, BARC, and CSIR labs.
2. **1983 — Technology Policy Statement:** Shifted focus to technology import, absorption, and indigenous development; emphasized technology forecasting (TIFAC, DSIR).
3. **2003 — Science & Technology Policy:** Introduced PPP (Public-Private Partnership) models; set the **2% GERD target**; promoted innovation clusters and technology business incubators.
4. **2013 — Science, Technology & Innovation Policy:** Brought in the **"STI"** (Science, Technology **and Innovation**) terminology; focus on inclusion, equity, affordability, and bottom-up innovation; led to Atal Innovation Mission (AIM), TIHs, NIDHI.
5. **2020 — STIP (Draft):** First policy drafted via **public consultation**; focus on **open science, AI, quantum, biotech, semiconductors, deep ocean, and space**; aligned with **Viksit Bharat @2047** vision.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Critically evaluate how STIP 2020 represents a paradigm shift from 1958.

**Model Solution:**

| **Dimension** | **1958 Policy** | **STIP 2020** | **Paradigm Shift?** |
|---------------|------------------|---------------|---------------------|
| **Approach** | Top-down, state-led | Bottom-up, public-consulted | **Yes — major shift** |
| **Sectors** | Atomic energy, agriculture | AI, quantum, biotech, semiconductor, green H₂ | **Yes — sectoral expansion** |
| **Funding** | Government-driven | Mixed (Govt + Private + Foreign) | **Yes — diversification** |
| **Innovation** | R&D focus | Innovation + Translation + Commercialization (TRL-based) | **Yes — outcome-based** |
| **Equity** | National-level | Gender, regional, social inclusion (STI for All) | **Yes — inclusive** |
| **Global Role** | Self-reliance (import substitution) | Global leadership (Atmanirbhar Bharat + Vasudhaiva Kutumbakam) | **Yes — outward-looking** |

**Conclusion:** The shift from a *defensive, state-led, self-reliance* model (1958) to an *offensive, multi-stakeholder, globally-engaged* model (STIP 2020) is indeed a **paradigm shift**, though the fundamental goal of *national development through science* remains unchanged since Nehru's vision.

---

### Question B: Discuss the role of major S&T institutions in India. Critically examine why India's R&D ecosystem, despite having world-class institutions, still ranks low on the Global Innovation Index.
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 | Module 4 | CO4 | Bloom: Apply/Analyze | 14 Marks]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Role of major S&T institutions in India.

**Model Solution:**

The major institutions can be classified into four functional clusters:

**1. Policy & Funding Bodies:**
- **DST (Department of Science & Technology, 1971):** Apex funding body; runs INSPIRE, SERB, FIST.
- **DBT (Department of Biotechnology, 1986):** Funds biotech, Genome India, BIRAC.
- **DSIR (Department of Scientific & Industrial Research):** Promotes industry R&D via TDB, PSIR.

**2. Mission-Oriented R&D Agencies:**
- **DRDO (1958):** India's 50-lab defence R&D network; produced Agni, BrahMos, Tejas LCA.
- **ISRO (1969):** World's most cost-effective space agency; Chandrayaan-3, Mangalyaan, NavIC, Gaganyaan.

**3. Knowledge & Frontier Science Labs:**
- **CSIR (1942):** 38 labs covering chemicals, drugs, materials, leather, petroleum; played a key role in COVID-19 diagnostics.
- **ICMR (1911):** Medical research; TB, polio, COVID protocols.
- **ICAR (1929):** Agricultural research; Green Revolution.

**4. Academic & Human Capital Institutions:**
- **IITs (23), IISc, IISERs, NITs, Central Universities** — produce over 16,000 PhDs annually.

**Summary Table of Landmark Contributions:**

| **Institution** | **Landmark Contribution** |
|-----------------|---------------------------|
| DRDO | Agni missile series, BrahMos cruise missile |
| ISRO | Chandrayaan-3 (Moon landing, 2023) |
| CSIR | Process technologies for generic drugs |
| BARC | Indigenous nuclear reactors, Dhruva |
| ICMR | COVID-19 national testing protocol |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Why does India rank low on the Global Innovation Index despite strong institutions?

**Model Solution:**

Despite being the **3rd largest research publication producer** and having a strong institutional base, India ranks only **40th in GII 2023**. The reasons are:

1. **Low GERD (0.65% of GDP):** Below the 2% target; restricts lab infrastructure, scholarships, and equipment modernization.
2. **Low Industry R&D Spending:** Only ~37% of GERD comes from industry, compared to 60–75% in developed nations.
3. **Weak Academia–Industry Linkage:** Most IITs publish papers; few produce commercializable patents.
4. **Brain Drain:** ~1.5 lakh Indian researchers work abroad, with limited reverse flow.
5. **Patent-to-Publication Imbalance:** India files ~66,000 patents/year vs. China's 1.5 million.
6. **Fragmented Funding:** Multiple ministries fund overlapping R&D without unified coordination.
7. **Regulatory Burden:** Slow approval processes, especially in biotech, GM crops, and clinical trials.
8. **IP Commercialization Deficit:** Indian universities' tech transfer offices are nascent compared to MIT, Stanford.

**Conclusion:** The paradox — "world-class institutions, subpar innovation ranking" — is resolved by understanding that **institutions ≠ innovation system**. India must transition from an **input-heavy** R&D model to an **output-driven, market-linked, translational** innovation model as envisioned in STIP 2020.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Writing "S&T" without expanding it once** — costs 0.5 marks in introductions.
> 2. **Quoting GERD without the target (2% by 2030)** — incomplete.
> 3. **Listing institutions without their year of establishment and one achievement each** — half-marks awarded.
> 4. **Forgetting to mention either 1958 OR 2020 policy in any S&T answer** — examiners explicitly check this.
> 5. **Concluding without using the phrase "Viksit Bharat 2047" or "Atmanirbhar Bharat"** — the current KTU valuation key actively rewards these as keywords.
> 6. **Writing "ISRO was founded by Vikram Sarabhai in 1969"** — correct, but NOT writing his role as "father of Indian space program" loses 0.5 marks.
> 7. **Confusing DST (S&T Ministry) with DBT (S&T Ministry) and DSIR (S&T Ministry)** — all three are under the same Ministry of Science & Technology, but have distinct mandates; mixing them up costs 1 mark.
> 8. **In SWOT answers, not separating Industry contribution (37%) from Government contribution (50%)** — examiners check this disaggregation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Five Major S&T Policies to Memorize:** 1958 (Nehru, Scientific Policy Resolution), 1983 (Technology Policy Statement, Indira Gandhi era), 2003 (S&T Policy, Vajpayee era), 2013 (STI Policy, Manmohan era), 2020 (STIP Draft, Modi era — public consultation).
- **One Numerical to Memorize:** GERD = **0.65% of GDP**, target = **2%**.
- **Two Global Rankings to Memorize:** GII 2023 = **40th**; Research Publications = **3rd globally**.
- **Three Iconic Achievements to Quote:** Chandrayaan-3 (2023, Moon), Mangalyaan (2013, Mars on first attempt), UPI (digital payments revolution).
- **Four Apex Institutions:** DST, DBT, DRDO, ISRO — all under different ministries but linked to the S&T ecosystem.
- **Five Founding Fathers of Indian S&T:** Jawaharlal Nehru, Homi Bhabha (nuclear), Vikram Sarabhai (space), M.S. Swaminathan (agriculture), A.P.J. Abdul Kalam (missile).
- **Six Flagship Missions (2014–Present):** Make in India, Digital India, Startup India, Atal Innovation Mission, National Quantum Mission, IndiaAI Mission.
- **Seven Socio-Technical Challenges:** Low GERD, low industry R&D, brain drain, IP commercialization deficit, gender gap, fragmented funding, regulatory delays.
- **"Atmanirbhar Bharat" (Self-Reliant India)** and **"Viksit Bharat @2047"** — these are the **mandatory conclusion keywords** for any S&T policy answer in KTU 2024.
- **The Triple Helix Model:** The Indian S&T system is gradually evolving from a **Double Helix (Government + Academia)** to a **Triple Helix (Government + Academia + Industry)** — mention this in any policy analysis answer for 2 extra marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Policies, Linkages at Enterprise Levels, Technology Support Systems: Financing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET417 - TECHNOLOGY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Policies, Linkages at Enterprise Levels, Technology Support Systems: Financing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Technology Policies at Enterprise Levels

### Formal Definition
**Technology Policy** at the enterprise level refers to a formally documented strategic framework that guides an organization in the acquisition, development, application, protection, and diffusion of technology to achieve its business objectives and competitive positioning. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, it encompasses the rules, procedures, and decision-making guidelines that govern technology-related choices within the firm.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a **Technology Policy** like the **rulebook of a cricket team**:
- The rulebook doesn't tell the players *how* to hit every ball, but it defines *boundaries* (e.g., what kind of bowlers to hire, what training methods to use, what equipment to invest in).
- Without it, the captain (CEO) makes ad-hoc decisions, and the team becomes inconsistent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** A technology policy is **NOT** a strategy — it is the *operational backbone* that ensures the technology strategy gets implemented consistently across all departments.

### Hierarchical Layers of Technology Policy

| Layer | Scope | Time Horizon | Key Concern |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Corporate** | Entire organization | 5–10 years | Strategic alignment of technology with vision |
| **Business Unit** | Division/Department | 3–5 years | Market positioning through technology |
| **Functional** | R&D, IT, Production | 1–2 years | Operational efficiency and standards |
| **Operational** | Project/Task level | 3–12 months | Day-to-day technology decisions |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Key Term:** **Technology Audit** — the periodic review that checks compliance with the established technology policy.

---

## 1.2 Linkages at Enterprise Levels

### Formal Definition
**Linkages at enterprise levels** refer to the structured, bidirectional relationships and information flows that connect the technology function of a firm with its internal business units and external stakeholders (suppliers, universities, government, customers).

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a **hospital**:
- The **Cardiology Department (Business Unit)** must constantly coordinate with the **Pharmacy (Supplier)**, the **Diagnostic Lab (University/R&D)**, the **Insurance Desk (Government)**, and the **IT Department (Internal)**.
- Without these linkages, even a simple test gets delayed.

### Two Major Categories of Linkages

1. **Internal Linkages** — Connect R&D/Technology department with Marketing, Production, Finance, and HR within the firm.
2. **External Linkages** — Connect the firm with Suppliers, Customers, Universities, Research Institutes, Competitors, and Government bodies.

---

## 1.3 Technology Support Systems

### Formal Definition
**Technology Support Systems** are the institutional, financial, informational, and human resource mechanisms that enable a firm to successfully acquire, absorb, adapt, and apply technology. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly identifies five support systems:
1. **Financing** (covered today)
2. Human Resource Development
3. Information Systems
4. R&D Infrastructure
5. Legal & Regulatory Framework

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a **car**:
- **Financing** = fuel/money to run it
- **HRD** = trained driver
- **Information** = GPS/dashboard
- **R&D Infrastructure** = engine and garage
- **Legal** = traffic rules and RC book

---

## 1.4 Technology Financing — Core Concept

### Formal Definition
**Technology Financing** refers to the mobilization, allocation, and management of financial resources required for the entire technology lifecycle — from basic research, prototype development, commercialization, to technology upgradation and replacement.

### The Five Stages of Technology Financing

$$\text{Stage}_{1} \rightarrow \text{Basic Research} \rightarrow \text{Applied R\&D} \rightarrow \text{Prototype} \rightarrow \text{Commercialization} \rightarrow \text{Upgradation}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Each stage has a **different risk profile** and therefore requires a **different financing source**. Public funding dominates Stage 1; private venture capital dominates Stages 3–4.

### Sources of Technology Financing — Master Overview

| Source | Type | Risk Appetite | Stage Funded | Cost of Capital |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Government Grants | Public | Very Low | Basic R&D | Zero (free) |
| Internal Accruals | Internal | Medium | All stages | Low |
| Bank Loans | Debt | Low | Commercialization | Moderate |
| Venture Capital | Equity | High | Startup/Growth | Very High dilution |
| Angel Investors | Equity | Very High | Seed stage | Very High |
| Crowdfunding | Mixed | Variable | Early | Low to Moderate |
| FDI / Joint Ventures | Strategic | Medium | Commercialization | Variable |
| Technology Bonds | Debt | Low | Specific projects | Low |
| Public-Private Partnerships | Hybrid | Shared | Infrastructure | Shared |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Mandate:** Students must be able to differentiate between **debt financing** (loans, bonds) and **equity financing** (VC, IPO) in the context of technology investments.

---

## 1.5 Why Technology Financing is Critical

- The **valley of death** between lab and market requires patient capital.
- Technology has **high obsolescence risk** — funding must be continuous.
- Emerging technologies (AI, biotech, clean energy) require **enormous upfront capex**.
- In India, over **70% of MSMEs** are self-financed, limiting their technology adoption.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Components of an Effective Technology Policy

A well-designed technology policy must address the following **eight critical components**:

1. **Technology Vision Statement** — Long-term direction aligned with corporate vision.
2. **Technology Acquisition Policy** — Buy vs. Make vs. License decisions.
3. **Technology Protection Policy** — IP rights, patents, trade secrets.
4. **Technology Diffusion Policy** — How technology flows within the firm.
5. **R&D Investment Policy** — % of revenue allocated to R&D (typically **3–6%** for tech firms).
6. **Technology Upgradation Policy** — Replacement and modernization cycles.
7. **Technology Audit Policy** — Periodic review mechanisms.
8. **Technology Partnership Policy** — External collaboration guidelines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** When asked about technology policy, students often forget the **protection** and **audit** components. Always include these for full marks.

---

## 2.2 Linkages — Detailed Theoretical Framework

### 2.2.1 Internal Linkages

**R&D ↔ Marketing:**
- Marketing feeds customer needs to R&D.
- R&D provides innovative features to Marketing.

**R&D ↔ Production:**
- Production provides manufacturability feedback.
- R&D provides process improvements.

**R&D ↔ Finance:**
- Finance allocates budgets to R&D projects.
- R&D provides ROI projections.

### 2.2.2 External Linkages

- **Vertical Linkages** — With suppliers and customers (upstream/downstream).
- **Horizontal Linkages** — With competitors, universities, research labs.
- **Institutional Linkages** — With government, trade bodies, banks.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Term:** **Open Innovation** — when firms source technology from external linkages (universities, startups) rather than developing entirely in-house.

---

## 2.3 Technology Financing — Theoretical Framework

### 2.3.1 Classification of Financing Sources

**A. Based on Source Origin:**
- **Internal Financing** — Retained earnings, depreciation reserves, working capital.
- **External Financing** — Debt (loans, bonds) + Equity (shares, VC, PE).

**B. Based on Risk:**
- **Low-risk capital** — Government grants, internal accruals.
- **High-risk capital** — Venture capital, angel funds, crowdfunding.

**C. Based on Stage of Technology Lifecycle:**
- **Seed Stage** — Angel investors, government seed grants.
- **Early Stage** — Venture capital, accelerators.
- **Growth Stage** — Private equity, IPO.
- **Mature Stage** — Bank loans, retained earnings.

---

## 2.4 Cost of Capital — Key Equations

### Formula 1: Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)

The most critical formula in technology financing decisions.

$$\text{WACC} = \left( \frac{E}{V} \right) \cdot R_{e} + \left( \frac{D}{V} \right) \cdot R_{d} \cdot (1 - T)$$

Where:
- $E$ = Market value of equity
- $D$ = Market value of debt
- $V = E + D$ = Total market value of financing
- $R_{e}$ = Cost of equity
- $R_{d}$ = Cost of debt
- $T$ = Corporate tax rate

### Formula 2: Cost of Equity (CAPM Model)

$$R_{e} = R_{f} + \beta \cdot (R_{m} - R_{f})$$

Where:
- $R_{f}$ = Risk-free rate (typically 10-year government bond yield)
- $\beta$ = Beta (systematic risk of the firm)
- $R_{m}$ = Expected market return
- $(R_{m} - R_{f})$ = Market risk premium

### Formula 3: Net Present Value (NPV) of a Technology Investment

$$\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{\text{CF}_{t}}{(1 + r)^{t}} - C_{0}$$

Where:
- $\text{CF}_{t}$ = Cash flow at time $t$
- $r$ = Discount rate (often WACC)
- $C_{0}$ = Initial investment
- $n$ = Project life in years

**Decision Rule:**
- $\text{NPV} > 0$ → Accept the technology project
- $\text{NPV} < 0$ → Reject the project
- $\text{NPV} = 0$ → Indifferent

### Formula 4: Payback Period

$$\text{Payback Period} = \frac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow}}$$

For uneven cash flows:

$$\text{Payback} = \text{Years before full recovery} + \frac{\text{Unrecovered amount at start of year}}{\text{Cash flow during the year}}$$

### Formula 5: R&D Intensity Ratio

$$\text{R\&D Intensity} = \frac{\text{R\&D Expenditure}}{\text{Total Revenue}} \times 100\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Benchmark:** Global tech giants like Google, Meta, and Amazon invest **15–20%** of revenue in R&D. Indian IT majors (TCS, Infosys) invest **1.5–2.5%**.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Application in Technology Financing |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{WACC} = \frac{E}{V} R_{e} + \frac{D}{V} R_{d} (1-T)$ | Choosing the right mix of debt and equity |
| 2 | $R_{e} = R_{f} + \beta (R_{m} - R_{f})$ | Cost of equity for risky tech startups |
| 3 | $\text{NPV} = \sum \frac{\text{CF}_{t}}{(1+r)^{t}} - C_{0}$ | Evaluating a new technology project |
| 4 | $\text{IRR}$ solves $\text{NPV} = 0$ | Comparing mutually exclusive tech projects |
| 5 | $\text{R\&D Intensity} = \frac{\text{R\&D Exp}}{\text{Revenue}}$ | Measuring innovation commitment |
| 6 | $\text{Payback} = \frac{\text{Investment}}{\text{Annual Cash Flow}}$ | Quick risk assessment |
| 7 | $\text{ROCE} = \frac{\text{EBIT}}{\text{Capital Employed}} \times 100$ | Efficiency of tech investments |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering Management

- **Software Industry:** NPV used to evaluate SaaS migration vs. legacy maintenance.
- **Manufacturing:** R&D intensity benchmarked against competitors.
- **Clean Energy:** Project finance structures used for solar/wind farms.
- **Biotech:** VC funding critical for clinical trials (valley of death).
- **Aerospace:** Long-gestation government contracts with milestone-based payments.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivations of Key Financing Formulas

### Derivation 1: Cost of Equity using CAPM (Detailed)

The Capital Asset Pricing Model is derived from the **Mean-Variance Portfolio Theory** of Harry Markowitz and William Sharpe. Let us derive it step-by-step.

**Step 1: Define the expected return on a single asset.**

$$E(R_{i}) = R_{f} + \frac{\text{Cov}(R_{i}, R_{m})}{\text{Var}(R_{m})} \cdot [E(R_{m}) - R_{f}]$$

This says: Expected return = Risk-free return + Risk premium scaled by covariance with market.

**Step 2: Substitute the definition of Beta.**

$$\beta_{i} = \frac{\text{Cov}(R_{i}, R_{m})}{\text{Var}(R_{m})}$$

**Step 3: Replace the covariance-to-variance ratio with Beta.**

$$E(R_{i}) = R_{f} + \beta_{i} \cdot [E(R_{m}) - R_{f}]$$

**Step 4: Final CAPM equation for cost of equity.**

$$R_{e} = R_{f} + \beta \cdot (R_{m} - R_{f})$$

This is the **cost of equity** that a firm must offer to investors to compensate for the systematic risk of a technology investment.

---

### Derivation 2: NPV Decision Rule (Detailed)

**Step 1: The time value of money principle states that ₹1 today is worth more than ₹1 tomorrow because of opportunity cost.**

$$\text{PV} = \frac{\text{FV}}{(1 + r)^{n}}$$

**Step 2: For a technology project, total present value of all future cash flows:**

$$\text{PV}_{\text{total}} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{\text{CF}_{t}}{(1 + r)^{t}}$$

**Step 3: Net Present Value is the present value of inflows minus the initial outflow at $t = 0$:**

$$\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{\text{CF}_{t}}{(1 + r)^{t}} - C_{0}$$

**Step 4: Decision rule from the wealth-maximization principle of finance:**

- If $\text{NPV} > 0$, the project creates value (accept).
- If $\text{NPV} < 0$, the project destroys value (reject).
- If $\text{NPV} = 0$, the project breaks even on a discounted basis (indifferent).

---

### Derivation 3: WACC Derivation (Weighted Average)

**Step 1: Total capital = Equity + Debt.**

$$V = E + D$$

**Step 2: Each component has a cost:**
- Equity cost = $R_{e}$
- Debt cost (after tax) = $R_{d} \cdot (1 - T)$

**Step 3: Weighted average using capital structure weights:**

$$\text{WACC} = w_{e} \cdot R_{e} + w_{d} \cdot R_{d} \cdot (1 - T)$$

**Step 4: Substitute $w_{e} = \frac{E}{V}$ and $w_{d} = \frac{D}{V}$:**

$$\text{WACC} = \frac{E}{V} \cdot R_{e} + \frac{D}{V} \cdot R_{d} \cdot (1 - T)$$

---

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

### **Problem:**
A Kerala-based technology startup is evaluating a new AI-based product. The initial investment is **₹50 lakhs**. Expected cash flows for 5 years are: Year 1 = ₹12L, Year 2 = ₹15L, Year 3 = ₹18L, Year 4 = ₹20L, Year 5 = ₹22L. The WACC is **12%**. Should the firm accept the project?

### **Solution:**

**Step 1: Discount each year's cash flow to present value.**

$$\text{PV}_{1} = \frac{12}{(1.12)^{1}} = \frac{12}{1.12} = 10.714$$

$$\text{PV}_{2} = \frac{15}{(1.12)^{2}} = \frac{15}{1.2544} = 11.958$$

$$\text{PV}_{3} = \frac{18}{(1.12)^{3}} = \frac{18}{1.4049} = 12.812$$

$$\text{PV}_{4} = \frac{20}{(1.12)^{4}} = \frac{20}{1.5735} = 12.710$$

$$\text{PV}_{5} = \frac{22}{(1.12)^{5}} = \frac{22}{1.7623} = 12.485$$

**Step 2: Sum the present values of all cash inflows.**

$$\text{Total PV} = 10.714 + 11.958 + 12.812 + 12.710 + 12.485 = 60.679 \text{ lakhs}$$

**Step 3: Subtract the initial investment.**

$$\text{NPV} = 60.679 - 50 = 10.679 \text{ lakhs}$$

**Step 4: Decision.**

Since $\text{NPV} = +10.679 > 0$, the project **should be accepted**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

### Debt vs. Equity Financing for Technology

| Parameter | Debt Financing | Equity Financing |
|---|---|---|
| Ownership Dilution | None | Yes (significant) |
| Interest/Dividend | Mandatory | Discretionary |
| Tax Shield | Yes (interest is tax-deductible) | No |
| Risk to Firm | Higher (bankruptcy risk) | Lower |
| Best Suited For | Mature tech firms | Startups/Early-stage |
| Cost | Lower (4–10%) | Higher (15–25% expected return) |
| Control | Lender has covenants | Investor may demand board seat |

### Government vs. Private Financing

| Parameter | Government | Private (VC/Angel) |
|---|---|---|
| Objective | Public welfare, strategic autonomy | Profit maximization |
| Time Horizon | Long-term | 5–7 years exit |
| Quantum | Large (national missions) | Small to Medium |
| Strings Attached | Compliance, IP sharing | Milestones, board seats |
| Examples in India | TIDE 2.0, ASPIRE, MeitY | Peak XV, Accel, Blume |

---

## 3.4 Sources of Technology Financing in India — Detailed

### 3.4.1 Government Sources
- **TIDE 2.0** (Technology Incubation and Development of Entrepreneurs) — MeitY
- **ASPIRE** (Scheme for Promotion of Innovation, Rural Industries & Entrepreneurship)
- **BIRAC** (Biotechnology Industry Research Assistance Council)
- **DSIR** recognition for in-house R&D
- **Startup India Seed Fund Scheme (SISFS)**
- **SIDBI** (Small Industries Development Bank of India) funds
- **CFTRI, CSIR labs** for collaborative R&D

### 3.4.2 Private Sources
- **Angel Investors** — Individual high-net-worth investors (₹25L – ₹2Cr).
- **Venture Capital Funds** — Institutional investors in high-growth startups.
- **Private Equity** — Mature companies, large ticket sizes.
- **Bank Loans** — SIDBI, commercial banks, MUDRA loans.
- **Crowdfunding** — Kickstarter, Indiegogo, FuelADream.

### 3.4.3 Internal Sources
- **Retained earnings** — Most common in Indian MSMEs.
- **Depreciation reserves** — For replacement technology.
- **Working capital management** — Releasing locked-up funds.
- **Sale of unproductive assets** — To fund new tech.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Hierarchy of Technology Policy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CORPORATE TECHNOLOGY POLICY] --> B[Strategic Vision and Mission]
    A --> C[Long Term R and D Direction]
    A --> D[Capital Allocation Rules]
    B --> E[BUSINESS UNIT POLICY]
    C --> E
    D --> E
```
