# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define technology policy. List any four components of an enterprise-level technology policy.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Technology policy is a documented set of rules, principles, and decision-making guidelines that govern an organization's acquisition, development, protection, application, and diffusion of technology, aligned with its strategic goals.

**Four Components:**
1. **Technology Acquisition Policy** — Buy vs. Make vs. License decisions.
2. **R&D Investment Policy** — Allocation of revenue to research.
3. **Technology Protection Policy** — Patents, IP, trade secrets.
4. **Technology Audit Policy** — Periodic review of technology usage.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between internal and external linkages in the context of technology management.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Internal Linkages | External Linkages |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Within the firm | Outside the firm |
| **Examples** | R&D-Marketing, R&D-Finance | R&D-University, Firm-Supplier |
| **Purpose** | Coordination across departments | Knowledge inflow, partnerships |
| **Time Horizon** | Short to medium | Medium to long |
| **Control** | Direct (formal authority) | Indirect (contracts/MoUs) |

---

## 📗 PART B QUESTIONS — INTERNAL CHOICE (14 MARKS)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a) Explain the various components of an enterprise-level technology policy in detail. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

An enterprise-level technology policy is a structured framework that guides technology decisions. Its components are:

1. **Technology Vision Statement:** The long-term direction that aligns technology with the corporate vision. Example: Tata Motors' vision to be a leader in electric mobility.

2. **Technology Acquisition Policy:** Guidelines on whether to *make* technology in-house, *buy* from vendors, or *license* from others. It must address cost, time, and strategic fit.

3. **R&D Investment Policy:** Defines what percentage of revenue must be spent on R&D. Global benchmarks suggest 3–6% for tech firms and 1–2% for traditional manufacturing.

4. **Technology Protection Policy:** Covers patents, trademarks, copyrights, trade secrets, and IP litigation strategy.

5. **Technology Diffusion Policy:** Defines how new technology moves from R&D to production to marketing to customers.

6. **Technology Upgradation Policy:** Schedules for replacing obsolete machinery and adopting new processes.

7. **Technology Audit Policy:** A periodic (annual/quarterly) review of technology performance and compliance.

8. **Technology Partnership Policy:** Rules for engaging with external partners, including NDAs, IP-sharing norms, and exit clauses.

---

**(b) Discuss the various internal and external linkages that a technology firm must develop. Why are these linkages critical for success? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Internal Linkages:**

- **R&D ↔ Marketing:** Market needs feed R&D priorities; R&D provides innovative products to marketing.
- **R&D ↔ Production:** R&D designs products; production provides manufacturability feedback.
- **R&D ↔ Finance:** Finance allocates R&D budgets; R&D justifies them with ROI projections.
- **R&D ↔ HR:** HR provides skilled scientists and engineers; R&D indicates skill gaps.

**External Linkages:**

- **With Universities and Research Labs:** Source of basic research, talent, and publications.
- **With Suppliers:** Co-development of components and early access to new materials.
- **With Customers:** Voice-of-customer for innovation.
- **With Government:** Policy support, grants, regulatory clarity.
- **With Competitors:** Pre-competitive R&D consortia, industry standards.

**Why Critical:**
- Reduce duplication of effort.
- Accelerate time-to-market.
- Pool risk and resources.
- Access complementary skills and knowledge.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) Explain in detail the various sources of financing available for technology development at different stages of the technology lifecycle. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Stage | Risk Level | Funding Source | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Basic Research** | Very High | Government Grants (DST, CSIR, MeitY) | No commercial returns for years |
| **Applied R&D** | High | Internal Accruals, Government Co-funded Projects | Firm bears some risk |
| **Prototype** | High | Angel Investors, Seed VC, TIDE 2.0, SISFS | High risk tolerated by VCs |
| **Commercialization** | Medium | Venture Capital, Bank Loans, PE | Revenue prospects visible |
| **Upgradation** | Low | Retained Earnings, Technology Bonds | Firm has stable cash flows |

**Internal Sources:**
- Retained earnings (most common in Indian MSMEs).
- Depreciation reserves.
- Working capital release.
- Sale of unproductive assets.

**External Sources:**
- **Debt:** Bank loans, SIDBI, MUDRA, technology bonds.
- **Equity:** Angel, VC, PE, IPO, strategic investors.
- **Hybrid:** Convertible notes, mezzanine finance.
- **Government:** Grants, subsidies, tax holidays (Section 80-IAC of Income Tax).

---

**(b) A Kerala-based firm is evaluating a new automation technology with an initial investment of ₹80 lakhs. Expected cash inflows are: Year 1 = ₹20L, Year 2 = ₹25L, Year 3 = ₹30L, Year 4 = ₹25L, Year 5 = ₹20L. The WACC is 10%. Calculate the NPV and advise the firm. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Discount each cash flow.**

$$\text{PV}_{1} = \frac{20}{1.10} = 18.182$$

$$\text{PV}_{2} = \frac{25}{1.21} = 20.661$$

$$\text{PV}_{3} = \frac{30}{1.331} = 22.539$$

$$\text{PV}_{4} = \frac{25}{1.4641} = 17.076$$

$$\text{PV}_{5} = \frac{20}{1.61051} = 12.418$$

**Step 2: Sum the present values.**

$$\text{Total PV} = 18.182 + 20.661 + 22.539 + 17.076 + 12.418 = 90.876 \text{ lakhs}$$

**Step 3: NPV = Total PV − Initial Investment.**

$$\text{NPV} = 90.876 - 80 = +10.876 \text{ lakhs}$$

**Step 4: Decision.**

Since $\text{NPV} = +10.876 > 0$, the project creates value.

**Advice:** The firm **should accept** the new automation technology. The investment will generate ₹10.876 lakhs of additional value for shareholders at a 10% discount rate.

---

## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Policy with Strategy** — Policy is the *how*, strategy is the *what*. Examiners deduct marks for this.
> 2. **Forgetting Tax Shield** — In WACC calculations, always use $R_{d}(1-T)$, not just $R_{d}$.
> 3. **Wrong NPV Formula** — Some students write NPV without subtracting initial investment. Always write full formula.
> 4. **Confusing Linkages with Outsourcing** — Linkages are relationships, not transactions.
> 5. **In Financing Questions** — Don't blindly suggest VC. Match the funding source to the **stage** of technology and the **risk profile**.
> 6. **No Diagram in 14-mark answers** — Always include a flowchart or table; examiners reward visual clarity.
> 7. **Ignoring Indian context** — KTU expects mention of SIDBI, MeitY, BIRAC, Startup India, DSIR, etc.

---

## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### 📌 Quick-Recall Bullets:

- **Technology Policy** = the operational rulebook for technology decisions; spans corporate, business-unit, functional, and operational levels.
- **Linkages** are bidirectional — internal (R&D-Marketing-Finance-HR-Production) and external (Universities, Suppliers, Government, Customers, Competitors, Banks).
- **Five Technology Support Systems:** Financing, HRD, Information, R&D Infrastructure, Legal Framework.
- **Technology Financing** covers the entire lifecycle: Basic Research → Applied R&D → Prototype → Commercialization → Upgradation.
- **Each stage has a different risk profile** → hence a different funding source.
- **WACC** = $\frac{E}{V} R_{e} + \frac{D}{V} R_{d}(1-T)$ — used to evaluate the right capital mix.
- **CAPM:** $R_{e} = R_{f} + \beta(R_{m} - R_{f})$ — gives the cost of equity.
- **NPV > 0** → Accept; **NPV < 0** → Reject. Always discount with WACC.
- **R&D Intensity** = R&D Spend ÷ Revenue. Tech giants spend 15–20%; Indian IT firms spend 1.5–2.5%.
- **Indian Government Financing Schemes:** TIDE 2.0, ASPIRE, BIRAC, SISFS, SIDBI, MUDRA, DSIR recognition.
- **Valley of Death** is the funding gap between lab proof-of-concept and market launch — typically bridged by VC and angel capital.
- **Open Innovation** relies on external linkages (universities, startups, customers).
- **Technology Audit** is a mandatory policy component — periodic review of compliance.
- **Debt vs Equity Trade-off:** Debt has tax shield but bankruptcy risk; equity dilutes ownership but reduces risk.
- **KTU Board Pattern:** Expect one 3-mark direct question + one 14-mark question with internal choice per module.

### 🧠 Mnemonics for Exam Recall:

- **For Linkages → "I-M-F-H-P"** (Internal: R&D interacts with **M**arketing, **F**inance, **H**R, **P**roduction).
- **For External Linkages → "S-U-G-C-C-B"** (**S**uppliers, **U**niversities, **G**ovt, **C**ustomers, **C**ompetitors, **B**anks).
- **For Financing Stages → "B-A-P-C-U"** (**B**asic, **A**pplied, **P**rototype, **C**ommercial, **U**pgrade).
- **For Support Systems → "F-H-I-R-L"** (**F**inance, **H**RD, **I**nformation, **R**&D Infra, **L**egal).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Information Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET417 - TECHNOLOGY MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Information Systems # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition An **Information System (IS)** is a socio-technical, integrated set of components designed to **collect, process, store, and distribute information** to support **decision-making, coordination, control, analysis, and visualization** within an organization. In the formal KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, an Information System is defined as: > **Information System (IS):** A formal, organized arrangement of interconnected components — *people, hardware, software, data, networks, and procedures* — that *gathers, processes, stores, and disseminates information* to support the strategic, tactical, and operational goals of an enterprise. **Key Idea:** Information is data that has been *processed, contextualized, and made meaningful* for a specific decision-maker. An IS is the *machine* that performs this transformation continuously. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMET417 Module 4):** > Information Systems encompass Management Information Systems (MIS), Decision Support Systems (DSS), Transaction Processing Systems (TPS), Executive Support Systems (ESS), and Expert Systems (ES). They are the *digital backbone* of any technology-driven enterprise. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you run a small restaurant. Every day: - The **waiter** writes down orders on a slip → **Data capture** - The slip goes to the **kitchen** where the chef understands it → **Processing** - The chef writes down what was cooked and the bill → **Storage & retrieval** - The owner at the end of the day reads the bills to see which dish sold most → **Information for decision-making** Now imagine you automate that same flow with a **POS (Point-of-Sale) terminal connected to a central computer**. That automated ecosystem — billing software, database, printer, network, and the manager reading the dashboard — *is your Information System*. > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Takeaway:** > - **Data** = Raw facts ("Ravi bought 2 coffees") > - **Information** = Processed, contextualized data ("Coffee sales on Monday were 40% higher than Tuesday — restock more coffee beans on Sundays") > - **System** = The organized machinery (hardware + software + people + procedures) that turns data into information. > - **Information System** = Data + People + Process + Technology → Better Decisions. --- ## 1.3 The Three Core Activities of an IS | Activity | Description | Real-World Example | |---|---|---| | **Input** | Captures raw data from inside/outside the org | Barcode scan at supermarket checkout | | **Processing** | Converts data into meaningful information | Calculating total bill, applying GST | | **Output** | Delivers information to decision-makers | Daily sales dashboard for the manager | Additionally, IS includes **Feedback** (correcting input) and **Control** (monitoring performance). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Metric / Definition to Remember:** > - **Data** — Unprocessed raw facts; context-less. > - **Information** — Data that has been *processed, organized, or structured* to add meaning. > - **Knowledge** — Information *combined with experience, context, and understanding* to support action. > > The hierarchy is: **Data → Information → Knowledge → Wisdom (DIKW Pyramid)**, an essential KTU short-answer concept. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet ## 2.1 Components of an Information System Every IS (irrespective of size) consists of **five interdependent components**. KTU repeatedly tests these: ### 2.1.1 Hardware - The **physical equipment** — servers, workstations, laptops, scanners, IoT sensors, networking devices. - Executes the data processing cycle. - Includes peripheral, processing, and storage devices. ### 2.1.2 Software - **System software** (Operating Systems like Windows/Linux, DBMS). - **Application software** (Enterprise Resource Planning — ERP, Customer Relationship Management — CRM, payroll). - Instructs the hardware on *what* to do. ### 2.1.3 Data - The **raw material** of any IS. Without data, the system is useless. - Stored in **databases**, **data warehouses**, **data marts**, or **data lakes**. - Must be **accurate, timely, relevant, and complete** (the four pillars of data quality). ### 2.1.4 People - **Users** (managers, employees, customers). - **IS specialists** (developers, DBAs, network admins). - **End-users** are the *actual* consumers of the information generated. ### 2.1.5 Procedures / Processes - Written rules, policies, and protocols governing **how** data is captured, validated, processed, and reported. - Examples: Data-entry SOPs, approval workflows, audit trails. --- ## 2.2 The DIKW Hierarchy (Detailed) $$ \text{Data} \;\xrightarrow{\text{contextualize}}\; \text{Information} \;\xrightarrow{\text{apply experience}}\; \text{Knowledge} \;\xrightarrow{\text{integrate values}}\; \text{Wisdom} $$ | Level | Definition | Example | |---|---|---| | **Data** | Discrete, objective facts | "Temperature = 38.5°C" | | **Information** | Data with context and purpose | "Patient Ravi has fever of 38.5°C" | | **Knowledge** | Information applied via experience | "Ravi likely has a viral infection; prescribe paracetamol" | | **Wisdom** | Knowledge evaluated with values & future vision | "Treat patient holistically and advise lifestyle changes to prevent recurrence" | > [!NOTE] > **Why It Matters in KTU:** Questions like *"Differentiate data and information"* or *"Explain the DIKW pyramid"* are **favourite 3-mark questions**. Always anchor your answer with a single-line crisp definition + one example. --- ## 2.3 Classification / Types of Information Systems KTU emphasizes **Laudon's Five-Level Pyramid** (sometimes called the **Anand & Behl Management IS Framework**). The pyramid maps *organisational levels* to the *type of IS* used: | Organisational Level | Type of IS | Primary Purpose | |---|---|---| | **Operational (Bottom)** | Transaction Processing System (**TPS**) | Process day-to-day transactions | | **Knowledge (Lower-Middle)** | Knowledge Management System (**KMS**) / Office Automation System (OAS) | Help knowledge workers & data workers | | **Middle Management** | Management Information System (**MIS**) | Provide routine reports & summaries | | **Senior Management** | Decision Support System (**DSS**) | Support semi-structured decisions | | **Strategic (Top)** | Executive Support System (**ESS)** / Strategic IS | Support long-term strategic planning | In addition, KTU highlights: - **Expert Systems (ES)** — capture human expert knowledge for repetitive specialized decisions. - **Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP)** — integrates *all* departments via a single database (SAP, Oracle). - **Customer Relationship Management (CRM)** — manages customer data and interactions. - **Supply Chain Management Systems (SCM)** — coordinates flows of goods, info, finances. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula / Framework Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Strategic Value of IS" Cheat Sheet — the most-tested KTU Module-4 framework:** > > The strategic value of an IS can be conceptualized by **linking it to Porter's Five Forces** and the **Value Chain**. | Concept | Role of IS | |---|---| | **Porter's Value Chain – Primary Activities** (Inbound Logistics, Operations, Outbound, Marketing & Sales, Service) | IS *automates, optimizes, and tracks* each activity | | **Porter's Value Chain – Support Activities** (HR, Tech, Procurement, Infrastructure) | IS *enables knowledge sharing* across the firm | | **Porter's Five Forces** | IS builds *switching costs, differentiation, barriers to entry* | | **Michael Hammer's Re-engineering** | IS enables *radical process redesign*, not just automation | | **McFarlan's Strategic Grid** | Classifies IS as *Operational, Strategic, High-potential, Factory* | ### 2.4.1 McFarlan's Strategic Grid — High-Yield KTU Table | | Present Strategic | Future Strategic | |---|---|---| | **High Potential** | Turnaround | Strategic | | **Low Potential** | Factory | Support | - **Factory** IS — Necessary but not strategic (e.g., payroll). - **Support** IS — Critical today, but won't be tomorrow. - **Turnaround** IS — Currently high-impact, likely to be replaced. - **Strategic** IS — Will define future competitive advantage. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Utility:** Companies like Amazon, Uber, and Zomato leverage *Strategic IS* (predictive analytics, recommendation engines, real-time logistics) to build **sustainable competitive advantage** — a frequent KTU essay topic. --- ## 2.5 Why Information Systems Matter — The Five Strategic Objectives 1. **Operational Excellence** — Improved efficiency, lower cost. 2. **New Products, Services & Business Models** — IS enables innovation (e.g., Netflix's streaming model). 3. **Customer & Supplier Intimacy** — Better relationships via CRM/SCM. 4. **Improved Decision-Making** — Real-time dashboards. 5. **Competitive Advantage** — Beating rivals using information asymmetry. 6. **Survival** — In a digital economy, no firm can *afford not to digitize*. --- ## 2.6 Information Systems & Competitive Forces (Porter's Model) IS impact the **Five Forces**: - **Threat of new entrants** — IS raises entry barriers (e.g., high IT capex). - **Bargaining power of suppliers** — IS enables better supplier management. - **Bargaining power of buyers** — CRM gives firms *information about customers*. - **Threat of substitutes** — IS enables product differentiation. - **Rivalry among competitors** — IS enables price/quality competition. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Board-Examiner Phrase to Memorize:** > *"Information Systems convert data into a strategic asset. They do not merely automate existing processes — they re-define the very basis of competition."* # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Decision-Making Matrix ## 3.1 The Information Processing Cycle (Step-by-Step) The complete operational flow inside any IS follows a **standard 4-stage cycle**: $$ \text{Input} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Processing} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Output} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Feedback} $$ ### Step 1 — Input **Logic:** Capture raw data from internal (e.g., sales transactions) or external (e.g., market prices) sources. **How:** Forms, barcode scanners, sensors, online forms, ERP transaction screens. **Engineering analogue:** A transducer converting a physical signal (e.g., temperature) into a digital signal. ### Step 2 — Processing **Logic:** Convert raw data into meaningful information through calculations, comparisons, sorting, aggregation. **How:** CPUs execute program instructions; DBMS engines apply queries (e.g., `SELECT SUM(sales) FROM orders WHERE date = 'today'`). **Engineering analogue:** Signal conditioning and filtering. ### Step 3 — Output **Logic:** Deliver useful information to the right person at the right time. **How:** Reports, dashboards, emails, alerts, mobile push notifications. **Engineering analogue:** Display units and actuators presenting the processed output. ### Step 4 — Feedback **Logic:** Compare output against expectations; correct input errors; refine processes. **How:** Audit logs, exception reports, control totals, performance dashboards. **Engineering analogue:** Closed-loop control systems (negative feedback). > [!NOTE] > **Continuous-flow observation:** In real-time IS (e.g., stock trading), the cycle repeats every **millisecond**. In batch IS (e.g., payroll), it may run **monthly**. Both follow the same 4-stage logic. --- ## 3.2 Decision-Making Matrix: TPS vs MIS vs DSS vs ESS vs ES KTU frequently asks: *"Compare TPS, MIS, DSS, ESS and Expert Systems."* Use this matrix to score full marks. | Dimension | **TPS** | **MIS** | **DSS** | **ESS** | **Expert System** | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **User** | Operations staff | Middle managers | Analysts / Sr. managers | Top executives | Non-experts | | **Decision Type** | Structured | Semi-structured | Semi-structured | Unstructured | Specialized, structured | | **Data Source** | Internal, transactional | Internal summarized DB | Internal + external | Internal + external + industry | Knowledge base + rules | | **Time Horizon** | Daily / real-time | Weekly / monthly | Ad-hoc | Years / strategic | Real-time | | **Output** | Transaction receipts, logs | Routine summary reports | What-if analyses, models | Strategic projections, trend graphs | Recommendations, diagnoses | | **Example** | ATM, POS billing | Monthly sales MIS report | What-if pricing model | 5-year strategy dashboard | Medical diagnosis system (MYCIN) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Board-Examiner Tip:** When asked *differentiate*, structure the answer as a *comparison table* on **at least 4 dimensions** (User, Decision Type, Data, Output). Tables are worth more visual marks in KTU valuation. --- ## 3.3 How IS Enables Strategic Decision-Making (Step-by-Step Reasoning) **Problem:** A retail firm is losing market share. The CEO needs an IS-driven answer. **Step 1 — Identify information need:** "We need to know *why* customers are leaving." **Step 2 — Determine the right IS layer:** - Operational data (sales by SKU) comes from **TPS**. - Customer churn patterns → **MIS** (drill-down reports). - Pricing simulations → **DSS** (what-if analysis). - 5-year strategic move → **ESS**. **Step 3 — Decide on data architecture:** $$ \text{TPS data} \;\xrightarrow{\text{ETL}}\; \text{Data Warehouse} \;\xrightarrow{\text{OLAP}}\; \text{MIS / DSS dashboards} $$ (ETL = Extract, Transform, Load) **Step 4 — Implement controls and feedback:** - Audit trails. - Data quality KPIs (accuracy %, completeness %, timeliness %). **Step 5 — Continuous improvement:** - Move up the strategic grid: Factory → Support → Strategic. > [!NOTE] > **Mathematical Expression of Data Quality Score (often tested as a 3-marker):** > >$$ >\text{Data Quality Index (DQI)} \;=\; w_1 \cdot \text{Accuracy} \;+\; w_2 \cdot \text{Completeness} \;+\; w_3 \cdot \text{Timeliness} \;+\; w_4 \cdot \text{Consistency} >$$ > >where $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + w_4 = 1$, and each weight represents the strategic importance of that dimension. --- ## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Building a Decision Support System A canonical **DSS build process** — useful for KTU descriptive answers: **Step 1 — Problem identification** — Define the semi-structured decision (e.g., "Which city should we open our next store in?"). **Step 2 — Data acquisition** — Pull historical sales, demographic, competitor data into a data mart. **Step 3 — Model selection** — Choose a quantitative model (regression, simulation, optimization). **Step 4 — User Interface Design** — Build intuitive dashboards so the manager can run "what-if" scenarios. **Step 5 — Sensitivity Analysis** — Vary inputs to test robustness of recommendations. **Step 6 — Decision and Implementation** — Manager commits; IS records the decision for future learning. > [!NOTE] > **Remember this order:** *Identify → Acquire → Model → Interface → Analyze → Decide.* Examiners love this as a structured 7-marker. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Five Components of an Information System ```mermaid flowchart TB A[PEOPLE
Users Managers IS Specialists] --> B[INFORMATION SYSTEM CORE] H[PROCEDURES
Policies Rules SOPs] --> B B --> C[OUTPUT
Reports Dashboards Alerts] D[HARDWARE
Servers PCs IoT Sensors] --> B E[SOFTWARE
OS DBMS ERP CRM Apps] --> B F[DATA
Raw Facts in Databases] --> B B --> G[FEEDBACK LOOP
Audit Logs Control Totals] G --> A G --> F ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The **central box** is the IS processing engine. - The **5 inputs** to it are the canonical components (People, Hardware, Software, Data, Procedures). - **Output** flows outward to decision-makers. - **Feedback** loops back to refine input and data quality. --- ## 4.2 The Five-Level Pyramid of Information Systems ```mermaid flowchart TB L1[STRATEGIC LEVEL - EXECUTIVES]:::top --> L2[SENIOR MANAGEMENT - ANALYSTS]:::mid L2 --> L3[MIDDLE MANAGEMENT - MANAGERS]:::mid L3 --> L4[KNOWLEDGE LEVEL - KNOWLEDGE WORKERS]:::low L4 --> L5[OPERATIONAL LEVEL - OPERATIONS STAFF]:::low L1 --> S1[EXECUTIVE SUPPORT SYSTEM - ESS] L2 --> S2[DECISION SUPPORT SYSTEM - DSS] L3 --> S3[MANAGEMENT INFORMATION SYSTEM - MIS] L4 --> S4[KNOWLEDGE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM - KMS] L5 --> S5[TRANSACTION PROCESSING SYSTEM - TPS] classDef top fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef mid fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef low fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The **right column** lists the corresponding IS type for each organisational level. - Each level feeds information *upward* and receives directives *downward*. --- ## 4.3 The DIKW Pyramid ```mermaid flowchart TB W[WISDOM
Apply values and judgement] --> K[KNOWLEDGE
Information + Experience] K --> I[INFORMATION
Data with context and meaning] I --> D[DATA
Raw isolated facts] D --> S[SIGNAL SOURCE
Transactions Sensors Forms] S --> D ``` **Reading the diagram:** - Information flows **upward** from raw signal source → wisdom. - At each step, *human cognitive effort* is added. - An IS typically handles Data → Information → Knowledge; *Wisdom remains a human responsibility.* --- ## 4.4 IS Impact on Porter's Value Chain ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PrimaryActivities["PRIMARY ACTIVITIES"] P1[Inbound Logistics] --> P2[Operations] P2 --> P3[Outbound Logistics] P3 --> P4[Marketing and Sales] P4 --> P5[Service] end subgraph SupportActivities["SUPPORT ACTIVITIES"] SU1[Procurement] --> SU2[HR Management] SU2 --> SU3[Tech Infrastructure] SU3 --> SU4[Firm Infrastructure] end SupportActivities --> PrimaryActivities IS[INFORMATION SYSTEM LAYER]:::center -.-> PrimaryActivities IS -.-> SupportActivities classDef center fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The **dashed line** from "IS Layer" indicates it is a *cross-cutting enabler*, not a stand-alone activity. - It touches **every** activity, both primary and support. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Differentiate between Data and Information with examples. *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Data | Information | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Raw, unprocessed facts | Processed, contextualized data | | **Nature** | Context-less | Meaningful, purposeful | | **Example** | "Ravi bought 2 coffees at 10:15 AM" | "Coffee sales on Monday were 40% higher than Tuesday — restock more coffee beans on Sunday" | | **Volume** | Large, structured or unstructured | Condensed, decision-oriented | | **Use** | Stored for later use | Used directly for decision-making | **One-line crisp definition (for the 1-mark part):** - **Data:** Discrete, objective facts without context. - **Information:** Data that has been processed, organized, or interpreted to convey meaning. --- ### Q2. What is an Information System? List its components. *[KTU University Exam – May 2024]* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** An Information System is a socio-technical arrangement of components that **collects, processes, stores, and disseminates information** to support decision-making, coordination, control, and analysis in an organization. **Components (2 marks — name any 4–5):** 1. **Hardware** — physical equipment. 2. **Software** — programs and OS. 3. **Data** — raw material. 4. **People** — users, developers, managers. 5. **Procedures** — policies and workflows. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) > **Note to students:** In KTU ESE, Part B Module questions carry **14 marks** and usually have *internal choice*. Two alternatives are given below. Attempt **ONE**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** #### (a) Explain the different types of Information Systems used in organizations. How do they support different managerial levels? *(7 marks)* *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]* **Model Answer (Step-by-Step, 7 marks):** **Introduction (1 mark):** Different managerial levels face *different kinds of decisions* — operational, tactical, strategic. Information Systems are classified based on the **organisational level they serve** and the **type of decision they support**. **Body (5 marks):** 1. **Transaction Processing System (TPS)** — Operational level. - Handles day-to-day transactions (sales, payroll, inventory). - Highly structured, high-volume, real-time. - Example: ATM, POS billing. 2. **Knowledge Management System (KMS)** / Office Automation — Knowledge level. - Helps knowledge workers (engineers, doctors) create & share knowledge. - Tools: document management, collaboration platforms. - Example: SharePoint, Confluence. 3. **Management Information System (MIS)** — Middle management. - Provides routine **summary reports** (weekly sales, monthly variance analysis). - Structured data flow from TPS to MIS via databases. 4. **Decision Support System (DSS)** — Senior management / analysts. - Supports **semi-structured** decisions using "what-if" models. - Integrates internal + external data. - Example: Pricing optimization, route planning. 5. **Executive Support System (ESS)** — Strategic level. - Provides **long-term strategic information** via dashboards, trend graphs, scenario analysis. - Tailored for top executives — minimal jargon. 6. **Expert Systems (ES)** — Specialized decision-making. - Encode human expert knowledge in **rule-based engines**. - Example: MYCIN (medical diagnosis), credit-scoring systems. **Conclusion (1 mark):** Together, these systems form the **organisational information architecture**, ensuring that the *right information reaches the right person at the right time* across every managerial layer. --- #### (b) Discuss the role of Information Systems in achieving strategic competitive advantage. Refer to Porter's Value Chain and Five Forces. *(7 marks)* *[KTU University Exam – May 2024]* **Model Answer (Step-by-Step, 7 marks):** **Introduction (1 mark):** Information Systems are not merely operational tools — when strategically deployed, they become **drivers of competitive advantage**. Porter's models (Value Chain & Five Forces) provide a robust framework to analyze this. **Porter's Value Chain (3 marks):** | Primary Activity | IS Role | |---|---| | Inbound Logistics | RFID, supplier portals, ERP for inventory | | Operations | Automation, robotics, real-time MES | | Outbound Logistics | GPS tracking, automated warehousing | | Marketing & Sales | CRM, targeted digital marketing | | Service | Knowledge bases, chatbots, ticket systems | | Support Activity | IS Role | |---|---| | HR | HRIS, e-learning, talent analytics | | Technology | R&D databases, simulation tools | | Procurement | E-procurement portals | | Infrastructure | Enterprise-wide reporting, governance | **Porter's Five Forces (2 marks):** - **Threat of new entrants:** High IT capex raises entry barriers. - **Bargaining power of suppliers:** IS enables benchmarking & e-sourcing. - **Bargaining power of buyers:** CRM gives firms superior customer insight. - **Threat of substitutes:** IS-enabled innovation (e.g., streaming vs. DVD). - **Rivalry:** Real-time price intelligence, fast-cycle product launches. **Conclusion (1 mark):** IS must align with the *business strategy*, not the other way around. Firms that integrate IS into *every primary and support activity* can build **durable, defensible competitive advantage** — Amazon, Uber, and Flipkart are textbook examples. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** #### (a) Explain the DIKW hierarchy. Why is it important in the context of Information Systems? *(7 marks)* *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]* **Model Answer (Step-by-Step, 7 marks):** **Introduction (1 mark):** The **DIKW (Data-Information-Knowledge-Wisdom) hierarchy** is the foundational model that explains how raw facts get transformed into actionable insight. It is the conceptual backbone of any Information System. **Levels Explained (4 marks):** 1. **Data** — Discrete, unprocessed facts with no context. - Example: "39.2, 38.5, 40.1" — three temperature readings. 2. **Information** — Data processed to add meaning. - Example: "Ravi's temperature reading was 38.5°C at 10 AM." 3. **Knowledge** — Information combined with experience, patterns, and context. - Example: "A 38.5°C fever in adults typically suggests a viral infection; paracetamol is the standard first-line treatment." 4. **Wisdom** — Knowledge evaluated with values, judgement, and foresight. - Example: "Beyond treating the fever, advise Ravi on lifestyle changes to prevent recurrence — diet, sleep, stress management." **Importance in IS (2 marks):** - IS primarily handles the *Data → Information → Knowledge* layers. - IS must deliver *the right information to the right person at the right time* (Alfred's Law). - MIS/DSS work at the **information layer**; KMS/Expert Systems work at the **knowledge layer**; ESS supports **wisdom** by surfacing strategic context. **Conclusion (1 mark):** Understanding DIKW helps IS designers avoid the *data-rich but information-poor (DRIP)* syndrome — a common failure mode where firms collect massive data but cannot convert it into useful information or knowledge. --- #### (b) Describe the components of an Information System. With a neat diagram, explain the Information Processing Cycle. *(7 marks)* *[KTU University Exam – May 2023]* **Model Answer (Step-by-Step, 7 marks):** **Components (3 marks):** 1. **Hardware** — The physical equipment: servers, PCs, mobile devices, IoT sensors, network gear. 2. **Software** — System software (OS, DBMS) and application software (ERP, CRM, custom apps). 3. **Data** — The raw material; stored in databases, warehouses, or lakes. 4. **People** — Users (managers, staff, customers), IS specialists (developers, DBAs). 5. **Procedures** — Documented rules, policies, SOPs governing data handling. **Information Processing Cycle (4 marks):** 1. **Input** — Capture raw data via forms, scanners, sensors. 2. **Processing** — Compute, sort, aggregate, classify. 3. **Output** — Deliver information via reports, dashboards, alerts. 4. **Feedback** — Compare output against expectations; correct input errors; refine procedures. > A neat diagram should show: > *(Input) → (Processing) → (Output) → (Feedback) → (back to Input)* **Conclusion:** This cycle is the *atomic unit* of every IS — from a basic POS to a global ERP. Understanding it allows managers to design systems that are accurate, timely, and aligned with decision needs. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Don't confuse Data with Information.** Many students write "data is information" — *instant 1-mark loss*. Always anchor the difference with *context*. > 2. **Don't list components without explaining roles.** Naming Hardware/Software/People/Data/Procedures is only 2 marks; *explaining what each does* fetches the remaining. > 3. **In Porter-related answers**, *mention both* the Value Chain *and* the Five Forces explicitly — students often forget one, losing 2 marks. > 4. **DIKW must show *examples at each level.*** Writing only the definitions loses the example marks. > 5. **For the Information Processing Cycle, *always* include the Feedback loop.** Many students stop at Output — a costly omission. > 6. **Avoid generic statements** like *"IS helps in decision-making"*. Be specific — *"MIS provides weekly sales summary reports to middle managers for variance analysis."* --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Definition:** Information System = integrated components (People, Hardware, Software, Data, Procedures) that collect, process, store, and distribute information. - **DIKW Hierarchy:** Data → Information → Knowledge → Wisdom — *always with examples*. - **Five components of IS:** People, Hardware, Software, Data, Procedures. - **Information Processing Cycle:** Input → Processing → Output → Feedback. - **Types of IS by organisational level:** - Operational → **TPS** - Knowledge → **KMS / OAS** - Middle management → **MIS** - Senior management → **DSS** - Strategic → **ESS** - Specialized → **Expert Systems (ES)** - **Decision-type mapping:** - Structured → TPS - Semi-structured → MIS / DSS - Unstructured → ESS - **Porter's Value Chain:** IS is a *cross-cutting enabler* of every primary and support activity. - **Porter's Five Forces:** IS raises entry barriers, enables CRM/SCM, supports differentiation. - **McFarlan's Strategic Grid:** Factory / Support / Turnaround / Strategic. - **Information quality pillars:** Accurate, Timely, Relevant, Complete. - **DRIP syndrome:** Data-Rich, Information-Poor — a common IS design failure. - **Alfred's Law:** *Right information, to the right person, at the right time, in the right format.* - **Key examples to memorize:** - ATM / POS → TPS - MYCIN → Expert System (medical) - SAP → ERP - Salesforce → CRM - Netflix recommendation engine → DSS / Strategic IS - **Strategic objectives of IS:** Operational excellence, new products/services, customer/supplier intimacy, improved decision-making, competitive advantage, survival. - **Avoid:** Mixing up *MIS* and *DSS*. MIS = routine reports (past data); DSS = what-if models (future-oriented). - **Remember the diagram:** Five-level pyramid (Strategic at top, Operational at bottom) maps cleanly to ESS → DSS → MIS → KMS → TPS. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Mnemonic for KTU Viva / Last-Minute Revision:** > **"P-H-S-D-Pr"** — *People, Hardware, Software, Data, Procedures* (the five components of any IS). > **"T-K-M-D-E"** — *TPS, KMS, MIS, DSS, ESS* (the five-level pyramid bottom-up).

topics.title: Organizing Technology at Enterprise Level, Technology Forecasting: Innovation Chain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET417 - TECHNOLOGY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Organizing Technology at Enterprise Level, Technology Forecasting: Innovation Chain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Organizing Technology at the Enterprise Level — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Organizing Technology at the Enterprise Level** is the structural and procedural framework through which a firm identifies, acquires, integrates, deploys, and renews its technological capabilities to achieve strategic objectives. It answers the *who, where, how, and with what authority* decisions governing technology across business units.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, the topic spans three interconnected decisions:
1. **Structural Decision** — Where does the technology function sit in the org chart? (Centralized R&D vs. Decentralized vs. Matrix)
2. **Authority Decision** — Who owns the technology budget, IP, and talent? (CTO, Technology Council, BU Heads)
3. **Process Decision** — How are projects selected, funded, and terminated? (Stage-Gate, Portfolio Models, Audits)

### 1.2 Technology Forecasting — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Technology Forecasting** is the systematic, probabilistic, and science-based anticipation of future technological capabilities, performance parameters, adoption rates, and discontinuities, used to inform R&D investment, product roadmaps, and strategic positioning.

It is **not** prediction in the deterministic sense; it is a *decision-support discipline* that reduces uncertainty within bounded confidence intervals.

### 1.3 Innovation Chain — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Innovation Chain** (also called the **Innovation Funnel** or **Research-to-Commercialization Pipeline**) is the sequenced set of value-adding activities that transform a scientific discovery into a marketable product, service, or process. Each link adds information, refines uncertainty, and transfers ownership of the knowledge asset from one functional silo to the next.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogies for Quick Retention

| Concept | Real-World Analogy | Core Insight |
|---|---|---|
| Organizing Technology | **An orchestra conductor** — coordinates strings (R&D), brass (manufacturing), and woodwinds (marketing) using a shared score (strategy) | Technology must be orchestrated, not left to play solo |
| Technology Forecasting | **Weather forecasting using satellites** — based on historical data, mathematical models, and probability, never 100% certain | Forecasting is *probabilistic*; it manages risk, not eliminates it |
| Innovation Chain | **A relay race** — the baton (idea) is passed between specialized runners (R&D, engineering, production, sales); drop the baton = innovation failure | The *handoff interface* is the weakest link — not the runners themselves |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Term to Memorize:** *S-Shaped Curve* — almost every technology follows an S-curve of performance vs. effort: slow start, steep growth, saturation. The strategic art is knowing **where on the S-curve your firm is operating** and **when to switch to the next S-curve**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Organizing Technology — Three Structural Models

#### 2.1.1 Centralized Model
- **Logic:** All technology personnel, labs, and budgets sit under a single Chief Technology Officer (CTO) at corporate HQ.
- **Why firms choose it:** Prevents duplication, enables firm-wide IP leverage, allows long-horizon research.
- **How it works:** Business units submit *technology requests*; central R&D executes and licenses back.
- **Limitation:** Slow response to BU-specific market needs; "Ivory Tower" syndrome.

#### 2.1.2 Decentralized Model
- **Logic:** Each Business Unit (BU) maintains its own engineering/technology team with independent budgets.
- **Why firms choose it:** Speed, market sensitivity, entrepreneurial culture.
- **Limitation:** Duplication of effort, fragmented IP, no firm-wide technology platform.

#### 2.1.3 Matrix Model (Most Common in KTU Case Studies)
- **Logic:** A dual-reporting structure — engineers report *both* to a functional manager (CTO) and a project/BU manager.
- **Why firms choose it:** Balances specialization with flexibility; resources can be pooled and redeployed.
- **How it works:** Project managers "borrow" engineers; functional managers "own" career paths and capability development.
- **Limitation:** Conflict of priorities, dual-boss confusion, requires mature HR systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** The question *"Compare centralized and decentralized technology organizations"* is a *guaranteed 7-marker* on ESE. Always structure the answer using **5 parameters**: Decision Speed, Cost, Innovation Type, IP Control, and Bureaucracy.

### 2.2 Organizing Technology — Four Auxiliary Mechanisms

1. **Technology Council / Steering Committee** — cross-functional body that reviews and prioritizes the technology portfolio.
2. **Stage-Gate Process** — funnel model where ideas pass through Discovery → Scoping → Business Case → Development → Testing → Launch.
3. **Technology Audit** — periodic assessment of *where* the firm's technology is, *where* it needs to be, and the *gap*.
4. **Strategic Alliances & Open Innovation** — leveraging external tech via licensing, JVs, university partnerships.

### 2.3 Technology Forecasting — Taxonomy of Methods

> [!NOTE]
> KTU expects students to know **at least 3 forecasting methods** with their strengths, weaknesses, and one worked example each. The four primary families are:

| Family | Methods | Best Used For | Time Horizon |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Normative (Goal-Oriented)** | Relevance Tree, Morphological Analysis, Mission Flow | Forecasting what is *needed* to reach a goal | 10–30 years |
| **Exploratory (Trend-Based)** | Trend Extrapolation, S-Curve, Growth Curves | Extending existing trajectories | 5–15 years |
| **Expert-Based (Judgmental)** | Delphi Method, Scenario Writing, Expert Panels | Disruptive/emerging tech | 5–20 years |
| **Quantitative (Statistical)** | Time-Series, Regression, Exponential Smoothing | Stable, mature technologies | 1–5 years |

### 2.4 The Innovation Chain — Stage-by-Stage Logic

The most pedagogically accepted model is the **Kline-Rosenberg Chain-Linked Model (1986)**, which replaced the older **"Linear Model"** (1945 — Bush, *Science, The Endless Frontier*).

#### 2.4.1 The Six Canonical Stages

1. **Basic Research (BR)** — Push from science. Generates *new knowledge* (e.g., semiconductor physics).
2. **Applied Research (AR)** — Directed toward a specific problem (e.g., MOSFET design).
3. **Development (D)** — Translates AR into a prototype (e.g., first transistor prototype).
4. **Engineering / Manufacturing Engineering (E)** — Scales the prototype for production.
5. **Production / Manufacturing (M)** — Volume production with quality control.
6. **Marketing & Commercialization (S)** — Launch, sales, distribution, after-sales service.

#### 2.4.2 The Coupling Loops (Why It Is "Chain-Linked")

Kline-Rosenberg identified **5 feedback loops** that make the chain non-linear:

$$f_1: M \rightarrow BR \quad \text{(Manufacturing problems trigger new basic research)}$$

$$f_2: M \rightarrow AR \quad \text{(Production issues spawn applied research)}$$

$$f_3: M \rightarrow D \quad \text{(Process feedback to design)}$$

$$f_4: S \rightarrow BR \quad \text{(Market failures push back to fundamentals)}$$

$$f_5: \text{Knowledge} \rightarrow BR \quad \text{(External scientific advances enter the chain)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to "explain the chain-linked model", always **draw the feedback loops** with arrows. The mere act of drawing $f_1$ through $f_5$ gets you 3 of the 14 marks. The linear model, by contrast, *fails* because it has no feedback — production problems never loop back to research.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are examinable. Units are SI unless otherwise noted.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Variables | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Linear Trend Extrapolation** | $y_t = a + b \cdot t$ | $y_t$ = forecast, $t$ = time, $a,b$ = regression coefficients | $y$ in original units | Short-term forecasting of mature technologies |
| 2 | **Exponential Smoothing** | $F_{t+1} = \alpha A_t + (1-\alpha) F_t$ | $\alpha$ = smoothing constant $(0 < \alpha < 1)$ | Dimensionless | Inventory, demand, and adoption-rate forecasting |
| 3 | **Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR)** | $\text{CAGR} = \left( \dfrac{V_f}{V_i} \right)^{1/n} - 1$ | $V_f$ = final value, $V_i$ = initial, $n$ = years | Decimal or % | Tech market sizing |
| 4 | **Bass Diffusion Model** | $\dfrac{dN(t)}{dt} = \left[ p + q \dfrac{N(t)}{m} \right] \left[ m - N(t) \right]$ | $p$ = innovation coeff, $q$ = imitation coeff, $m$ = market potential | $N$ in units | Forecasting new product adoption (e.g., smartphones, EVs) |
| 5 | **S-Curve Logistic Growth** | $y(t) = \dfrac{L}{1 + e^{-k(t - t_0)}}$ | $L$ = ceiling, $k$ = steepness, $t_0$ = inflection time | $y$ in original units | Technology performance maturity |
| 6 | **Technology Audit Score (TAS)** | $\text{TAS} = w_1 P + w_2 R + w_3 C + w_4 S$ | $P$ = patents, $R$ = R&D spend, $C$ = capability, $S$ = strategic fit; $w$ = weights | Weighted index (0–100) | Internal technology assessment |
| 7 | **R&D Intensity** | $\text{R\&D Intensity} = \dfrac{\text{R\&D Expenditure}}{\text{Revenue}} \times 100\%$ | — | Percentage | Benchmarking tech investment |
| 8 | **Time-to-Market (TTM)** | $\text{TTM} = t_{\text{launch}} - t_{\text{concept}}$ | $t$ in months | Months | Innovation chain efficiency |

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Manufacturing firms** use Bass Diffusion to plan production capacity for EVs.
- **Pharmaceutical companies** use Stage-Gate to manage the 10–12 year drug development chain.
- **Semiconductor firms** use S-curve analysis to time the transition from 7nm to 5nm nodes.
- **Engineering consultancies** use Technology Audits to advise clients on R&D portfolio gaps.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Exponential Smoothing (α = 0.3) for a Technology Adoption Series

**Problem:** A firm tracks the number of customers adopting its new IoT platform. The actual values $A_t$ for 5 months are given below. Using **simple exponential smoothing** with smoothing constant $\alpha = 0.3$ and initial forecast $F_1 = 50$, compute the forecasts for months 2 through 6 and the Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD).

| Month $t$ | Actual $A_t$ |
|---|---|
| 1 | 50 |
| 2 | 55 |
| 3 | 60 |
| 4 | 58 |
| 5 | 65 |

#### Step 1 — State the Recurrence Relation

The fundamental exponential smoothing equation is:

$$F_{t+1} = \alpha A_t + (1 - \alpha) F_t$$

where:
- $F_{t+1}$ = forecast for next period
- $A_t$ = actual value in current period
- $F_t$ = forecast made for current period
- $\alpha$ = smoothing constant

**Logic:** The new forecast is a *weighted average* of the actual value (weight $\alpha$) and the previous forecast (weight $1 - \alpha$). Low $\alpha$ = smoother, less reactive. High $\alpha$ = more responsive, more noise.

#### Step 2 — Apply to Month 2

$$F_2 = \alpha A_1 + (1 - \alpha) F_1$$

Substituting $\alpha = 0.3$, $A_1 = 50$, $F_1 = 50$:

$$F_2 = (0.3)(50) + (0.7)(50) = 15 + 35 = 50$$

#### Step 3 — Apply to Month 3

$$F_3 = \alpha A_2 + (1 - \alpha) F_2$$

$$F_3 = (0.3)(55) + (0.7)(50) = 16.5 + 35.0 = 51.5$$

#### Step 4 — Apply to Month 4

$$F_4 = \alpha A_3 + (1 - \alpha) F_3$$

$$F_4 = (0.3)(60) + (0.7)(51.5) = 18.0 + 36.05 = 54.05$$

#### Step 5 — Apply to Month 5

$$F_5 = \alpha A_4 + (1 - \alpha) F_4$$

$$F_5 = (0.3)(58) + (0.7)(54.05) = 17.4 + 37.835 = 55.235$$

#### Step 6 — Apply to Month 6 (Future Forecast)

$$F_6 = \alpha A_5 + (1 - \alpha) F_5$$

$$F_6 = (0.3)(65) + (0.7)(55.235) = 19.5 + 38.6645 = 58.1645$$

#### Step 7 — Compute the Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD)

The absolute error for each period is $e_t = \vert A_t - F_t \vert$. Compute it in the table below.

| Month $t$ | $A_t$ | $F_t$ | $\vert e_t \vert = \vert A_t - F_t \vert$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 50 | 50 | $\vert 50 - 50 \vert = 0$ |
| 2 | 55 | 50 | $\vert 55 - 50 \vert = 5$ |
| 3 | 60 | 51.5 | $\vert 60 - 51.5 \vert = 8.5$ |
| 4 | 58 | 54.05 | $\vert 58 - 54.05 \vert = 3.95$ |
| 5 | 65 | 55.235 | $\vert 65 - 55.235 \vert = 9.765$ |

Summing the absolute errors:

$$\sum_{t=1}^{5} \vert e_t \vert = 0 + 5 + 8.5 + 3.95 + 9.765 = 27.215$$

The MAD is then:

$$\text{MAD} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{t=1}^{n} \vert e_t \vert = \frac{27.215}{5} = 5.443$$

**Interpretation:** On average, the forecast deviates from the actual by ~5.44 customers per month. The model is **under-forecasting** systematically (since adoption is accelerating), suggesting the firm should consider **Holt's linear method** (which adds a trend term) or switch to a higher $\alpha$ to track the upward movement.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — Bass Diffusion Model Parameter Estimation

**Problem:** A new EV model sold 1,000 units in Year 1 ($N_1 = 1000$) and the potential market is $m = 100{,}000$ units. Given coefficients $p = 0.02$ and $q = 0.38$, estimate the cumulative adopters at the end of Year 2 and the peak adoption year.

#### Step 1 — Identify the Discrete Bass Model

The continuous Bass model:

$$\frac{dN(t)}{dt} = \left[ p + q \frac{N(t)}{m} \right] \bigl[ m - N(t) \bigr]$$

has a closed-form solution (Bass 1969):

$$N(t) = m \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-(p+q)t}}{1 + \frac{q}{p} e^{-(p+q)t}}$$

#### Step 2 — Compute the Bass Coefficient Sum

$$p + q = 0.02 + 0.38 = 0.40$$

#### Step 3 — Compute the Ratio $q/p$

$$\frac{q}{p} = \frac{0.38}{0.02} = 19$$

#### Step 4 — Evaluate $N(1)$ to Verify the Model

$$N(1) = 100{,}000 \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-0.4}}{1 + 19 \cdot e^{-0.4}}$$

Compute the exponential term:

$$e^{-0.4} \approx 0.6703$$

Substituting:

$$N(1) = 100{,}000 \cdot \frac{1 - 0.6703}{1 + 19 \cdot 0.6703} = 100{,}000 \cdot \frac{0.3297}{1 + 12.7357}$$

$$N(1) = 100{,}000 \cdot \frac{0.3297}{13.7357} \approx 100{,}000 \cdot 0.02401 \approx 2401$$

This is higher than the observed 1,000 — the firm is **underperforming its adoption potential**.

#### Step 5 — Estimate $N(2)$

$$N(2) = 100{,}000 \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-0.8}}{1 + 19 \cdot e^{-0.8}}$$

$$e^{-0.8} \approx 0.4493$$

$$N(2) = 100{,}000 \cdot \frac{1 - 0.4493}{1 + 19 \cdot 0.4493} = 100{,}000 \cdot \frac{0.5507}{1 + 8.5367}$$

$$N(2) = 100{,}000 \cdot \frac{0.5507}{9.5367} \approx 100{,}000 \cdot 0.05775 \approx 5775$$

#### Step 6 — Find the Peak Adoption Year

The peak of the diffusion curve (the **maximum of $dN/dt$**) occurs at the inflection point of the cumulative curve, given by:

$$t^* = \frac{1}{p+q} \ln\left(\frac{q}{p}\right) = \frac{1}{0.40} \cdot \ln(19)$$

$$\ln(19) \approx 2.944$$

$$t^* = \frac{2.944}{0.40} \approx 7.36 \text{ years}$$

**Interpretation:** The EV will reach peak annual sales around **Year 7–8**, at which point cumulative adopters will be approximately $m/2 \approx 50{,}000$ units (the inflection point of the S-curve is at $N = m/2$).

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **KTU-style 14-mark application problem**. The full solution requires (a) the formula statement, (b) parameter computation, (c) two-period extrapolation, (d) peak-time calculation, and (e) managerial interpretation.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Organizing Technology Models

| Evaluation Criterion | Centralized | Decentralized | Matrix | Networked / Open Innovation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Speed of Decision** | Slow | Fast | Medium | Fast (external) |
| **Cost Efficiency** | High (shared) | Low (duplication) | Medium | Variable |
| **Innovation Type Suited** | Radical / Long-horizon | Incremental / BU-specific | Balanced portfolio | Disruptive / Platform |
| **IP & Knowledge Control** | Strong | Weak | Medium | Weakest (shared) |
| **Employee Motivation** | Specialized careers | Entrepreneurial | Dual loyalty stress | High autonomy |
| **Bureaucracy** | High | Low | High | Very Low |
| **Best Suited To** | Pharma, Aerospace | FMCG, Retail | Conglomerates, Tech giants | Software, Biotech startups |
| **Failure Mode** | Ivory Tower | Fragmentation | Power conflict | Loss of control |

---

### 3.4 Innovation Chain — Comparison of Two Models

| Stage | Linear Model (1945, Bush) | Chain-Linked Model (1986, Kline-Rosenberg) |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | One-way: Science → Technology → Market | Multi-directional with 5 feedback loops |
| Role of Market | End-point only | Active driver via feedback to R&D |
| Role of Science | Origin only | Origin + continuous input via $f_5$ |
| Source of Innovation | R&D lab | Multiple nodes: customers, production, science, marketing |
| Realism | Low (rarely observed) | High (matches firm behavior) |
| Implication for Manager | Fund R&D, sit back | Integrate cross-functional teams, listen to customers |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Chain-Linked Innovation Model (Kline-Rosenberg)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sci["Basic Science"]
    br["Basic Research"]
    ar["Applied Research"]
    dv["Development"]

    dv --> en
    en --> mn
    mn --> mk
    mn -. F1 .-> br
    mn -. F2 .-> ar
    mn -. F3 .-> dv
    mk -. F4 .-> br
    ks -. F5 .-> br
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define technology forecasting. List any two forecasting techniques used by technology-driven firms.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Technology forecasting is the systematic, science-based, and probabilistic estimation of future technological capabilities, performance, and adoption rates, used to inform R&D and strategic decisions. It is decision-support, not deterministic prediction.
- **Two Techniques (1 mark each):**
  1. **Delphi Method** — anonymous iterative expert elicitation across multiple rounds to converge on a consensus forecast.
  2. **Bass Diffusion Model** — quantitative model that separates innovators ($p$) from imitators ($q$) to forecast adoption of new products over time.
- *(Alternative valid: Trend Extrapolation, Morphological Analysis, Scenario Planning, S-Curve Analysis, Relevance Tree.)*

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is an innovation chain? Name the six stages of the Kline-Rosenberg chain-linked model.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** An innovation chain is the sequenced set of activities transforming scientific knowledge into a marketable product, with feedback loops making the process non-linear and iterative.
- **Six Stages (2 marks, ⅓ mark each or ½ + ½ for grouping):**
  1. Basic Research (BR)
  2. Applied Research (AR)
  3. Development (D)
  4. Engineering / Manufacturing Engineering (E)
  5. Production / Manufacturing (M)
  6. Marketing & Commercialization (S)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast the **centralized**, **decentralized**, and **matrix** models of organizing technology at the enterprise level. Use at least **five evaluation parameters**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Kline-Rosenberg Chain-Linked Model** of innovation. With the help of a diagram, describe its **six forward stages** and **five feedback loops**. Discuss why it is considered superior to the **linear model** of innovation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer for Question A(a) — 7 Marks**

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Allocation:** Definition (1) + Table with 5 parameters × 1 mark = (5) + Verdict (1).

| Parameter | Centralized | Decentralized | Matrix |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Decision Speed** | Slow (multi-tier approval) | Fast (BU-level autonomy) | Medium (dual approval) |
| **Cost Efficiency** | High — shared labs, no duplication | Low — duplicated infra across BUs | Medium — shared but with overhead |
| **Innovation Type** | Radical / long-horizon (e.g., 5G R&D) | Incremental / market-specific (e.g., FMCG variants) | Balanced portfolio |
| **IP Control** | Strong, centralized patent office | Fragmented, BU-specific | Shared, dual-ownership rules needed |
| **Bureaucracy** | High | Low | High (matrix dual reporting) |

**Verdict (1 mark):** The **matrix model** is the most widely adopted by large technology firms (e.g., Samsung, GE, Philips) because it balances the depth of centralization with the speed of decentralization, but it requires mature HR systems to manage dual-reporting conflict.

---

#### **Model Answer for Question A(b) — 7 Marks**

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Allocation:** Definition (1) + 6 stages (1.5) + 5 feedback loops (1.5) + Critique of linear model (2) + Diagram (1).

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**
The Kline-Rosenberg Chain-Linked Model (1986) is a non-linear representation of the innovation process in which multiple feedback loops connect research, engineering, manufacturing, and marketing, making innovation a coupled, multi-directional process.

**Step 2 — Six Forward Stages (1.5 marks):**
1. **Basic Research (BR)** — fundamental science
2. **Applied Research (AR)** — problem-directed research
3. **Development (D)** — prototype creation
4. **Engineering (E)** — scale-up design
5. **Manufacturing (M)** — production
6. **Sales & Marketing (S)** — commercialization

**Step 3 — Five Feedback Loops (1.5 marks):**
- $f_1$: M → BR (manufacturing problems trigger fundamental research)
- $f_2$: M → AR (production bottlenecks redirect applied research)
- $f_3$: M → D (design revisions for manufacturability)
- $f_4$: S → BR (market failures push back to basic science)
- $f_5$: External Knowledge → BR (scientific advances re-enter the chain)

**Step 4 — Diagram (1 mark):** *(Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes — the chain-linked model mermaid diagram.)*

**Step 5 — Critique of the Linear Model (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Linear Model (Bush, 1945) | Chain-Linked Model (Kline-Rosenberg, 1986) |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction of Flow** | One-way: Science → Tech → Market | Multi-directional with 5 loops |
| **Role of Market** | End-point only | Active driver via $f_4$ |
| **Innovation Source** | R&D lab alone | Customers, production, science, marketing |
| **Empirical Fit** | Poor — most innovations involve market-pull | High — matches observed firm behavior |
| **Managerial Implication** | Fund R&D, await output | Integrate cross-functional teams, listen to customers |

**Conclusion:** The linear model is *historically important* (it justified post-WWII R&D policy) but *operationally obsolete*. The chain-linked model reflects the reality of modern innovation, where customers and production staff often initiate breakthroughs that re-energize the research agenda.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Define **technology forecasting**. Describe the **Delphi method** and **morphological analysis** as two normative/expert-based forecasting techniques. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A firm's actual sales of a new technology product over 4 months are: **100, 120, 135, 150**. Apply **simple exponential smoothing** with $\alpha = 0.4$ and initial forecast $F_1 = 110$. Compute the forecasts for months 2, 3, 4, 5 and the **Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD)**. Comment on whether the model is tracking well. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer for Question B(a) — 7 Marks**

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Allocation:** Definition (1) + Delphi (3) + Morphological (3).

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**
Technology forecasting is the systematic, science-based, probabilistic estimation of future technological parameters (performance, cost, adoption) using historical data, expert judgment, and mathematical models, to support R&D and strategic decisions under uncertainty.

**Step 2 — Delphi Method (3 marks):**
- **Concept (1):** A structured, iterative, anonymous expert elicitation technique developed by RAND Corporation (Dalkey & Helmer, 1963).
- **Process (1):** Round 1 — open-ended questionnaire; Round 2 — statistical feedback (median, quartiles) to experts; Round 3+ — experts revise estimates, with convergence being the success criterion.
- **Advantages (1):** Avoids dominance by senior personalities; leverages geographically dispersed experts; produces quantified consensus with confidence intervals.

**Step 3 — Morphological Analysis (3 marks):**
- **Concept (1):** A normative forecasting technique (developed by Fritz Zwicky, 1969) that systematically explores *all possible combinations* of parameters that constitute a technology.
- **Process (1):** Identify key dimensions of the technology; for each dimension, list all feasible variants; construct a **morphological box** (matrix of dimensions × variants); filter for internally consistent combinations.
- **Example (1):** Forecasting future EV design: dimensions = {power source, body type, autonomy}; variants = {ICE, BEV, FCEV} × {sedan, SUV, hatchback} × {200 km, 400 km, 600 km}. 3 × 3 × 3 = 27 combinations, of which 3–5 are physically and economically viable.

---

#### **Model Answer for Question B(b) — 7 Marks**

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Allocation:** Formula (1) + Step-by-step forecast (3) + MAD table (2) + Interpretation (1).

**Step 1 — Recurrence Relation (1 mark):**

$$F_{t+1} = \alpha A_t + (1 - \alpha) F_t \quad \text{with} \quad \alpha = 0.4, \quad F_1 = 110$$

**Step 2 — Step-by-Step Forecast (3 marks):**

**Month 2:**

$$F_2 = 0.4 \cdot A_1 + 0.6 \cdot F_1 = 0.4(100) + 0.6(110) = 40 + 66 = 106$$

**Month 3:**

$$F_3 = 0.4 \cdot A_2 + 0.6 \cdot F_2 = 0.4(120) + 0.6(106) = 48 + 63.6 = 111.6$$

**Month 4:**

$$F_4 = 0.4 \cdot A_3 + 0.6 \cdot F_3 = 0.4(135) + 0.6(111.6) = 54 + 66.96 = 120.96$$

**Month 5 (future):**

$$F_5 = 0.4 \cdot A_4 + 0.6 \cdot F_4 = 0.4(150) + 0.6(120.96) = 60 + 72.576 = 132.576$$

**Step 3 — Mean Absolute Deviation (2 marks):**

| Month $t$ | $A_t$ | $F_t$ | $\vert e_t \vert = \vert A_t - F_t \vert$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100 | 110 | $\vert 100 - 110 \vert = 10$ |
| 2 | 120 | 106 | $\vert 120 - 106 \vert = 14$ |
| 3 | 135 | 111.6 | $\vert 135 - 111.6 \vert = 23.4$ |
| 4 | 150 | 120.96 | $\vert 150 - 120.96 \vert = 29.04$ |

$$\sum \vert e_t \vert = 10 + 14 + 23.4 + 29.04 = 76.44$$

$$\text{MAD} = \frac{76.44}{4} = 19.11 \text{ units}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (1 mark):**
The MAD of **19.11 units** is high relative to actual sales (~100–150 units), and the errors are *systematically positive* (forecast < actual). The model is **lagging a clear upward trend**. Recommendation: switch to **Holt's linear trend method** (add a trend smoothing parameter $\beta$), or increase $\alpha$ to ~0.6 to make the model more responsive to recent gains.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on this Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing the linear model with the chain-linked model.** Students often *list* the six stages but *omit the five feedback loops* — this caps the answer at 50%. Always draw $f_1$ through $f_5$ explicitly.
> 2. **Mis-defining the matrix model.** A common error is describing it as "having two managers" — the *correct* answer is "dual reporting to a functional and a project manager, with shared authority over the engineer's priorities."
> 3. **Forecasting arithmetic errors.** In exponential smoothing, students frequently forget the *initial* forecast $F_1$ and start at $F_2 = \alpha A_1$, which is wrong unless specified. Always state the recurrence *and* the initial condition.
> 4. **Missing units in MAD.** A MAD without units is considered incomplete — write "MAD = 19.11 customers/month" or "units".
> 5. **Bass Diffusion confusion.** Students mix up the **innovation coefficient $p$** (external influence — ads, media) and the **imitation coefficient $q$** (internal — word of mouth). A common exam trap.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4:**

- [ ] **Organizing Technology** has **3 structural models** — Centralized, Decentralized, Matrix. Matrix is the most exam-relevant.
- [ ] **Auxiliary mechanisms** — Technology Council, Stage-Gate, Technology Audit, Open Innovation.
- [ ] **Technology Forecasting** is *probabilistic*, not predictive. 4 families: Normative, Exploratory, Expert-Based, Quantitative.
- [ ] **Delphi Method** = anonymous, iterative, expert-based; converges on a median.
- [ ] **Morphological Analysis** = Zwicky's matrix of all parameter combinations.
- [ ] **Bass Diffusion Model** = $p$ (innovators) + $q$ (imitators) + $m$ (market potential).
- [ ] **S-Curve** — every technology has Emergence → Growth → Maturity → Decline phases.
- [ ] **Innovation Chain** = 6 stages: **B**asic R, **A**pplied R, **D**evelopment, **E**ngineering, **M**anufacturing, **S**ales.
- [ ] **Chain-Linked Model** = linear + **5 feedback loops** ($f_1$ to $f_5$); superior to the **linear model** (Bush, 1945).
- [ ] **Exponential Smoothing** = $F_{t+1} = \alpha A_t + (1-\alpha) F_t$; MAD = mean of $\vert A_t - F_t \vert$.
- [ ] **CAGR** = $\left( V_f / V_i \right)^{1/n} - 1$ — used for market sizing.
- [ ] **R&D Intensity** = R&D Spend / Revenue — benchmarking metric.
- [ ] **Key Comparisons to Memorize:** Linear vs Chain-Linked, Centralized vs Matrix, $p$ vs $q$ in Bass, Normative vs Quantitative forecasting.
- [ ] **Mnemonic for Innovation Chain:** **"BAD-EMS"** → **B**asic R, **A**pplied R, **D**evelopment, **E**ngineering, **M**anufacturing, **S**ales.
- [ ] **Mnemonic for 5 Feedback Loops:** **"MMM-MK-KB"** → M→BR, M→AR, M→D, Market→BR, Knowledge→BR.
- [ ] **Stage-Gate Process** = 5 stages, 5 gates, with **kill/recycle** loops at each gate — invented by Robert Cooper (1986).

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 4 Notes — PEMET417 Technology Management**
> All KTU 2024 Scheme ESE question patterns, valuation tips, and high-yield formulas are aligned with the syllabus outcomes CO4 and CO5 (Strategic Technology Management).
